Alongside potential pitfalls and a future vision, these guidelines equip biopsychological researchers with actionable support for integrating AI-based methods into their work in line with open science principles.

articles.title: Autistic imagination sub-traits are unrelated to visual temporal integration or inference
articles.authors: Yurika Tsuji; Yuki Nishiguchi; Akari Noda; Shu Imaizumi
articles.abstract: Autistic individuals experience atypical temporal integration, and previous studies have suggested that this atypicality is related to difficulties in imagination. Temporal integration in vision was assessed using slit-viewing tasks that require unifying successive partial views of a moving target into a coherent percept. This task also requires inference ability when the targets are complex, as in line drawings. We hypothesized that imagination difficulties would be more closely related to inference difficulties than to temporal integration difficulties. To test this, we conducted an experiment with 67 Japanese university students using slit-viewing tasks with line drawings, characters, and meaningless shapes, as well as a fill-in-the-blank task involving word inference. The results showed that neither imagination difficulties nor overall autistic traits were associated with performance in any of these tasks. These findings suggest that imagination difficulties and autistic traits may not be related to visual temporal integration or inference abilities.

articles.title: Motions Speak Louder Than Words: Designing Human-like AV Speed Profiles at Crosswalks with a Bayesian Optimisation–Driven Adaptive Experiment
articles.authors: Yuwei Wang; Gustav Markkula; Yee Mun Lee
articles.abstract: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) must communicate yielding intent to pedestrians in mixed traffic to ensure safe interaction, and vehicle decelerating motion is a promising implicit communication channel at crosswalks. Designing AV deceleration motion remains difficult because it is continuous with interdependent kinematics (e.g., braking onset, deceleration rate), making fixed‑design experiments inefficient. Therefore, we parameterised deceleration behaviour using a minimum‑jerk model (MJM), which can generate smooth speed profiles from tuneable parameters (stopping distance and duration). We will then formulate profile design as an optimisation problem that maximises pedestrian‑rated safety and efficiency. We will conduct an adaptive experiment in an immersive pedestrian simulator in which Bayesian optimisation sequentially proposes profiles that are most likely to improve safety and efficiency ratings based on pedestrians’ evaluations of previous profiles. Preliminary results from fitting MJM to 62 naturalistic human profiles indicate that the MJM reproduces the overall human profile shape (median RMSE = 0.88 m/s) with only 2 parameters (i.e., stopping distance, duration), supporting its use as a profile generator for the experiment. This work can contribute a human-in-the-loop framework for designing AV motion as an implicit interface.

articles.title: Precision-weighting across domains: A predictive coding framework for language-music integration under uncertainty
articles.authors: Aris Kargakis; Eleanor E. Harding; Matt Coler; Wander Lowie
articles.abstract: Predictive Coding Theory (PCT) proposes that perception minimizes mismatches between anticipated and actual input. Although PCT has received empirical support in language comprehension, an open question concerns whether predictive mechanisms are domain-general or language-specific. Building on extra-linguistic evidence, this position paper proposes a cross-domain conceptual framework to explore how PCT functions during real-time processing, particularly in the auditory domains of language and music. We propose that precision-weighting–a core mechanism of PCT–operates across domains, weighting cues from language and music according to their learned reliability. This process may selectively enable cross-domain integration as a function of input uncertainty. Given accounts treating language and music as either shared or separate systems, this framework moves beyond binary approaches by specifying how and when cues from one domain may reduce uncertainty in another to optimize conceptualization. This perspective may contribute to broader debates on modularity in cognition, offering a complex dynamic systems interaction perspective.

articles.title: People have an accurate folk theory of conspiracy believers
articles.authors: Marius Mercier; Noëmon Baudouin; Hugo Mercier
articles.abstract: Seeing someone endorse a conspiracy theory could be informative of which other conspiracy theories they might endorse and reveal broader personality traits. Across three pre-registered experiments (N = 775 US participants), we show that these inferences are supported by the nested structure of conspiracy beliefs: individuals who endorse less popular theories tend to endorse more popular ones, but not vice versa. This pattern holds for real-world conspiracy theories (false and true) as well as for invented ones. When presented with the information that someone endorses or rejects a conspiracy theory, participants could predict which other theories that individual would endorse or reject. Moreover, participants could infer the personality traits of conspiracy believers (e.g., narcissism, manichaeism) by appropriately weighing the rarity of the endorsed conspiracy theory. These findings suggest that people use a folk theory of latent conspiracism to draw broadly accurate inferences about conspiracy believers (or non-believers) from limited information.

articles.title: Prediction Under Pressure: Predictive Sentence Processing in Dyslexia and Second-Language Reading
articles.authors: Sam Boeve; Ella Salgado Salgado Figueroa; Louisa Bogaerts
articles.abstract: Predictive processing plays a central role in fluent reading, yet it remains unclear how it is modulated by reading difficulties and language context (first vs. second language). The present study investigated predictive sentence processing in typical readers and in readers with dyslexia reading a novel in their first language (Dutch) and second language (English) (GECO; Cop et al., 2017 & DECO; Boeve et al., 2026). Using eye-tracking measures and computational estimates of word surprisal and entropy, we examined the influence of word predictability on reading behaviour. Results revealed systematic differences across groups and languages. Early reading measures (first fixation duration) showed a surprisal-entropy interaction consistent with prediction error effects, that were stronger in dyslexia and in second language reading. Later measures (gaze duration and total reading time) showed prediction error effects only in typical readers, while readers with dyslexia showed facilitation from predictable words without modulation by entropy. Skipping behaviour also differed between groups. Typical readers skipped more words overall than readers with dyslexia. Higher entropy and surprisal generally reduced skipping, yet readers with dyslexia showed a stronger reduction of skipping in high-uncertainty contexts. Together, the results suggest that predictive processing adapts to processing demands and reader ability. Readers with dyslexia appear to rely primarily on early predictions about word-form information, possibly as a compensatory strategy for difficulties in word identification, whereas typical readers seem to engage in both form-based and semantic prediction during reading.

articles.title: Client AI-First Disclosure in Social Work: Readiness Gaps, Ethics, and Organizational Governance
articles.authors: Ho Nam Cheung; Hui Yun LI; Huinan Hu; Vivian W.Q. Lou
articles.abstract: Purpose: Clients may now disclose sensitive concerns to artificial intelligence (AI) tools before contacting a social worker, a practice we define as client AI-first disclosure. This exploratory study estimates its prevalence and examines whether social workers’ confidence in responding is associated with technical familiarity, ethical competence, or organizational guidance. Method: A cross-sectional survey of 401 registered social workers in Hong Kong assessed AI competence, AI use, wariness, training, organizational boundaries, exposure to AI-first disclosure, and response confidence. Analyses included paired-samples t tests, ordinal logistic regression, diagnostics, and sensitivity checks. Results: Among respondents who answered the item, 56.6% had encountered AI-first disclosure; 35.2% felt confident responding, and 36.4% reported clear organizational guidance. Confidence was associated with organizational boundaries and ethical competence, whereas technical proficiency, use, training, and exposure were not. Discussion: Within the limits of a cross-sectional design and single-item disclosure measures, the findings suggest a readiness gap that is linked more clearly to organizational guidance and ethical capacity than to technical familiarity alone. It carries practice and policy implications of renovating the existing training framework to address the gap.

articles.title: Family Genetic Risk Scores for Major Psychiatric and Neurodevelopmental Diagnoses in the Norwegian Population
articles.authors: Ludvig Daae Bjørndal; Mathias Valstad; Ziada Ayorech; Espen Moen Eilertsen; Oda van Jole; Hans Fredrik Sunde; Toralf James MacCulloch Grande; Fartein Ask Torvik; Kenneth S. Kendler; Eivind Ystrom
articles.abstract:
Importance: Family history is a prominent indicator of psychiatric disorder risk and used in clinical assessment. Pearson-Aitken Family-Genetic Risk Scores (PA-FGRSs) aim to quantify genetic risk based on diagnostic history of family members and genetic relatedness. However, there is limited knowledge about how PA-FGRSs characterize psychiatric and neurodevelopmental diagnoses, clinical indicators (age of onset, recurrence), and their assumptions. 

Objective: Provide an overview of how PA-FGRS characterize psychiatric and neurodevelopmental diagnoses, relationships with clinical indices, partner similarity, and use a within-family design to examine assumptions of the PA-FGRS. 

Design, Setting, and Participants: This cohort study included 6 242 506 Norwegian residents born between 1934 and 2023. We observed participants from January 1st 2006 through December 31st 2023 using Norwegian primary and specialist healthcare registry data (including inpatient and outpatient clinics). We also incorporated a genotyped parent-offspring subsample from the Norwegian Mother, Father and Child Cohort Study.

Exposures: PA-FGRSs for 10 major psychiatric (alcohol use, drug use, schizophrenia, bipolar, depression, anxiety and stress-related, obsessive-compulsive, and eating disorders) and neurodevelopmental diagnoses (ADHD and autism). We incorporated polygenic indices (PGIs) for drug use, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, depression, autism, and ADHD.
Main Outcomes and Measures
We calculated individual-level PA-FGRSs for diagnoses, estimated associations with clinical indicators, and examined partner similarity. We compared empirical and theoretically expected PA-FGRS and PGI correlations to evaluate the PA-FGRS as a between-family index of risk. 

Results: Diagnosis-specific PA-FGRSs were most strongly elevated when comparing affected and unaffected individuals for most diagnoses, while PA-FGRSs for depressive and anxiety disorders were elevated across all. Higher PA-FGRS was associated with earlier age of onset and higher recurrence and partners were correlated for PA-FGRSs. The within-family design generally supported assumptions of the PA-FGRS but also suggested it does not solely capture direct genetic effects for alcohol use disorder.

Conclusions and relevance: PA-FGRSs exhibit varying patterns across psychiatric and neurodevelopmental diagnoses and are associated with recurrence and age of onset. While we found support for key assumptions of PA-FGRS, potential impact of assortative mating should be explored further. The PA-FGRS offers a scalable approach to identify familial liability in population registry data and its application can provide insights into the etiology of psychiatric and neurodevelopmental conditions.

articles.title: Olfactory facilitation: A participatory framework for scent as a designed sensory medium
articles.authors: Anastasiia A Stepanchuk
articles.abstract: Scent is increasingly used across design, education, wellness, dementia care, the arts, and events, yet applied scent practice remains fragmented across perfumery, scent marketing, aromatherapy, reminiscence, and smellscape design, without a shared vocabulary, agreed methods, or explicit ethical structure for facilitation. This paper proposes olfactory facilitation as a structured, ethical, and participatory approach to using scent as a medium for perception, memory, identity, atmosphere, and connection in non-clinical settings. Drawing on olfactory cognition, sensory and atmospheric design, participatory and community-based research, and disability-led design, the framework distinguishes facilitation from treatment and organises scent encounters across five interacting dimensions. It translates these dimensions into six practice principles and situates them within an ethical structure addressing sensitivities, consent, cultural specificity, and the limits of the facilitator’s role. The paper is explicitly non-clinical: it does not claim therapeutic or cognitive outcomes, which would require measurement under ethics. Its contribution is conceptual and practical, naming and structuring a practice that is already widespread so that it can be taught, evaluated, adapted, and held to account. Limitations and directions for empirical testing and co-design are discussed.

articles.title: Social disconnection, depression, and anxiety during maternity leave among Slovak women
articles.authors: Hana Celusakova; Petra Brandoburová; Simona Krakovska; Michal Hajdúk; Jakub Januška
articles.abstract:
Objectives
The study aimed to estimate the prevalence of social disconnection, depression, and anxiety among Slovak mothers on parental leave. The secondary objective was to assess the contribution of perceived loneliness and social isolation to depressive symptoms among mothers.
Methods
The final sample consisted of 410 mothers with a mean age of M = 33.4 and SD = 4.61. Data were collected online via battery of standardized self-report questionnaires, focused on depression, anxiety, perceived loneliness, and social isolation. 
Results
The prevalence rates of moderately severe to severe depression (PHQ-9 ≥ 10) and anxiety (GAD-7 ≥ 10) were 38.3% and 28.3%, respectively. The co-occurrence of both conditions was observed in 23.2% of mothers. Elevated levels of loneliness were reported by 78.3% of participants (UCLA-3 ≥ 6), while 26.8% were classified as socially isolated (LSNS-6 < 12), indicating a high prevalence of both loneliness and social isolation in this sample. Regression analysis showed that anxiety and loneliness were significant positive predictors of depressive symptoms, with the models explaining 61–62% of the variance.
Conclusion
The result of the study revealed elevated rates of depression and anxiety among mothers on parental leave. Psychological factors, particularly anxiety, loneliness, and social isolation, played a more prominent role in explaining depressive symptoms than demographic variables, including length of parental leave. The findings underscore the need for systematic screening and accessible perinatal mental health care among mothers on parental leave.

articles.title: Opening a File Drawer: Surprisingly Consistent Evidence for Semantic Interference from Distractor Pictures in Picture Naming
articles.authors: Andreas Mädebach; Stefan Wöhner; Jörg D. Jescheniak
articles.abstract: Semantic-categorically related distractor words slow down picture naming more than semantically unrelated distractor words do (e.g., the words “dog” vs. “car” for a picture of a horse). This semantic interference effect in the picture-word interference task has been replicated numerous times and is an empirical cornerstone of speech production research. However, studies using the picture-picture interference task, in which the distractor words are replaced with distractor pictures, have yielded highly inconsistent results, reporting semantic interference, semantic facilitation, and null effects. Here, we present a meta-analytic summary of the results of published picture-picture interference studies. We found the estimated semantic effect to be close to zero (2 ms, 95% CI [ 5, 9]). This contrasts with the results of 14 previously unpublished picture-picture interference experiments conducted in our laboratory and which we report here. Across these experiments, the estimated semantic interference effect was 15 ms (95% CI [9, 21]). When these data were included in the meta-analysis, the pooled estimate was 9 ms (95% CI [4, 14]). These results suggest that the magnitude of semantic interference from distractor pictures has been underestimated and that it is not as elusive as previously thought. We discuss the implications for modelling word production.

articles.title: Genetic and Environmental Contributions to Sensory Processing Sensitivity in Rural and Urban Areas
articles.authors: Dmitry V. Kuznetsov; Janina Neufeld; Simone Kühn
articles.abstract:
Sensory Processing Sensitivity (SPS) describes a personality trait or a style of processing both external and internal inputs, ranging from very deep and reflective to shallow and quick. Variance in the components of SPS has been recently demonstrated to be attributable to both genetic and unique environmental factors, confirming the presence of stable biologically-influenced individual differences in SPS. Meanwhile, the knowledge of whether these estimates are replicable across other populations and whether particular factors may modulate the genetic-environmental architecture of SPS is limited. 
Thus, in the sample of 3455 German twins from the TwinLife twin registry (Nmonozygotic twins=1641, Ndizygotic twins=1814; Mage=18.17(4.89) years), we explored the structure and the degree of genetic and environmental contribution to SPS using a shorter version of the Hypersensitive Person Scale (HSP). We considered three subscales (EOE, Ease of excitation; LST, Lower sensitivity threshold; and AES, Aesthetic Sensitivity), and, further, examined whether the proportion of genetic (A) and environmental (E) variances varied between individuals in urban or rural environments.
Approximately one third of the differences in the latent trait SPS (A:37%[30;43], E:(63%[57;70]) and its components were attributed to additive genetic factors, and the two thirds to unique environmental factors. Genetic and environmental factors tent to explain the shared variances within three subscales. For EOE, in contrast to the other subscales, genetic differences were slightly larger in urban areas (34%[28;40]) than in rural areas (30%[23;38]). The nature and development of SPS, the potential influence of living environment and the usefulness of the SPS for environmental neuroscience are, further, discussed.

articles.title: Social Psychology of Generative AI: An Algorithmic Sociocognitive Model and the Legitimation of a Disciplinary Framework
articles.authors: Antonio Aguilar
articles.abstract:
This article proposes a theoretical-documentary agenda for a Social Psychology of Generative AI
based on the Algorithmic Sociocognitive Model. From this perspective, generative AI is understood as
a form of algorithmic sociocognitive mediation that intervenes in processes of trust, agency, attention,
cognitive delegation, influence, knowledge legitimacy, social representation, and the shared
construction of reality.
The study derives a matrix of nine lines of research and applies it to a corpus of 96 projects funded by
the Spanish State Research Agency between 2023 and 2025. The results show an uneven presence of
the proposed dimensions. Algorithmic persuasion and social influence account for the largest number
of projects, particularly around disinformation, polarization, political communication, hate speech, and
the digital public sphere. Interdisciplinary scientific innovation also stands out, linked to linguistic,
educational, media, health, and scientific applications of AI.
By contrast, methodological innovation in Social Psychology, psychosocial public health, structural
critique, the ecological dimension, and the conceptual revision of the discipline appear in a more
limited or fragmentary way. The discussion also shows the relevance of cross-cutting problems such as
epistemic authority, the algorithmic production of representations, trust, agency, cognitive delegation,
dependence, and inequality. The analysis reveals a paradoxical position of Social Psychology: although
the Psychology area has less quantitative weight in the corpus, many of the problems identified refer to
psychosocial processes distributed across different disciplinary areas.
The article concludes that Social Psychology offers a necessary framework for understanding
generative AI as a sociocognitive, relational, institutional, and structural phenomenon. Its contribution
does not lie only in studying attitudes toward, or individual uses of, technology, but in articulating the
psychosocial processes that cut across contemporary interaction among subjects, institutions, and
algorithmic systems.

articles.title: The Neuroscience of Metaphor as Abstraction
articles.authors: Bálint Forgács
articles.abstract: Metaphor has been suggested to be one of the pinnacles of human cognition, yet the neural underpinnings of figurative language is hotly debated and findings are often controversial. The goal of the present paper is to demonstrate that the available neuroscientific evidence on metaphor opens a window on an essential core of human language. Contrary to the widely shared assumptions of embodied cognition, metaphor reveals the fundamentally abstract functioning of the language system in its pristine form. Following an overview of the diverse neuroscientific (fMRI, EEG, etc.) and behavioral results, and a critical evaluation of the available theories, the paper proposes a novel model for metaphor. Recent experimental data shows that metaphor is not processed serially (cf. the structuralist linguistic and the Gricean pragmatic traditions) but as any other form of (literal) language, and its contents are not provided by sensorimotor systems (cf. embodiment) but recovered through an “abstractness effect”. Since current theories are not able to account for these data or are hampered by structural controversies, a novel model, Abstract Conceptual Substitution, is proposed: metaphor involves a particular polysemy-like meaning disambiguation, where concrete semantic features are suppressed, and abstract properties are enhanced. Figurative meaning is a vivid byproduct of a fundamental feature of the language system that allows the abstract interpretation of concrete, perceptual, physical words, demonstrating that the language processor operates based on symbolic, rather than sensorimotor representations. “Metaphorization” plays an important role in everyday social negotiations by creating parallel layers of meaning that allow for strategic ambiguity.

articles.title: Learned Value and Context-Dependent Probability Independently Bias the Priority of  Competing Goals in Visual Search
articles.authors: Chris Robert Harrison Brown
articles.abstract:
In life we often have multiple concurrent goals which compete to guide attention. It, 
however, remains unclear how we select which goal to prioritise, and whether simple 
associative pairings can underpin this process. The current study, therefore, explored how 
participants utilised associative value and context-dependent probability learning to guide 
visual search. Participants completed a visual search task with letter targets appearing in 
one of two colours, one associated with a high-value outcome, the other a low-value 
outcome (Experiment 1a: symbolic points; Experiments 1b - 3: financial reward). Contextual 
probability was manipulated by presenting a context scene (indoor/ outdoor) prior to visual 
array onset, each target colour was presented more frequently (80% probability) after one 
scene versus the other (20%). In an extinction phase, reward feedback was removed 
(Experiments 1a, 1b; N = 26, 40) or explicit devaluation feedback was presented 
(Experiment 2; N = 40), and both target colours were made equally probable across 
contexts. In Experiment 3 (N = 40), to test goal-independent effects, previously value
associated colours were presented as task-irrelevant distractors preceded by previously 
associated context scenes. Across experiments, value and contextual probability 
independently predicted prioritisation of target features, and this persisted into extinction. 
When stimuli were presented as distractors there was only weak evidence that they captured 
attention, at odds with a purely goal-independent mechanism driving the substantial 
prioritisation effects observed when stimuli were task-relevant. These findings are 
compatible with models which posit that simple associative mechanisms can underpin the 
flexible configuration of top-down attentional control settings.

articles.title: Unraveling the interplay between sensory information and semantic architecture in brain concept representation: the role of abstraction
articles.authors: Chiara Battaglini; Davide Bottari; Giacomo Handjaras; Martina Berto; Ella Striem-Amit; Pietro Pietrini; Alessandro Lenci; Giovanna Marotta; Emiliano Ricciardi
articles.abstract:
INTRODUCTION: Understanding how abstract and concrete words are represented in the brain requires disentangling the contribution of sensorimotor information from the organization of semantic knowledge. These dimensions, here referred to as abstractness and abstraction, respectively, are typically confounded, limiting our ability to identify their distinct neural correlates.
OBJECTIVES: The present study aimed to dissociate the roles of abstractness and abstraction in word processing by introducing superordinate words as a critical comparison category.
METHODS: Behavioral norming and feature production tasks were used to quantify sensory-related properties and semantic architecture, operationalized in terms of generalizability, feature relevance, and representational flexibility. These measures informed computational models capturing abstractness, abstraction, sensorimotor content, and distributional similarity. Neural data were acquired using electroencephalography and analyzed with representational similarity analysis to assess model–brain correspondence over time.
RESULTS: Behaviorally, superordinate words patterned with concrete words on sensory properties but with abstract words on measures of semantic architecture. In the electrophysiological data, concrete words elicited stronger negativities than both abstract and superordinate words in the N400 and N700 time windows, whereas abstract and superordinate words did not differ. Representational similarity analysis revealed that neural response patterns were best explained by the abstraction model, outperforming models based on sensorimotor or distributional information.
CONCLUSION: These results indicate that the neural dynamics of word processing are primarily shaped by semantic architecture rather than by sensorimotor grounding alone. Abstraction plays a central role in structuring conceptual representations, providing a unifying account that bridges embodied and formal theories of semantic representation.

articles.title: Beyond model-based and model-free learning: irrational successor feature learning in addictive behaviour
articles.authors: Zeming Fang; Yu-Yan Gao; Yu-Feng Xia; Zi-Jian Cheng; Lingxiao Yang; Wei Li; Tomoko Kishimoto; Lei Guo; Trevor William Robbins; RU-YUAN ZHANG
articles.abstract: Addiction has been widely viewed as an example of reinforcement learning gone awry, often attributed to impaired model-based (MB) control and a compensatory shift toward model-free (MF) strategies. However, such “MB-MF” dichotomy is largely grounded in single-task settings and may not capture how learning operates in environments that require generalization across multiple tasks. Here, we examine learning in individuals with substance use disorders (SUD) using a two-step, multi-task paradigm in which participants navigate a tree-structured environment to achieve goals with varying reward contingencies. Our results show that, across groups, behavior is not well explained by canonical MB or MF accounts. Instead, participants acquire successor features that serve as reusable components for generalization across tasks. Leveraging information theory, we develop a resource-rational model that balances expected reward and cognitive cost in multi-task learning. This model successfully reproduced several characteristic behaviors in both groups. Critically, individuals with SUD show increased sensitivity to cognitive cost and preferentially adopt simplified policies and reuse them across tasks, at the expense of reward maximization. Individual differences in cost sensitivity and inferred cognitive effort predict addiction severity. These findings challenge the dominant MB–MF dichotomy of addiction and instead point to a systematic shift in how cognitive resources are allocated during learning. More broadly, our findings highlight resource-rational generalization as a key principle for understanding atypical behavior in complex, multi-task environments.

articles.title: Artificial observers and real individual differences: A roadmap for a computational psychometrics of vision
articles.authors: David Henry Peterzell
articles.abstract: Deep neural networks are increasingly used as models of vision, yet they are evaluated against the average observer: average human performance, average neural response, average representational geometry. This discards one of the richest constraints on theory: stable individual differences. Fitting the average is necessary but not sufficient. I argue that future models should be judged not only by their fit to mean human performance, but by whether populations of artificial observers reproduce the covariance structure and latent factors found across human observers. Two mature but disconnected literatures motivate this. Decades of factor-analytic psychophysics and electrophysiology show that interindividual variation is signal, not noise: the pattern of correlations among observers reveals the number and tuning of underlying mechanisms, and fractionates vision into many narrow, domain-specific factors rather than one general ability. Meanwhile, neural networks differing only in random initialization develop measurably different internal representations, so any single trained network is one sample from a distribution of possible observers. Joining these yields a computational psychometrics of vision: construct a designed population of artificial observers (varying in architecture, initialization, training history, simulated optics, cone sampling, and developmental input), submit them to the same latent-variable analyses used on humans, and test whether the recovered factor structure matches. Recent work showing that seed-varied networks align with specific individuals is an existence proof for the behavioral core of this idea. This article generalizes it into a framework, a benchmark, and a roadmap for using artificial observer populations to test hypotheses about human visual variation.

articles.title: Autistic traits and temporal integration of auditory and visual stimuli: The role of imagination
articles.authors: Yurika Tsuji; Yuki Nishiguchi; Akari Noda; Shu Imaizumi
articles.abstract: Autistic individuals face temporal integration difficulties in some sensory modalities that may be related to imagination difficulties. In this study, we tested the hypotheses that among Japanese university students, (1) higher autistic traits and (2) greater imagination difficulties lead to correspondingly lower performance in tasks that require temporal integration. Two tasks were used to assess their temporal integration abilities: a speech-in-noise test using noise with temporal dips in the auditory modality and a slit-viewing task in the visual modality. The results showed that low performance in the speech-in-noise test was related to autistic traits and some aspects of imagination difficulties, whereas the slit-viewing task was related to neither autistic traits nor imagination difficulties. One must have the ability to temporally integrate fragments of auditory information to perceive speech in noise with temporal dips. The difficulties in perceiving sensory information as a single unified percept using priors may cause difficulties in temporally integrating auditory information and perceiving speech in noise. Furthermore, the structural equation modeling suggests that imagination difficulties are linked to difficulties in perceiving speech in noise with temporal dips, which in turn links to social impairments.

articles.title: The four conceptualizations of social connection
articles.authors: Elisa C Baek; Razieh Pourafshari; Joseph Bayer
articles.abstract: Research on social connection has proliferated across psychology over the past two decades, with rapid growth in the past 10–15 years. However, this broad interest has led to ‘social connection’ becoming a catch-all term, such that disparate strands of work are based on different approaches to conceptualization and measurement. In this Review, we synthesize prior research and highlight four distinct uses of the term ‘social connection’ — as subjective perceptions, social networks, interpersonal synchrony and interpersonal behaviours — and describe how research based in each conceptualization has contributed to understanding of the concept. We also review how constant connectivity via smartphones and social media has changed and challenged the landscape of social connection research. We conclude by highlighting the complex links between the different conceptualizations of social connection (and constant connectivity) and discuss how fragmentation poses challenges to scientific progress and public communication. By categorizing and clarifying subsets of the literature, we aim to facilitate communication across areas of psychological science and thereby guide research towards a more holistic understanding of social connection.

articles.title: On Great Ape Personality Structure: Problems in Minkov (2026)
articles.authors: Alexander Weiss
articles.abstract: A recent paper (Minkov, 2026) claimed that great ape personality is not well-described by the five personality dimensions found in humans, those being Neuroticism, Extraversion, Openness to Experience, Agreeableness, and Conscientiousness. To support this claim, the author analyzed personality factor structures obtained in studies of great apes in which personality was assessed using rating scales. The analyses tested for ‘configural invariance’ by computing the percentage of traits that loaded on the same factor as in humans in chimpanzees, orangutans, mountain gorillas, and bonobos, and the percentage of traits that loaded on the same factor across four studies of chimpanzees. The paper also reviewed the evidence for Conscientiousness in chimpanzees, including by citing the results of studies that used other ratings scales, and concluded that this factor’s evidentiary basis was weak. The paper concluded by proposing an alternative approach to studying personality in great apes. This commentary exposes fundamental problems with the article. First, I show that the paper’s author mischaracterized the research program of comparative psychologists who study personality. Second, I review the evidence the paper uses to support its claim that there is little evidence for configural invariance, highlighting problems and errors in the analyses and reporting of results. Third, I review the evidence presented against the presence of Conscientiousness in chimpanzees and find it problematic. Finally, I demonstrate that the literature review lacks due care. I conclude by dispelling a misunderstanding about comparative research on personality and reaffirm the importance and promise of pluralism in the study of the phenomenon.

articles.title: Translational Evidence for Habenula’s Role in Human Addiction: A Scoping Review
articles.authors: Charles Parisien; Norah Ho-El Abidi; Elina Khalaf; Jean-Simon Fortin; Narun Pat; Sébastien Hétu
articles.abstract: Addiction is a chronic, relapsing disorder comprising three stages: binge, withdrawal, and preoccupation. Preclinical evidence suggests that the habenula, a small epithalamic region, is involved across these stages and may contribute to long-term neuroadaptations in reward, stress, and control systems. Human research on the habenula in addiction is growing but remains fragmented. This scoping review synthesizes human evidence on the habenula in addiction and assesses convergence with preclinical models. Following PRISMA-ScR guidelines, databases and grey literature were searched in November 2025. Studies were included if they examined habenular measures in relation to addiction in humans, and findings were synthesized by measure category: volume, structural connectivity, functional connectivity, activity, and genetic measures. Within each category, findings were interpreted by addiction stage where applicable. Nineteen studies were included. Across categories, studies reported reduced fronto–habenula white-matter integrity, elevated habenula–striatal resting-state functional connectivity, and withdrawal-related habenula–frontal connectivity. By contrast, volumetric studies did not find consistent evidence linking habenula volume to addiction status. Activity findings included increased habenula blood flow during withdrawal, as well as task-based fMRI evidence of reduced differentiation between positive and negative feedback and heightened reward responses. Genetic findings most consistently implicated nicotine-related habenula signaling. Human findings converged most clearly with preclinical models for withdrawal and nicotine mechanisms, but no domain showed direct one-to-one human–preclinical alignment. Several domains were either one-sided, based on non-equivalent measures, or absent in both literatures. Future studies using comparable designs are needed to clarify how the habenula contributes to addiction.

articles.title: A Distinct Role for Perceived Realism in Juror Decision Making
articles.authors: Alexandria Breann Rogers; Seth Madlon-Kay; David E. Huber; R. McKell Carter; J. H. Pate Skene
articles.abstract: Prominent theories of juror decision making assume that jurors create a narrative to make sense of a case, with this narrative relying on retrieved information. The retrieved information might concern everyday knowledge (factual recall) and personal experience (autobiographical recall). Beyond this reliance on retrieved information, a coherent narrative can give rise to a sense of perceived realism, supporting stronger ratings of prosecutorial case strength. To assess how perceived realism and explicit recall interact to affect juror decisions, we asked jury-eligible subjects (N = 83) to read mock criminal cases, rating the strength of each case. Participants also reported their ability to recall a similar case (autobiographical recall), their knowledge of similar cases (factual recall), and the perceived realism of the case. Perceived realism was the most influential component of mock-juror decisions. Additional analyses identified two contributions of perceived realism: a common factor combining realism with memory recall and an independent factor, which may include additional effects of explicit memory, mental models, and schema not captured in our explicit memory measures. Variance partitioning and structural equation modeling indicated that the effects of explicit memory on case strength are mediated by perceived realism. The results support narrative and coherence-based reasoning models of complex decisions, where the evidence may be incomplete, ambiguous, or conflicting. Because perceived realism depends on individual knowledge, experiences, and beliefs, a substantial role for perceived realism in juror decisions represents a mechanism/path for the influence of individual biases, stereotypes, and cultural beliefs in jurors and other complex decisions.

articles.title: Emotion expression in song and speech: Determining acoustic cues through supervised machine learning techniques
articles.authors: Lena Heng
articles.abstract:
Humans modify their voices in various ways when expressing emotions vocally. Such timbral changes are not randoma consistent pattern of acoustic characteristics enables successful communication of emotion information. An understanding of the acoustic features used in expressing diﬀerent emotions is useful in many domains, including greater understanding of how humans comprehend emotion expression, applications in automatic emotion recognition tools, applications in emotional speech and music synthesis, improvement in hearing technologies, and aid in mediating communication challenges, both
in reception and in expression.
This study analyzes audio recordings from the RAVDESS dataset for various acoustic descriptors and derives a model of emotion classification through supervised machine learning (XGBoost) using this set of features. From the classification process, interpretable methods (SHAP, ALE) are utilized so as to explicitly derive contributions of features in emotion classification. This makes it possible to interpret the roles of individual acoustic features, in ways that traditional linear-model statistical analysis are unable to do.

articles.title: Revisiting the Four Stages of Creativity: The Role of Metacognition
articles.authors: Jeb Puryear; Kamila Urban; Marek Urban
articles.abstract: Wallas’ four-stage model has long served as a heuristic for understanding creativity, but contemporary metacognitive theory clarifies the mechanisms within each stage. This article reconceptualizes the model through the lens of metacognition, focusing on the role of knowledge, regulation, and experiences. In the Preparation stage, declarative, procedural, and conditional knowledge guide planning and problem representation. In the Incubation stage, stepping back from effortful processing while remaining open to associations represents a regulatory decision. In the Illumination stage, the sudden “Aha!” moment is best understood as a metacognitive experience that flags a candidate solution and signals further regulation. In the Verification stage, ideas are judged against internalized standards through monitoring, with subsequent regulatory decisions determining whether ideas are pursued, adapted, or discarded. Across stages, transitions are emphasized, and creativity is framed as a dynamic, self-regulatory system. Revisiting Wallas’ model in this way highlights that each stage involves distinct forms of knowledge, monitoring, and regulation, offering a more precise account of processes underlying his framework. This perspective yields testable hypotheses about stage-specific regulation and points to interventions, such as explicit instruction and metacognitive feedback, that can strengthen creativity.

articles.title: Single-Item Reliability for Intensive Longitudinal Data: A Three-Level Linear Mixed-Effect Autoregressive Modeling Approach
articles.authors: Tzu-Yao Lin; francis tuerlinckx; Sophie Vanbelle
articles.abstract: With the advancement of technology, measures are more and more often collected in real-time, resulting in intensive longitudinal data (ILD). ILD allow us to study dynamic properties of one or more variables, such as moment-to-moment fluctuations in affect or changes in heartbeat rates. Despite the widespread use of ILD, the issues of measurement error and reliability are often inadequately addressed, particularly in the presence of serial correlation and complex multilevel structures. This paper introduces a three-level linear mixed-effects model that explicitly accounts for the nested data structure and the serial correlation (i.e., moments within days, days within persons). The model incorporates two distinct autoregressive processes to capture serial correlations at both the moment and day levels, while simultaneously partitioning measurement error from within-person variability. Building on this model, we delineate two approaches to defining reliability: a variance-partitioning approach and a correlation approach. This framework yields a multifaceted set of reliability coefficients, enabling researchers to distinguish between-person from within-person reliability, and to assess measurement agreement across different time scales (e.g., moment-to-moment and day-to-day). We further discuss the relationship between the proposed reliability measures and those derived from simpler models. We apply our model to study the reliability of a single negative affect item in an ILD study in which students were measured multiple times a day for several days before and after receiving their exam results.

articles.title: Consolidating the measurement of intellectual humility: The Collected Intergroup Intellectual Humility (CIIH) scale
articles.authors: Philip Pärnamets; Jay Joseph Van Bavel; Anni Sternisko; Mark Alfano
articles.abstract: Intellectual virtues such as intellectual humility are dispositions to seek out and respond to evidence and the testimony of others in ways that are conducive to the acquisition, maintenance, and transmission of knowledge. In recent years there has been a growth of interest in this topic, with the development of ten different scales designed to capture individual differences in intellectual humility and related constructs. In a series of ten studies (N = 5,922), we analyze the psychometric properties of these measures to consolidate them into a single inventory with five subscales (open-mindedness, sense of intellectual superiority, intellectual defensiveness, intellectual arrogance, and tendency to criticize one’s ingroup). Given the importance of intellectual humility in intergroup relations, we intentionally include items designed to capture the presence (or lack) of intellectual humility in an intergroup context. These items allow us to capture distinct aspects of intellectual humility where people remain open-minded about the perspectives of outgroup members or turn a critical eye to the beliefs and reasoning of ingroup members. Our new inventory — the Collected Intergroup Intellectual Humility scale (CIIH) — demonstrates good convergent and discriminant validity, as well as the power to predict a range of important outcome variables such as acceptance/rejection of warranted/unwarranted conspiracy theories, medical (mis)information, and (fake) news, as well as to update credences in light of social feedback. CIIH offers strong psychometric properties and greater utility than existing measures for assessing the willingness to learn from a wide variety of individuals and groups. Our results suggest that the disposition to criticize oneself and the disposition to criticize one’s ingroup are distinct.

articles.title: Testing Alternative Approaches to Manipulation of Environmental Identity: Behavior Recall, Anchoring, and Category-Range Manipulations
articles.authors: Jan Urban; Štěpán Bahník; Markéta Braun Kohlová
articles.abstract: Environmental identity, defined as the extent to which an individual sees themselves as an environmentally friendly person, has become an important concept in environmental psychology. One of the reasons for the prominence of environmental identity is that it has been proposed as one of the causal factors of pro-environmental behavior. Experimental studies of environmental identity have relied on a recall manipulation that alters the frequency scales on which people report their past pro- and anti-environmental behaviors, thus making past pro- or anti-environmental behaviors more salient. In the current study, we propose two alternative approaches to experimental manipulation of environmental identity. These rely on two powerful strategies of changing the perception of one’s past behavior, namely anchoring manipulation and category-range manipulation. We conducted a high-power web-based study (N = 4,139) to assess and compare the efficacy of the behavioral-recall manipulation and the two alternative approaches of manipulating environmental identity and affecting pro-environmental intention. We found strong evidence that the three manipulations altered people's self-reports of their past pro-environmental behavior, but this had no effect on either environmental self-identity or environmental intention. Other manipulations of environmental identity that do not rely on self-perception theory are needed for experimental research on environmental identity. Practical applications are unlikely to affect pro-environmental behavior by highlighting or changing people's records of their past pro-environmental behavior.

articles.title: A Comparative Multi-Method Re-Analysis of the Longitudinal Evidence for Causal Intergroup Contact Effects on Attitudes
articles.authors: Maria-Therese Friehs; Sarina J. Schäfer; Kirsten Wüst; Carina Dürr; Lennart Engel-Wolters; Sebastian Emrys Kalkuhl; Tamara Böttcher; Günter Resch; Benedikt Sander; Lydia Tschekorsky Orloff
articles.abstract: Intergroup contact has been promoted as a powerful means to improve intergroup relations, but causal evidence for the effectiveness of contact in affecting outgroup attitudes is scare. One important and increasingly used approach to estimate causal effects in contact research relies on naturalistic longitudinal survey data. However, causal inference based on longitudinal data depends strongly on the control of (un)observed confounders. The cross-lagged panel model (CLPM), commonly used in contact research, is an analytical model that has been criticised for biasing causal inference by insufficiently accounting for unobserved confounders, and advanced statistical models have been proposed, including the full-forward (FF-CLPM) and random-intercept CLPM (RI-CLPM). To explore the extent of potential bias between the three models introduced by the varying level of control for unobserved confounders, we investigated the robustness of longitudinal positive and negative contact-attitude effects in a systematic re-analysis using 31 datasets from 21 published studies (N = 33,348). Using internal meta-analysis, we found substantial discrepancies between the models: While the CLPM provided significant longitudinal effects of both positive and negative intergroup contact on outgroup attitudes, analytical models that more strongly controlled for the effects of unobserved confounders found no such longitudinal evidence (FF-CLPM and RI-CLPM), but substantial between-person correlations which cannot be causally interpreted (RI-CLPM). Thus, the potential of intergroup contact to longitudinally affect outgroup attitudes in naturalistic survey data remains questionable. We discuss theoretical and methodological implications of these results and outline potential routes for future research to develop a more robust and informative longitudinal (contact) research.

articles.title: The Music-Evoked Kinesthetic Imagery Scale: A Novel Self-Report Measure of Imagined Movement During Music Listening
articles.authors: Karen Electra Christianson; Elizabeth Hellmuth Margulis
articles.abstract: While music is known to induce body movement and to stimulate the imagination of listeners, the convergence of these responses in the form of imagined movement has not yet been explored. A significant challenge is that existing methods targeting overt movement behaviors cannot detect sensations of movement that are felt internally but not physically expressed. This study presents a framework to systematically investigate imagined sensations of movement that arise during music listening. We introduce Music-Evoked Kinesthetic Imagery (MEKI) as a novel construct and develop a robust self-report scale that reliably measures individual differences in the frequency and contents of MEKI during everyday music listening. Exploratory and confirmatory factor analyses on two independent samples revealed the 14-item Music-Evoked Kinesthetic Imagery Scale (MEKIS) with three factors: Kinesthetic Self-Motion, Dance, and Music-Afforded Motion. All factors demonstrated good internal consistency, temporal stability, and convergent validity with related constructs. Across both samples, 93% of participants reported experiencing MEKI. We provide the first evidence that imagining movement is a prevalent way of engaging with music and, importantly, is a widely accessible listening behavior that does not require specialized training. As the first validated measure of imagined movement evoked by music, the MEKIS allows researchers to observe invisible, subjective experiences of movement that are a widespread but previously neglected component of music listening. It reveals a hidden dimension of how music captivates listeners and more broadly contributes a new perspective on kinesthetic imagery as a spontaneous phenomenon in everyday contexts.

articles.title: Automating abstract screening in research synthesis using large language models: A tutorial and proof-of-concept study
articles.authors: Mirka Henninger; Jan Radek; Jean-Paul Snijder; Martin Pauly
articles.abstract: Screening abstracts is a crucial yet labor-intensive step in research synthesis projects such as systematic reviews and meta-analyses. Large language models (LLMs) promise an opportunity to streamline and automate this process. However, there is still little guidance and practical insight into how such automated workflows can be implemented in research practice. In this article, we propose, describe, and illustrate an abstract screening procedure using LLMs including a quantification of uncertainty of model outputs. We provide a step-by-step tutorial in which we address how to select an LLM, access LLMs from within R, develop and refine suitable prompts, define structured output formats, and whether and how model hyperparameters should be set. We then illustrate this procedure in a proof-of-concept study using a set of human-rated abstracts from a recent meta-analysis for comparison. We demonstrate that LLM-assisted screening can substantially reduce the time and cost of review preparation while maintaining accuracy comparable to human raters. To support psychological researchers in adopting LLM-based workflows for research synthesis, we also provide R scripts and implementation guidance on Gitlab. At the same time, we emphasize that this work represents an initial step, and that continued refinement and validation are essential as LLM technologies and their applications continue to evolve rapidly.

articles.title: Meta analysis shows video-based monitoring technology  (Oxevision) has no statistically significant association with  four of five measures of patient safety
articles.authors: Hat Porter
articles.abstract:
A meta-analysis by Kekic, Rose and Bayley (2026) examines the outcomes associated with the installation of Oxevision (now rebranded as ‘LIO’) across six NHS Mental Health Trusts. Oxevision is a video-based monitoring technology used in mental health patient bedrooms, seclusion and police custody. It uses a video-camera, infrared, and video analytics to provide location-based alerts; measure pulse and respiration rate (if the patient is still); and display a live video of the patient for up to 15 seconds (LIO, 2026). Oxevision was once used by half of NHS Trusts in England, however, at the time of writing, the figure is now 36% after several NHS Trusts removed the technology (Stop Oxevision, 2026). 

In 2024, a systematic review concluded that there is “insufficient evidence to suggest that surveillance technologies in inpatient mental health settings are achieving their intended outcomes” (Griffiths et al., 2024, pg.52). Kekic, Rose and Bayley’s study is therefore timely. However, due to the lack of clarity and transparency in the reporting, in particular the failure to acknowledge the non-significance of the analyses, the article overstates and misrepresents its statistical conclusions. In addition to methodological and reporting limitations, Kekic, Rose and Bayley’s meta-analyses reveal that Oxevision does not result in statistically significant patient safety benefits, supporting calls, not for, but against its use

articles.title: Validation of the Teamwork-in-Engineering-Education Situational Judgment Test (TEE-SJT)
articles.authors: Chris C. Martin
articles.abstract: There are few measures to evaluate whether engineering students have the interpersonal skills that lead to effective teamwork. Many interventions that putatively teach interpersonal skills use team-level outcomes such as psychological safety to measure effectiveness instead of assessing skills themselves. To address this gap, we created and evaluated a Teamwork-in-Engineering-Education Situational Judgment Test (TEE-SJT). This situational judgment test presents conflict-driven scenarios to students and elicits responses about how they would act. Students respond to multiple-choice questions, which are graded according to a rubric. The TEE-SJT was developed using expert guidance and input from professors and students. To test the construct validity of the TEE-SJT, we used Westen and Rosenthal’s Quantifying Construct Validity (QCV) procedure, which jointly tests convergent and discriminant validity and outputs two precise effect sizes of construct validity. We hypothesized that both effect sizes would exceed .60. The hypotheses were pre-registered before data collection. Predicted convergent and discriminant correlations between the test and criterion variables were also preregistered. The criterion variables included Big Five trait facets (e.g., deliberation, empathy, anger proneness) and other constructs (e.g., need for closure, social dominance orientation). As hypothesized, the first construct validity effect size (r-alerting) was positive (.77) and greater than .60. The second effect size (r-contrast) was .55 but its 95% CI [.44, .64] included .60. These results indicate good construct validity. Serendipitously, the test was more responsive to variation in deliberation and honesty-humility than predicted. Further testing is needed to determine the predictive validity of the test.

articles.title: Cognitive Security: The study and practice of protecting the human mind and other Cognitive Assets from cognitive threats
articles.authors: Torvald Fossåen Ask; Stefan Sütterlin; Lea Müller; Ricardo Lugo; Dominic Saari; Hilkka Grahn; Matthew Canham; Daniel Hermansen; Benjamin J. Knox
articles.abstract: Technological and scientific advances in neuroscience, information technology, and artificial intelligence have expanded how human and machine cognition can be targeted, manipulated, and weaponized. These developments have heightened the need for a coherent framework of Cognitive Security. Following review of current definitions, conceptualizations, and applications of Cognitive Security across military, academic, and organizational sectors, we propose a unifying definition of Cognitive Security as ‘the state of having trusted boundaries protecting cognitive assets against all forms of unauthorized influence or access’. Cognitive assets are defined broadly, encompassing individual cognitive processes, artificial intelligence systems, organizational decision-making structures, and neurobiological substrates. Drawing on this definition, we identify four interdependent cognitive resilience factors essential to maintaining Cognitive Security: (1) cognitive agility, the ability to adaptively manage biases, information processing, pursued goals, and decision processes; (2) machine psychology, safeguarding cognition-like functions in AI and ML systems; (3) neurosecurity, protecting human and non-human neural systems from biological or technological intrusion; and (4) systems engineering, the design of organizational and societal structures that foster resilience. We argue that effective Cognitive Security requires a holistic, multi-level approach that integrates these factors to defend against cognitive warfare, cyber-enabled influence operations, and emerging threats in human–machine ecosystems. By consolidating fragmented perspectives into a domain- and species-agnostic framework, this paper advances Cognitive Security as a distinct discipline and provides a foundation for research, policy, and practice aimed at strengthening resilience to ensure cognitive safety and superiority.

articles.title: The double dissociation between cortical blindness and aphantasia: a review of 55 cases
articles.authors: Stefano Zago; Dounia Hajhajate; Alice Naomi Preti; Teresa Difonzo; Silvia Cattaneo; Paolo Bartolomeo
articles.abstract: A long-standing assumption in cognitive neuroscience is that visual mental imagery (VMI) is “visual perception in reverse” and, as such, requires reactivation of the primary visual cortex (V1). On this view, total destruction of V1 should abolish VMI and provoke neurological aphantasia. We tested this prediction by reviewing 55 published cases of cortical blindness (CB) in which VMI abilities were assessed. Vivid VMI is repeatedly documented in CB patients with complete or near-complete bilateral destruction, disconnection, or functional loss of V1; in impaired-VMI cases with informative anatomical or functional evidence, the lesion extends well beyond V1, typically into the left temporal cortex. No impaired-VMI case with informative anatomical or functional evidence shows damage confined to V1. These data are incompatible with a necessary role for V1 in VMI and instead align with recent neuroimaging and lesion evidence pointing to a left temporal hub (the fusiform imagery node, FIN) together with domain-preferring ventral temporal regions, embedded within frontoparietal working memory and semantic networks. We outline practical guidelines for future neuropsychological studies of visual mental imagery in CB.

articles.title: Prospective effects of marital satisfaction on postpartum depressive symptoms remain unresolved: A commentary on Ji et al. (2026)
articles.authors: Kimmo Sorjonen; Bo Melin; Marika Melin
articles.abstract: Ji et al. recently reported evidence suggesting that marital satisfaction serves as a longitudinal protective factor against postpartum depressive symptoms, basing this conclusion on statistically significant paths estimated with cross-lagged panel models (CLPMs). Nevertheless, methodological research has repeatedly demonstrated that cross-lagged coefficients can arise even when no genuine prospective influence exists. In the present commentary, we reexamined data simulated to reproduce the sample characteristics and correlational structure reported by Ji et al. Depending on the analytic approach adopted, marital satisfaction was associated with decreases, increases, or no detectable changes in later postpartum depressive symptoms. Moreover, when these conflicting estimates were synthesized using random-effects meta-analysis, the resulting overall effect was not statistically significant. These findings indicate that the conclusions advanced by Ji et al. may not be sufficiently supported by the available evidence. We recommend that investigators evaluate prospective associations across multiple plausible analytic specifications and place greater emphasis on conclusions that are robust across models.

articles.title: Binary Path Sort: A Sorting Algorithm to Assess the Ranking Order of a Single Respondent
articles.authors: Markus Thomas Jansen; Philipp Doebler
articles.abstract: Preference based sorting is integral to experimental research and applications across various disciplines, including psychology, marketing, and education. Under the transitivity assumption, it is not necessary to compare all objects directly to determine the preferred order. The field of computer science offers a rich literature on comparison-based sorting algorithms, presenting numerous strategies for the adaptive selection of pairwise comparisons. Typically, the focus is on minimizing resource utilization, such as computer memory. However, in many fields, particularly in psychology, comparisons are made by human respondents. Here, the key considerations are a low number of expected pairwise comparisons and a robust performance in worst-case scenarios. This study introduces Binary Path Sort (BPS), a novel and efficient sorting algorithm based on a priority queue. It will be demonstrated that BPS requires fewer pairwise comparisons in the worst-case scenario compared to the currently best priority queue-based sorting algorithm. Moreover, BPS is equivalent in terms of the number of comparison required for best, average, and worst-case scenarios to the non-priority queue algorithm Mergesort. A simulation study is presented to illustrate the avoidance of worst-case scenarios and which elements are compared. Finally, the study discusses the implications of these findings, particularly for forced-choice measurement applications.

articles.title: Developing the AAA Assessment Battery and a Multimodal Diagnostic Model for Stress-Related Disorders
articles.authors: Sedric A Newman; Ashley Spencer; Caleb Anthony Rios
articles.abstract: The goal of this paper is to develop an RDoC-aligned precision psychiatry framework and multi-modal model for the implementation of precision psychiatry; by combining findings and theories from the fields of neurobiology, genetics, and psychology. Modern research has greatly advanced our knowledge of how disruptions in brain networks involve emotion, cognition, and behavior regulation in underlying mental disorders. However, research in these fields have yet to form a standardized framework for psychiatric disorders, limiting their effective application in clinical practice. Therefore, the secondary goal is to explain how interventions from these fields can support a more precise diagnosis and treatment planning framework, as well as identify the biological and neural pathways implicated in stress-related disorders. The integration of these methods allows for the identification of psychological profiles associated with differential responsiveness to interventions, such as psychotherapy and pharmacology. This responsiveness can be influenced by epigenetic regulation through molecular pathways associated with stress-related biological dysfunctions, learning, and neural plasticity. We also address common ethical, clinical, and implementation concerns surrounding precision psychiatry, including risks of stigmatization, limited generalizability, and discuss ways to reduce barriers to real-world clinical adoption. By combining epigenetic, neural circuit, and behavioral measures, this approach provides a framework for understanding how early life adversity, attachment, and individual differences interact to shape treatment response, offering a pathway toward more individualized and effective care.

articles.title: “It is very much a body language and vibe class”:  How do learning-disabled children and young people in a UK special school communicate, and what does this mean for inclusive research?
articles.authors: Kathryn Asbury; Laura Fox; Kayleigh Doyle
articles.abstract: Learning-disabled children and young people (CYP) are entitled to express their views, yet their voices are rarely heard in research. This is partly a result of researchers’ limited understanding of, and competence in using, relevant communication strategies. We conducted a nine-week team-based ethnographic study of learning-disabled CYP (n=25) in a specialist school in England.  We documented our observations in 197,776 words of ethnographic fieldnotes and 12 staff interview transcripts.  We analysed the data using both template analysis and reflexive thematic analysis, developing four themes related to deliberate and non-deliberate communication. We discuss the implications of our observations, introducing a new online library of inclusive data collection approaches based on our findings, known as ADLib (Accessing Disability Library).  We call for a concerted global research effort to co-produce a participant-centred and capability-supportive approach to research, using ADLib as an open-access and open-source platform.

articles.title: Covert attention dynamically shapes decision making
articles.authors: Amy Xiangjin Li; Benjamin Chan; Sofya Donets; Sage Boettcher; Laurence Hunt
articles.abstract: Decision-making is driven by where attention is allocated during choice. Humans can attend through both overt eye gaze as well as covertly, yet investigations of attention’s role in choice have been largely confined to overt eye movements. Here, we combine an attentional probe with value-based choice to directly measure how covert attention is allocated to peripheral options during decision-making. Results revealed that covert attention itself is dynamically shaped by decision-relevant factors, such as the decision relevance and value of peripheral options, and comes at the expense of what is overtly attended. Further, covert attention exerted downstream consequences for choice behavior, attenuating fixation-linked choice biases. By demonstrating that attentional influences on decision-making extend beyond eye gaze, our findings challenge current gaze-based accounts of choice, and motivate decision-making models that incorporate covert attentional mechanisms alongside overt attentional dynamics.

articles.title: Exploring cultural and individual differences in the hierarchy of moral concern: Evidence from student samples across 41 cultural groups
articles.authors: Işıl Ayça Akkuş; Charlie R Crimston; Kelly Kirkland; Brock Bastian; Bastian Jaeger
articles.abstract: Disagreement about the rights and protections owed to different individuals underpins many important historical and current debates (e.g., on the abolishment of slavery, the feminist movement, abortion, and environmental protection). Recent work on moral concern (i.e., the subjectively felt moral obligation to consider an individual’s welfare and interests) has uncovered a seemingly robust hierarchy. For example, people extend most moral concern toward family and friends, followed by ingroup members (e.g., a co-worker), outgroup members (e.g., a foreign citizen), the environment (e.g., a coral reef), and, lastly, criminals (e.g., a murderer). However, these studies were all conducted in a few countries from the Anglosphere, raising doubt about the cross-cultural generalizability of the results. We analyzed a data set with 6,665 participants recruited from 41 cultural groups to explore cultural and individual variation in the hierarchy of moral concern. We find that (1) on average, across the 41 cultural groups, 11 of the 13 rankings in the typical hierarchy of moral concern replicated, pointing to substantial cultural similarities; (2) countries that are culturally more similar to the reference sample from the initial U.S. study showed a more similar hierarchy; (3) some rankings (e.g., prioritizing family and friends over human ingroups) replicated more often than others (e.g., prioritizing human outgroups over the environment) across countries; and (4) both cultural and individual differences were largest most pronounced examining people’s concern for non-human animals and the environment.

articles.title: Structure, dynamics and origin of the phenomenal space
articles.authors: Léa Moncoucy; Inès Mentec; Axel Cleeremans
articles.abstract: According to structuralist views of experience, red feels red because it feels closer to pink than to green. A conscious experience corresponds to a point in the phenomenal space, and its qualitative character is determined by the distances between that point and other experiences along the dimensions of the space. In this paper, we propose a new model divided into four claims. 1. The structure of the phenomenal space reflects our sensory feelings, structured along intra-modal dimensions. Intra-modal dimensions are defined by their systematic co-variance, which echoes the definition of a sensory modality as sensorimotor contingencies. 2. Trans-modal dimensions such as valence or confidence explain multisensory interactions and allow similarity evaluation among different modalities. 3. The phenomenal space evolves at two timescales: short-term dynamics are influenced by cognitive factors such as attention, and long-term dynamics occur with the acquisition of expertise (accumulation of experiences along one dimension). 4. There is no pre-constructed space prior to experience, and the incoming sensory stimulations gradually outline the dimensions of the space. Overall, this paper proposes a framework about the structure, dynamics and origin of the phenomenal space.

articles.title: Benevolent Childhood Experiences, Adversity, and Emotion Regulation as Pathways to Adolescent Mental Wellbeing: A Longitudinal Test of Promotive and Protective Mechanisms
articles.authors: Stephanie Cahill; Neil Humphrey
articles.abstract: Adolescence is marked by intersecting exposures to adversity and access to positive developmental experiences, yet how these jointly shape wellbeing through emerging regulatory capacities is not well understood. We tested a pre-registered three-wave longitudinal moderated mediation model in 18,616 adolescents nested within 136 secondary schools from the #BeeWell Greater Manchester programme, with cumulative adversity at Year 8, emotion regulation at Year 9, and wellbeing at Year 10 (controlling for baseline wellbeing). Benevolent childhood experiences (BCEs) were tested as both a direct promotive asset and a moderator, with moderation estimated co-primary at first stage (adversity to emotion regulation) and second stage (emotion regulation to wellbeing). Adversity predicted lower wellbeing largely through impaired emotion regulation, and BCEs directly raised both emotion regulation and wellbeing at all levels of adversity. A dissociation between the two moderation placements emerged: BCEs amplified rather than buffered the first-stage pathway, with no moderation at the second stage. A sub-domain decomposition identified parent/carer support as the principal driver of this amplification, while school connectedness operated as a direct promotive lift without moderation. Pathway magnitudes were equivalent across sex/gender identity and ethnicity, but minoritised young people faced markedly higher adversity exposure. The findings show that BCEs are valuable developmental assets in their own right but do not function as classical buffers in adolescence, and that the upstream task for reducing inequality lies in reducing identity-related adversity rather than differential regulatory training.

articles.title: A Cluster Randomised Controlled Trial of a Transdiagnostic Preventative Mental Health Intervention for Adolescents: Building Resilience through Socioemotional Training (ReSET)
articles.authors: Alex Lloyd; Pasco Fearon; Peter Martin; Roslyn Law; Laura Lucas; Tom Chin-Han Wu; Nick Midgley; Nikolaus Steinbeis; René Veenstra; Jaime Smith
articles.abstract:
Abstract
Background: Rates of mental health problems in adolescents have increased in the last decade, prompting urgent calls for effective, scalable preventative interventions. To determine whether a novel, group-based, transdiagnostic indicated prevention programme (Building Resilience through Socioemotional Training; ReSET), delivered through existing school mental health provision, improves mental health and wellbeing in at-risk adolescents.
Methods: Two-arm cluster randomised controlled superiority trial (1:1) evaluating ReSET plus usual care versus usual care only, with outcomes assessed immediately post-intervention and at 12-month follow-up. Adolescents aged 12–14 identified through whole-school screening as scoring in the top 25th percentile for overall psychopathology symptoms, in schools selected to over-represent high-need communities (≥30% free school meals eligibility). ReSET is an 8-session group -based intervention that targets two key mechanisms implicated in risk for psychopathology in adolescents – social functioning and emotional processing. Pre-registered co-primary outcomes were overall self-reported psychopathology (SDQ) and wellbeing (WEMWBS), assessed immediately post-intervention and at 12-month follow-up.
Results: Of 4,005 adolescents screened, 1465 met eligibility, 727 consented and 560 were enrolled. There was no evidence of intervention effects on either co-primary outcome immediately post-intervention. At 12-month follow-up, there was evidence of improvement in overall psychopathology (adjusted mean difference −1.36, 97.5% CI −2.23 to −0.50, p=0.0004; SMD=0.25, 95% CI 0.11 to 0.42). Secondary outcomes showed a consistent pattern: no effects immediately post-intervention, but significant benefits for anxiety, depression, general psychopathology, and internalising symptoms at 12-month follow-up. No effects on externalising symptoms were observed at either timepoint.
Conclusions: The delayed emergence of benefit is consistent with a skills-based prevention model in which adolescents acquire skills that support resilient functioning when facing subsequent emotional and interpersonal challenges. These findings suggest that transdiagnostic interventions targeting socioemotional processes have the potential to prevent common adolescent mental health problems, though replication and longer-term evaluation are needed.

articles.title: Basic argument structure is adult-like by 30 months: An evaluation of half a million child utterances
articles.authors: Trevor K. M. Day; Brian Reese
articles.abstract:
Abstract
Children rapidly acquire a wide range of linguistic skills, from perceptual to pragmatic over the first few years of life. Syntax, the ability to construct and infer meaning from word order, blossoms around 2 years. Within syntax, some of the most widely studied features are the development of subjects and objects. However, computational identification of subjects and objects in natural language is not yet a solved problem. In this paper, we use a pre-trained machine-learning model to identify internal and external arguments from child speech, and show that it functions with high sensitivity and specificity for both subjects and objects compared to hand coding. We demonstrate this model is suitable for application to child speech, and show growth curves where children plateau at rates of argument use similar to adult speech between 2 and 3 years.

articles.title: Climate Advocacy and Activism by Scientists: A Narrative Review
articles.authors: Samuel Finnerty; Maien S. M. Sachisthal; Viktoria Cologna; Fabian Dablander; Lydia Messling; Christel van Eck
articles.abstract: In the context of accelerating climate and environmental crises, advocacy and activism by scientists is becoming increasingly prominent. This growing visibility has intensified the debate surrounding the effectiveness of such engagement and its impact on scientific credibility, public trust, and professional norms.
