Yet, research on scientist climate advocacy is dispersed across disciplines, methodologies, and forms of advocacy, often yielding divergent findings. In this narrative review, we map, summarize, and synthesize the existing conceptual and empirical research on the motivations, constraints, and consequences of climate and environmental advocacy by scientists. Across this literature, we find that the effects of scientist climate advocacy are context dependent, shaped by the dynamic interplay between the advocate, the audience, the mode of advocacy, and the wider political and social context. We identify key factors that help explain when and why climate advocacy enhances or undermines credibility, trust, and influence. Finally, we discuss conceptual, methodological, and empirical gaps, and highlight priorities for future research on responsible and effective scientist engagement.

articles.title: Comparing the Beta-Binomial and the Linear Models to Analyze Discrete, Bounded Scores for Normative Data from Neuropsychological Tests
articles.authors: Javier Oltra-Cucarella; Rafael de Andrade Moral; Rubén Pérez-Elvira; Beatriz Bonete-López; Miriam Sánchez-Sansegundo; Esther Sitges-Maciá
articles.abstract: Using data from 165 cognitively healthy individuals, we compared the linear model with the binomial and the beta-binomial models to interpret performance on a verbal memory test. Model adequacy was assessed using half-normal plots with a simulated envelope. Goodness-of-fit was assessed using AIC and BIC. Four examples were developed to show the advantages of the beta-binomial model. Compared to the linear model (BIC = 583.4), the binomial models, especially the beta-binomial, showed better model fit to the real data (BIC = 463.6), did not predicted scores without the real range, and showed a better model adequacy (points out of the simulated envelope for linear = 32.73%, beta-binomial = 1.82%). The beta-binomial model is more adequate than the linear model for discrete, bounded data from neuropsychological tests. R scripts are provided for researchers and clinicians to calculate randomized quantile residuals from beta-binomial models

articles.title: EEG Predictors of Transition from Clinical High Risk to Psychotic Disorders: A Systematic Review with Implications for Data Harmonisation
articles.authors: Finn Brady; Anja Stanojlovic; Sean Naughton; Mary Clarke; Keith Gaynor; Klaus Kessler
articles.abstract:
Background
Prognostic markers capable of predicting future conversion from a clinical high-risk (CHR) state to psychosis are vital for early intervention. Although electroencephalography (EEG) abnormalities have been associated with psychotic disorders, their prognostic utility in CHR populations remains unclear. This systematic review examined baseline EEG differences between CHR individuals who later transitioned to psychosis (CHR-T) and those who did not (CHR-NT).

Methods
Following PRISMA guidelines, Pubmed, CINAHL and APA Psycarticles were searched in March 2026, with additional studies identified through hand searching. Studies comparing EEG markers between CHR-T and CHR-NT groups were included. A tailored narrative synthesis was used to summarise findings due to methodological heterogeneity. Study quality was assessed with Diagnostic Accuracy Studies and Clinical Appraisal of Prognostic Studies. Meta-analyses of Event-Related Potentials (ERPs) were performed where data were comparable.

Results
Out of 1,032 screened studies, 34 met inclusion criteria. Findings on EEG prognostic value were mixed. Random-effects meta-analyses yielded pooled effect sizes indicating that Mismatch Negativity (MMN; k = 14, g = -1.05 to 0.54) and P300 ERPs (k = 9, g = -0.28 to -0.52) showed the highest consistency in predicting conversion to psychosis with frequent medium to large effect size. 

Discussion
The findings underscore the difficulty of achieving reliable predictive sensitivity of EEG biomarkers during the clinically and diagnostically heterogeneous CHR period. Substantial inconsistency across EEG methodologies and outcome definitions likely contributes to variability in reported prognostic effects. Recommendations are provided to improve methodological consistency and support future biomarker research.

Keywords: Psychosis, Prodrome, EEG, MMN, P300, Theta

articles.title: Efficacy of Imitation Training for Individuals with Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Systematic Review
articles.authors: Hooshang Dadgar; Zahra Moshtaghi Fard; Yang Zhang
articles.abstract: This review examined imitation training for children with autism. Based on 38 articles—including 27 single-subject designs and 6 randomized controlled trials (RCTs)—findings suggest imitation-based approaches, especially reciprocal imitation training (RIT), may be a helpful first option. These approaches can lead to moderate-to-strong gains in imitation skills, potentially supporting growth in other social/developmental areas. Effectiveness may depend on the child's age, delivery format, imitation type, and existing skills. Structured methods might bring quick progress, while naturalistic, socially responsive approaches could lead to more lasting and transferable skills. New methods like telehealth and robot-assisted training appear promising but need more study. Overall, imitation training seems to work best when started early, tailored to the child, and done with care.

articles.title: Relational Specificity of Mentalizing and Structural Personality Functioning in Adult Attachment Relationships
articles.authors: Karolin Holy; Tobias Nolte; Sebastian Walther; Sarah Kittel-Schneider; Martin Walter; Anna Linda Leutritz
articles.abstract:
Mentalizing - the ability to understand one's own and others' experience and behavior in terms of thoughts, feelings, and intentions - and personality functioning (e.g., affect experiencing, self-regulation, regulation of object relations) is central to adult attachment relationships. However, whether these capacities vary within individuals across relational contexts remains underexplored.
This preregistered cross-sectional study examined within-person differences in reflective functioning (Interest and Curiosity; Certainty about Mental States) and personality functioning across romantic partner versus primary parental attachment contexts. In a community sample of 100 German-speaking adults in romantic relationships, participants completed context-specific self-report measures of mentalizing (reflective functioning and general mentalizing deficits), personality functioning (OPD-SQ subscales), relationship-specific stress and self-efficacy, attachment anxiety and avoidance, and depressive symptoms.
Linear mixed-effects models indicated significantly higher Interest and Curiosity and Certainty about Mental States in the partner context compared to the parental attachment context. Participants also
T his is a provisional file, not the final typeset article 2
reported lower levels of relationship-specific stress and fewer impairments in self-regulation and
affect experiencing in the partner context. No significant context differences emerged for regulation
of object relations or relationship-specific self-efficacy.
Multiple regressions identified relationship-specific self-efficacy as the strongest, most consistent
correlate of both reflective functioning (particularly Certainty about Mental States) and capacities in
parental contexts. Conversely, attachment insecurity, mentalizing deficits, and relational stress
predicted regulatory difficulties primarily in partner contexts.
Exploratory analyses showed small associations between perceived parental relationship quality and
participants' romantic functioning.
These findings suggest that mentalizing and personality functioning differ across adult attachment
relationships. Perceived relational competence may be a key factor shaping reflective capacities,
especially in biographically significant relationships with one’s parents.
This manuscript has been submitted for publication and has not yet undergone peer review.

articles.title: Is everyone looking at me? The perception of direct gaze in social anxiety and its moderation by emotional expression
articles.authors: Madeleine Moses-Payne; Georgina Krebs; Raphaelle Delpech; Tosia Przyborowska; Argyris Stringaris
articles.abstract: The “Cone of Gaze” (CoG), a measure of the range of gaze angles that one perceives to be looking at them, has been proposed as a biobehavioural marker of psychiatric disorders characterised by symptoms in the social domain, such as social anxiety disorder. However, the evidence remains inconsistent and its mechanisms unclear. Here, we combine meta-analysis and well-powered experimental replications to investigate: 1) individual differences in the CoG between those with and without social anxiety; 2) within-subjects differences in the CoG in response to faces with angry versus neutral expressions; and 3) the interaction between these factors. The meta-analyses revealed significantly wider CoG for socially anxious versus non-anxious individuals (representing 10 studies and 403 participants), and wider CoG for angry versus neutral expressions (9 studies, 523 participants). However, the existing evidence was sparse, highly heterogeneous and often involved small samples. Our experiments (N=514) did not replicate the effect of social anxiety or emotional expression on CoG. Our integrated approach, combining meta-analyses with experimental replications and extensions, provides a rigorous framework for assessing the robustness and clinical relevance of gaze perception effects. We highlight the need for larger, more transparent datasets to accurately estimate CoG effects and other putative markers to assess their translational potential.

articles.title: Selective attenuation of neural responses to own infant's face in early motherhood: A longitudinal MEG study
articles.authors: Elisa Vuoriainen; Piia Astikainen; Xueqiao Li; Qianru Xu; Santeri Yrttiaho; Mikko J. Peltola
articles.abstract: The transition to parenthood is associated with heightened neural responsiveness to infant cues, particularly to one’s own infant. Yet it remains unclear how this sensitivity changes with caregiving experience. Magnetoencephalography was recorded from 16 first time mothers viewing images of their own infant, an unfamiliar infant, and an unfamiliar adult at two timepoints spanning infancy and early toddlerhood. We examined early orbitofrontal activity (~140 ms), structural face encoding (M170, ~170 ms), and later motivated attention indexed by the late positive potential (LPP, ~300–600 ms). Source-level brain data were analyzed with a depth-weighted minimum-norm estimate. Results showed that early LPP responses in the fusiform cortex and late LPP responses in fusiform and occipital regions were larger for own infant faces at the first session but decreased to the level of other faces by the second session. In contrast, early orbitofrontal activity and M170 responses were not modulated by face type or session, and M170 responses showed high temporal stability. These findings indicate that neural activity associated with motivated attention to one’s own infant attenuates across early motherhood, whereas early responses reflecting perceptual and affective processes remain stable. The findings provide longitudinal evidence for changes in maternal neural reactivity to one’s own infant.

articles.title: Visuospatial Orienting in Working Memory is Preserved in Adults with ADHD Symptoms
articles.authors: Luísa Superbia-Guimarães; Amy Louise Atkinson; Richard John Allen; Valerie Camos
articles.abstract: People with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) are reported to have pervasive deficits in working memory (WM), yet mechanistic explanations for such deficits are lacking. Research suggests that attentional mechanisms such as reward-based prioritisation and visuospatial orienting are intact in this population (Atkinson et al., 2025; Superbia-Guimarães et al., 2022, 2023). To further examine these mechanisms, we investigated the hypotheses that deficient visuospatial orienting during encoding and maintenance are at the root of WM deficits in adults with ADHD symptoms.  Eighty-four symptomatic participants (Mage = 28.4 years, SD = 4.6) and 107 controls (Mage = 29.4, SD = 4.4) had their symptoms assessed with the Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale. They each took part in one of two experiments involving an item (colour or unfamiliar shape) recognition memory task that incorporated pre- and retro-cues. Across both experiments, we found strong evidence of beneficial pre- and retro-cueing effects upon memory accuracy and reaction time (RT). However, we found no evidence for the expected group differences nor the interaction between symptom status and cueing condition. There was also no evidence for correlations between ADHD symptom scores, recognition accuracy, RT, and cueing benefits. Therefore, attentional mechanisms are unlikely to explain deficits in WM in individuals with symptoms of ADHD.

articles.title: Imagining oneself versus the object affects response times but not accuracy: Evidence from mental paper folding and mental body rotations
articles.authors: Stephan Frederic Dahm; Carla Czilczer; Marcos Moreno-Verdú; Pierre Sachse
articles.abstract: Although mental imagery of movements (action imagery) can be conceptualized through kinesthetic self-imagery and visual object-imagery, the exact role of agency on performance outcomes is still unclear. The present study examined agency-specific and cross-agency relationships in mental paper folding and mental body rotations. 142 participants completed both tasks twice, once using self-imagery and once using object-imagery. Manipulation checks confirmed that instructions successfully shifted subjective imagery experiences, with self-imagery emphasizing bodily sensations and object-imagery emphasizing visualization. Performance analyses revealed robust task-specific effects: mental paper folding difficulty increased with the number of folds, and mental body rotations were faster and more accurate for back-view and hand stimuli than for front-view and foot stimuli. Crucially, no significant differences in errors emerged between self-imagery and object-imagery. However, response times were longer in object-imagery than self-imagery in mental body rotations. In mental paper folding, response times were shorter in object-imagery than self-imagery. This result was modulated by the order in which these were used, indicating a potential carryover-effect of self-referential processing from self-imagery to object-imagery. Correlation analyses showed moderate, significant associations within the same agency (self-imagery folding with self-imagery rotation; object-imagery folding with object-imagery rotation) but not across instructions, suggesting agency-specific processes. The results indicate that self-imagery and object-imagery instructions shape subjective experience, agency-specific correlations and response times suggesting that self-imagery and object-imagery rely on partially distinct representational systems.

articles.title: The Neuroscience of Metaphor as Abstraction
articles.authors: Bálint Forgács
articles.abstract: Metaphor has been suggested to be one of the pinnacles of human cognition, yet the neural underpinnings of figurative language is hotly debated and findings are often controversial. The goal of the present paper is to demonstrate that the available neuroscientific evidence on metaphor opens a window on an essential core of human language. Contrary to the widely shared assumptions of embodied cognition, metaphor reveals the fundamentally abstract functioning of the language system in its pristine form. Following an overview of the diverse neuroscientific (fMRI, EEG, etc.) and behavioral results, and a critical evaluation of the available theories, the paper proposes a novel model for metaphor. Recent experimental data shows that metaphor is not processed serially (cf. the structuralist linguistic and the Gricean pragmatic traditions) but as any other form of (literal) language, and its contents are not provided by sensorimotor systems (cf. embodiment) but recovered through an “abstractness effect”. Since current theories are not able to account for these data or are hampered by structural controversies, a novel model, Abstract Conceptual Substitution, is proposed: metaphor involves a particular polysemy-like meaning disambiguation, where concrete semantic features are suppressed, and abstract properties are enhanced. Figurative meaning is a vivid byproduct of a fundamental feature of the language system that allows the abstract interpretation of concrete, perceptual, physical words, demonstrating that the language processor operates based on symbolic, rather than sensorimotor representations. “Metaphorization” plays an important role in everyday social negotiations by creating parallel layers of meaning that allow for strategic ambiguity.

articles.title: Memory-driven attentional capture is not explained by inefficiënt task switching
articles.authors: Caro Hautekiet; Jarrod Lewis-Peacock; Christian Nicolas Leon Olivers
articles.abstract: Actively maintained information in working memory (WM) has been shown to automatically bias attention toward memory-matching information in the environment, even when detrimental to the task. This has been taken as evidence for WM and visual attention being closely intertwined. Here we test the alternative hypothesis that memory-driven attentional biases arise due to inefficient task switching, such that observers might initially look for the memorized content in the environment even when it is not relevant for the new task. To test this hypothesis, in two pre-registered experiments, participants memorized a color that had to be reproduced at the end of each trial. In between, they performed a visual search task including a distractor in a color related or unrelated to the memorized color. Critically, participants were explicitly cued to focus attention either on the search task or on the memory task (Experiment 1), or were presented with an interruption task (Experiment 2), in order to aid disengagement from the memory task and switching towards the search task. Across both experiments, we replicated the memory-driven attentional capture effect, with slower responses when a related distractor was present than when it was unrelated. Critically, none of the manipulations aimed to disengage attention from the memory task convincingly eliminated this bias. These findings indicate that memory-driven attentional capture cannot be fully explained by inefficient task switching, and further support the view that representations maintained in WM automatically bias attentional selection.

articles.title: Expanding Understanding of University Students' Agency: A Multidimensional Analysis of Student-Perceived Resources, Stances, and Actions of Agency, and Their Relation to Teaching Practices
articles.authors: Päivikki Jääskelä; Jingwen Jiang; Asko Tolvanen; Maarit Arvaja; Elina Vaara; Anna-Maija Poikkeus
articles.abstract: Comprehensive, empirically grounded and multidimensional conceptualizations of student agency in higher education remain limited, which impedes the accumulation of empirical knowledge on agency, its variation across individuals and courses, and its links to teaching-related factors. In this study, multilevel modeling was used to examine university students’ perceptions of their agency resources, agentic stances and actions in a total of 15 dimensions. We analyzed variance attributed to individual and course levels, as well as interrelations among agency dimensions and their associations with student-perceived teaching practices. The sample comprised 1,140 students across 47 courses at a Finnish university. The results indicated moderate to high mean levels of agency overall. However, multilevel analyses revealed that students’ agency varies within and between courses, with some dimensions such as opportunities for interaction as resource showing particularly high between-course variance. Dimensions capturing resources, stances, and actions showed moderate to strong intercorrelations. Furthermore, teaching practices such as transparent course design practices, learning activities involving higher-order thinking, and teacher roles characterized by supervision rather than management were found to be positively associated with student agency. The study advances the conceptual understanding of student agency as a multidimensional phenomenon and empirically demonstrates how it manifests and varies across university courses. It also extends empirical evidence on links between agency and teaching practices. The findings underscore the importance of pedagogical choices in courses, such as incorporating learning activities that challenge students’ thinking, orchestrating interactions that support personal learning, and adopting course design practices that actively involve students.

articles.title: Adaptive Prediction in Heritage Bilingualism: Morphosyntactic Cues, Word Order and Language Engagement in Turkish
articles.authors: Fatih Bayram; Jason Rothman; Marit Westergaard
articles.abstract: This study investigates predictive processing in heritage Turkish, focusing on how morphosyntactic cues, word order and language engagement shape real-time comprehension. Using a web-based visual world eye-tracking paradigm, 219 Turkish–German heritage speakers and 102 monolingual Turkish speakers completed a sentence-comprehension task involving transitive sentences varying in case marking and word order. The monolingual group was included for methodological validation only. Time-continuous gaze analyses showed that heritage speakers used case marking to anticipate referential roles before verb onset across structures, although timing and strength varied by condition. Predictive processing depended not simply on the presence of an early cue, but on how morphosyntactic and positional information unfolded and were integrated over the sentence. Crucially, predictive efficiency scaled with overall Turkish engagement, speakers with greater Turkish engagement showed earlier and stronger anticipatory fixations. These findings show that heritage grammars retain active morphosyntactic predictive mechanisms shaped by language experience.

articles.title: Internal Structure, Reliability, and Gender-Related Item Functioning of the Borderline Personality Inventory
articles.authors: Emilia Soroko; Paweł Kleka; Lidia W. Cierpiałkowska; Falk Leichsenring
articles.abstract: The study evaluated the psychometric properties of the Polish version of Leichsenring's Borderline Personality Inventory (BPI), a self-report tool grounded in Kernberg's theory of personality pathology (borderline personality organization) and borderline personality disorder. A gender- and age-matched sample of 600 participants (aged 18-36) was drawn from an initial pool of 1,640 individuals. Internal consistency of the BPI was acceptable across subscales, with high composite reliability. Confirmatory factor analysis supported a four-factor model reflecting Diffusion, Primitive Defences, Impaired Reality Testing, and Fear of Fusion. Multidimensional item response theory (mIRT) analyses indicated greater measurement precision at higher levels of BPI-defined borderline personality organization. Differential Item Functioning was examined using the Mantel-Haenszel method. After Holm correction, no item showed statistically significant gender-related DIF, supporting the comparability of item functioning across women and men in this sample. These findings support the use of the Polish version of the BPI in research settings and suggest its potential applicability in clinical assessment, particularly within object relations-based frameworks.

articles.title: Electroconvulsive Therapy (ECT) in Depression: The Role of Anhedonia
articles.authors: Sandro Menegola; Sarah Ulrich; Magdalena D. Ridder; Gunnar Deuring; Annette Brühl; Else Schneider
articles.abstract:
Background. Anhedonia is a core symptom of depression associated with a poor disease course, high symptom burden, and non-response to standard therapies. As first-line treatments exhibit limited effects on anhedonia, alternative interventions with robust antianhedonic properties are urgently needed. Clinical observations and preliminary studies suggest that electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) might alleviate anhedonic symptoms; however, empirical evidence remains scarce. 
Aims. To address this research gap, we evaluated the effectiveness of ECT in reducing anhedonia and explored its temporal relationship with general depressive symptoms. 
Method. Method In a clinical sample of 67 patients with depression undergoing ECT, anhedonia (Snaith–Hamilton Pleasure Scale) and depression severity (Montgomery–Åsberg Depression Rating Scale; Beck Depression Inventory-II) were assessed across four time points: pre-ECT, after the sixth session, post-ECT, and at a 2–3 month follow-up during the maintenance phase. Data were analysed using multilevel models, cross-lagged panel models, and response and remission analyses. 
Results. Clinically meaningful improvements in anhedonia occurred after the sixth session (d = –0.73, 95% CI [–1.00, –0.46], p < .001), peaked at post-treatment (d = –1.07, 95% CI [–1.36, –0.78], p < .001), and were largely sustained at follow-up (d = –0.84, 95% CI [–1.15, –0.54], p < .001). Exploratory analyses suggested that reductions in anhedonia after the sixth session may preceded and predicted subsequent alleviation of broader depressive symptoms during acute ECT. 
Conclusions. ECT exerted rapid, clinically meaningful effects on anhedonia. Thus, ECT could be considered earlier in treatment algorithms for patients with pronounced anhedonia and prior resistance to standard therapies, particularly in severe or urgent cases of depression and anhedonia. Future studies using intensive longitudinal designs are needed to clarify whether improvements in anhedonia act as a potential catalyst for broader depressive symptoms in ECT.

articles.title: PSYCHOMETRIC VALIDATION OF THE AZERBAIJANI VERSION OF THE ATTITUDES TOWARDS MENTAL HEALTH PROBLEMS SCALE (ATMHPS-AZ) AMONG UNIVERSITY STUDENTS: THE ROLE OF CULTURAL FACTORS
articles.authors: Nigar Shahhuseynbayova; Aynur Bunyatova; Mushviq Mustafayev; Yasuhiro Kotera
articles.abstract: Attitudes toward mental health problems and the experience of shame are significant barriers to help-seeking among university students, particularly in cultural contexts where family expectations, social norms, and concerns about disclosure shape behaviour. In Azerbaijan, a culturally validated instrument to assess these attitudes was lacking. This study developed and psychometrically validated an Azerbaijani version of the 35-item Attitudes Towards Mental Health Problems Scale (ATMHPS) among 946 undergraduates. Following WHO guidelines, the scale was translated and culturally adapted. Analyses included descriptive statistics, internal consistency (Cronbach’s α, McDonald’s ω), inter-subscale correlations, exploratory and confirmatory factor analyses, convergent validity with the Self-Compassion Scale, criterion-related validity with the Stigma-9 Questionnaire, and regression analyses of demographic and cultural factors. Results showed excellent total reliability (α = 0.954, ω = 0.956), supported the original seven-factor structure, and indicated moderate stigma overall, highest in Disclosure Concerns and lowest in Family Attitudes and Family Burden. Year of study emerged as the only statistically significant (albeit weak) predictor of stigma, with students in later years reporting slightly higher levels after controlling for other variables. The Azerbaijani ATMHPS is reliable, valid, and culturally appropriate for research and stigma-reduction efforts.

articles.title: Comparing Risk Perception in Transport and Non-Transport Contexts
articles.authors: Amotz Perlman
articles.abstract:
This article examines whether risk perception is a general trait that spans across contexts or whether it is domain-specific, with a focus on the transportation environment. To this end, the study investigates the relationships between risk perception in car driving and risk perception in other transportation situations (primarily road crossing and motorcycle riding), as well as in non-transportation contexts (such as work, home, investments, nature, and COVID-19).
In a series of seven studies based on attitude questionnaires toward risk situations in convenience samples, a consistently strong positive association was found between risk perception in driving and risk perception in other transportation contexts—particularly road crossing and motorcycle riding. Regression analyses indicated that risk perception in driving is the primary predictor of risk perception as a pedestrian or as a motorcyclist. In contrast, most studies did not find significant associations between transportation-related risk perception and risk perception in other domains, except for partial and limited associations observed in the first study.
These findings suggest that within the road environment there exists a relatively general perception of risk that is shared across different types of road users, and imply that drivers are capable of understanding the risks to which pedestrians and motorcyclists are exposed. In contrast, risk perception in non-transport contexts appears to be context-dependent and distinct. The discussion proposes a possible explanation based on exemplar theory, according to which road-related risk experiences are stored as a shared pool in memory, whereas in other domains representations remain separate.
In addition, the study has several limitations, including the use of small and non-representative samples, reliance on self-report measures, and a correlational design that does not allow for causal inference. Nevertheless, the findings contribute to the theoretical understanding of the structure of risk perception and highlight practical potential for developing educational interventions to improve road safety.

articles.title: Disentangling experience from reportability: A massive-report paradigm for visual experience outside goal-oriented attention
articles.authors: Zhang Chuyin; Naotsugu Tsuchiya
articles.abstract: When we look at something, we might feel we see a richly detailed external world, but if being asked, our reports are often limited. This discrepancy is one of the central questions in cognitive science. Despite its fundamental importance, philosophers and psychologists still do not agree on whether our “experience” is rich or sparse, depending on their stance on consciousness and associated psychological processes. In this Registered Report, we propose to disentangle these psychological processes from consciousness to better understand if experience is rich or sparse. To solve previously identified methodological concerns, our paradigm effectively combines partial-report, pre-/post-cues, and a massive number of natural stimuli that were previously annotated by many participants in a separate free-report paradigm. Our results will support either rich or sparse hypotheses on consciousness with minimal confounds by other psychological processes.

articles.title: The view-tolerance of human identity recognition depends on horizontal face information.
articles.authors: Alexia Roux-Sibilon; Hélène Dumont; Vincent Bremhorst; Christianne Jacobs; Valerie Goffaux
articles.abstract: This study investigates which visual information enables humans to recognize facial identity across different viewpoints, a key unresolved question in vision science. Participants completed an identity recognition task using faces rotated across a range of yaw angles and filtered to retain specific orientation ranges of visual information. Regardless of viewpoint, human performance consistently relied on horizontal facial information. To understand why, we used model observers to assess the identity information physically available in the images. A view-selective model, which matched identities within the same viewpoint, indicated that diagnostic identity cues shift from predominantly horizontal in frontal views to more vertical in profile views. In contrast, a view-tolerant model, which matched identities across different viewpoints, revealed that horizontal information provides the most stable and reliable identity cues across views. Furthermore, horizontal facial information best predicted the average appearance of a face across viewpoints, supporting its role in forming stable identity representations. These findings suggest that view-tolerant face representations are acquired through exposure to the stable statistical properties of faces primarily conveyed by horizontal information. By specifying the spatial information underlying recognition across viewpoints, the study offers valuable empirical constraints for the development of theoretical and computational models of face recognition.

articles.title: Could Brain-Computer Interfaces Advance Our Understanding of the Nature of Consciousness?
articles.authors: Kseniya P. Solovyeva; Vladislav Beliaev; Andrey Grunin; Mikhail Lebedev
articles.abstract: Recent developments in theoretical frameworks and experimental approaches have improved our understanding of the problems that need to be solved for us to advance in the understanding of the neural mechanisms underlying consciousness. Yet, there is a long way ahead as evident from the recent Cogitate adversarial collaboration that attempted to directly contrast Integrated Information Theory (IIT) and Global Neuronal Workspace Theory (GNWT)  and concluded that both theories received only partial support. Here we propose that brain-computer interfaces (BCIs), whose effectiveness for consciousness research remains underappreciated, could become a valuable methodological tool for moving beyond this impasse. Indeed, BCIs combine recording and stimulation methods and enable monitoring and influencing both conscious and unconscious brain mechanisms. As such, they offer an integrated approach for testing theories of consciousness (e.g. IIT versus GNWT) and even tackling philosophical conundrums, such as the hard problem of consciousness. Clinically, BCIs are already aiding patients with disorders of consciousness. Overall, BCIs hold the potential to bridge empirical investigation and intervention, making them a valuable tool in addressing the open questions regarding the nature of consciousness.

articles.title: Childbearing Motivation: An Incentive Motivation from An Evolutionary and Biopsychological Perspective
articles.authors: Ming Li
articles.abstract: Childbearing motivation—a fundamental driver of reproductive behavior—has predominantly been investigated through sociocultural or demographic lens, largely overlooking the integration of its underlying psychological and neurobiological mechanisms. This paper proposes a comprehensive bio-psycho-social theoretical framework that conceptualizes childbearing motivation as a specialized, evolutionarily-conserved form of incentive motivation, mechanistically dissociating “wanting” (desire) from “liking” (affective experience). Childbearing motivation is posited to result from the activation of the innate “parental care motivational system” and operates at multiple levels. At the primitive, unconscious biobehavioral level, the motivation is subserved by conserved the Parental Brain Network and regulated by peripartum steroids, neuropeptides and monoamines. At the conscious, psychological level, it is modulated by individuals’ emotional and cognitive factors; and externally by socio-cultural influences. Accordingly, childbearing should be measured using tools to assess its “wanting” and “liking” components at both conscious and unconscious levels. The model generates testable predictions concerning environmental factors, psychosocial moderators, hormonal influences and neural correlates of childbearing motivation. It offers a roadmap for empirical validation through behavioral priming, neuroendocrine manipulation, and longitudinal tracking. This integrative perspective provides a mechanistic account of why (evolution), how (neural circuits and psychological mechanisms) and when (socio-cultural) individuals are motivated to reproduce in contemporary contexts.

articles.title: Koshering Psychedelics: Ayahuasca in the Ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) Jewish World
articles.authors: Jonathan David; Aviva Berkovich-Ohana; ‪Yair Dor-Ziderman‬‏; Michael Fine; Hagit Bonny-Noach; Leor Roseman
articles.abstract: Background: Despite the rapid growth of psychedelic research, limited attention has been given to how psychedelics are used, experienced and integrated within highly conservative religious communities, and in Jewish contexts more broadly. This study addresses this gap by examining ayahuasca use among the ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) community, a highly religious, conservative, and collectivist Jewish group. Methods: This study used inductive thematic analysis of interviews with Haredi participants in Israel and the US (N = 23; age range: 21–60; 8 women), supplemented by participant observation and analysis of religious materials. Results: Six themes were identified: (a) settings: Haredi ayahuasca ceremonies were adapted to Jewish contexts; (b) motivations: use was primarily therapeutic; (c) acute ayahuasca experiences: experiences included distinct Jewish and Jewish mystical visionary content; (d) longer term religious effects: ayahuasca was linked to strengthened belief, connection to Judaism, and changes in religious practice; (e) religious tensions: included ayahuasca’s perceived foreignness to Haredi life, concerns about idolatry, mixed gender participation, and competing structures of authority (experience vs. text); and (f) strategies for addressing tensions: included medicalization, “koshering” ayahuasca’s set, setting, and experience (i.e., making them religiously permissible), and framing ceremonies as liminal spaces. Conclusions: This study broadens the scope of psychedelic science by examining ayahuasca use in a highly religious Orthodox context rarely included in the field. The findings show how ayahuasca settings, experiences, and long term effects are translated through Haredi and Jewish structures, involve religious tensions and resolutions, and highlight psychedelics’ contextual flexibility and continued diffusion into understudied populations.

articles.title: Advancing Idiographic Methodology for Data-Driven Treatment Personalization: Comparing ARIMAX, Network, and Tree-Based Approaches
articles.authors: William Li; Cristobal Eduardo Hernandez Contreras; Joseph Ciarrochi; Madeleine I Fraser; Steven C. Hayes; Clarissa W. Ong; Baljinder K. Sahdra
articles.abstract: Idiographic approaches offer a promising path to personalized psychological interventions, though guidance on selecting optimal methods remains limited. Researchers have applied various time series, network, and tree-based methods to identify individual-level process-outcome relationships, yet their comparative effectiveness is under-evaluated. We examined six advanced methods: two ARIMAX-based time series models (bivariate and multivariate i-ARIMAX), two network approaches (GIMME, indSEM), and two tree-based algorithms (Boruta and our novel Time Series Boruta, or tsBoruta). Simulations tested these methods across diverse longitudinal data conditions, varying in linear/nonlinear effects, autoregressive patterns, trends, interactions, predictor counts, and sample sizes. We evaluated performance using sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1 scores. For linear effects, i-ARIMAX, iBoruta, and tsBoruta achieved the highest F1 scores and specificity. Tree-based approaches performed best at detecting nonlinear effects and interactions. Methods that failed to account for time-series dependencies showed significant performance drops as these factors increased. In an empirical application, i-ARIMAX and tsBoruta replicated the group-level loneliness–depressed mood link while revealing substantial individual-level heterogeneity and distinct predictor patterns, informing potential personalized targets. Combining i-ARIMAX and tsBoruta offers a practical workflow that balances sensitivity and specificity, captures both linear and nonlinear processes, and supports data-driven, idiographic psychological treatment planning.

articles.title: AI and Agency: Benefiting Users by Understanding and Serving Explicit Long-Term Aspirations
articles.authors: Ravi Iyer; Rachel Xu; Emily Xing; Yuning Liu; Beth Goldberg; Nathanael Fast
articles.abstract: This paper introduces a framework for understanding how generative AI affects user agency, drawing on psychological research distinguishing between expressed long-term preferences and immediate, often-regretted, desires. We review research on how AI can increase users’ self-reported capacity for action and the benefits of providing users with a sense of control. Two studies with a nationally representative sample of American generative AI users examine a) users’ aspirational preferences for AI usage and b) where they perceive desired capability (outcome control) gains from AI (Study 1), along with c) users’ perceived control over AI experiences (process control) and d) the relationship between perceived control over AI experiences and reported capability gains (Study 2). Results from Study 1 indicate that users primarily value and report benefit from AI in instrumental domains (e.g. task completion, information gathering, and learning). Benefits in social and affective domains were found to be less common and less salient for users. In Study 2, users who felt more empowered to shape LLM outputs perceived greater control over their AI experiences, and also reported greater capability gains. We argue that centering user agency, encompassing control over product experience and product outcomes, should be a primary design principle.

articles.title: Towards a More Comprehensive Understanding of Parental Mediation in Adolescents’ Gaming: A Circumplex Approach to Assess Parents’ Need-Supportive Communication
articles.authors: Lowie Bradt; Maarten Vansteenkiste; Bieke Zaman; Rozane De Cock; Bart Soenens
articles.abstract: Parental mediation of adolescent gaming has received growing scholarly attention, yet research focusing solely on restrictive and active mediation strategies has yielded inconsistent effects. Drawing on Self-Determination Theory and the circumplex model of motivating and demotivating communication (Aelterman et al., 2019), this study introduces a fine-grained, situation-based instrument assessing how parents communicate about gaming. This new measure captures the full range of need-supportive and need-thwarting communication, from autonomy-support and structure through control and chaos, across ten gaming-related scenarios. Both parents and adolescents (14-18-year-olds, daily gamers; N = 300 dyads) completed parallel versions of the questionnaire in the fall of 2022 and 2023, alongside established parenting and adolescent measures on gaming, school adjustment, and mental health. The measure was proven to be psychometrically valid; our findings suggest that how parents communicate about gaming is associated with adolescents’ adjustment. The measure provides added value on top of already existing measures on parental mediation.

articles.title: Aha-Related Surprise Tracks  Violated Expectations at Multiple Levels  in Perceptual Insight
articles.authors: Paola Gega; Teuta Dzaferi; Maxi Becker
articles.abstract: Insight is often accompanied by an Aha experience, but why some solutions feel especially insightful remains unclear. The insight-as-prediction-error (IPE) account proposes that this experience arises when a solution violates expectations formed before recognition. Here, we tested whether this account generalizes to perceptual insight and whether Aha-related surprise reflects violated expectations at multiple levels. Across two online experiments (Experiment 1: N = 50; Experiment 2: N = 43), participants briefly previewed Mooney images, rated expected solvability, attempted to identify the hidden object, and then rated the Aha dimensions surprise, suddenness, pleasure, and certainty. In both experiments, meta-cognitive prediction error (PE) about solvability reliably predicted greater surprise on correctly solved trials. Longer preview durations increased expected solvability (from 250 to 650 ms), suggesting that these expectations form rapidly from minimal perceptual input but are not simply accurate forecasts of later identification success. Critically, content-based PE—defined as mismatch between anticipated and actual solution content—explained additional variance in surprise beyond meta-cognitive PE and showed clearer selectivity for surprise than the other Aha dimensions. Together, these findings extend the IPE account to perceptual insight and support a multi-level account in which insight-related surprise is associated with trial-wise violations of expectations about both whether a solution will occur and what that solution will be.

articles.title: Supporting Self-Regulated Learning through Performance Profile Feedback: An Experimental and Survey Study in Online Psychology Education
articles.authors: Felix Adams; Robert Gaschler; Björn Fisseler; E. Helin Yaban
articles.abstract:
Feedback is a key mechanism for supporting the high self-regulatory demands of distance-learning students. While multifaceted feedback is widely assumed to enhance learning, the effects of feedback granularity in graphical performance profiles on feedback use and learning outcomes remain insufficiently differentiated. Across two studies, we investigated how varying levels of detail in such profiles influence students' perceptions of feedback and their academic performance. In an experiment (Study I; N = 91), undergraduate psychology students received either a basic or a detailed performance profile after a mock exam. Detailed profiles significantly increased perceived usefulness, but no additional performance gains were observed on subsequent exams compared with basic profiles. Study II (N = 192) examined learners' feedback preferences across dimension- and temporal-resolution visualizations using a survey design. Most students preferred detailed profiles that visualized performance trajectories across multiple mock exams. Results suggest that detail enhances perceived usefulness, whereas performance differences are more closely related to learners' voluntary engagement with granular feedback formats. These results point to the need for adaptive, learner-centered feedback designs in higher education learning analytics.

Keywords: performance feedback, feedback granularity, self-regulated learning, learning analytics, distance higher education

articles.title: Critical Mass Theory: An Integrative Conceptual Review
articles.authors: Traci Sitzmann; Rong Su; Zhengyan Kang
articles.abstract: While the axiom that diversity is a competitive advantage has become commonplace, research has not provided a definitive answer regarding how much diversity is needed to attain this advantage. Critical mass theory proposes that a qualitative shift in group dynamics occurs when minority group representation reaches a threshold, strengthening members’ influence on work processes and outcomes. We conducted an integrative conceptual review of 277 studies to evaluate and advance critical mass theory. Nearly a third of quantitative studies do not meet the methodological requirements for testing critical mass theory, including failing to model nonlinear relationships and lacking sufficient representation to detect a threshold. Among studies meeting methodological requirements, 83% support critical mass threshold effects, with a mean threshold of 29% (SD = 6.67%). Across methodologically sound studies, attaining a diversity critical mass is associated with improved interpersonal dynamics with downstream effects on employee satisfaction, the success of diversity and corporate social responsibility initiatives, misconduct, financial risk, innovation, organizational reputation, and performance. Critical mass effects are stronger and the threshold is lower when underrepresented members hold leadership roles, organizations operate in more equitable contexts, organizational and societal values align with those of the minority group, and organizations have stronger prior performance. Political divides and other within-group differences can hinder coordination among minority group members, weakening critical mass effects. Our review clarifies when and why a diversity critical mass contributes to competitive advantage and provides guidance for future research and for organizations seeking to leverage critical mass effects.

articles.title: Investigating Language and Cognition  in Virtual Reality
articles.authors: Jonas Nölle; Alex Titus; Chen Zhou; David Peeters
articles.abstract: The experimental study of language and cognition has traditionally relied on laboratory paradigms that isolate linguistic processing from its physical and social context. While these methods offer high experimental precision, they often fail to capture the multimodal, situated, and interactive nature of real-world communication. Here we argue that Virtual Reality (VR) offers a transformative solution to this historical trade-off by combining the rigorous control of the laboratory with the ecological validity of immersive environments. We review how VR enables researchers to investigate language not as an isolated cognitive faculty, but as a dynamic activity grounded in physical space and social interaction. Specifically, we examine the affordances of VR for studying multimodal signaling (e.g., speech, gaze, gesture), environmental context, and social presence. Beyond these theoretical opportunities, we provide a pragmatic guide to the methodological challenges inherent in VR research, addressing critical issues such as simulator sickness, the "uncanny valley," embodiment illusions, and the technical demands of 3-D experimental design. We conclude by outlining future directions, including the integration of VR with neurophysiological measures, computational modelling, and AI-driven virtual agents, aiming toward an empirically grounded science of language that bridges the gap between abstract cognitive models and the complexity of everyday communicative life.

articles.title: Brazilian L2 English Accentedness Ratings by LLMs: Systematic Bias and the Limits of Automated Perception
articles.authors: Felipe Flores Kupske; Laura Zorzi
articles.abstract: This study investigates whether audio-capable large language models (LLMs) categorize L2 English accentedness in ways similar to human raters. Evaluations produced by Google Gemini Flash and Gemini Pro are compared against those of native American English raters, using recordings of Brazilian speakers from the CSLU: Foreign Accented English Corpus (Lander, 2007). Analysis revealed systematic divergences: both LLMs consistently overestimated accentedness, assigning strong accent ratings at proportions absent from the human baseline. Gemini Pro exhibited the greatest bias (OR = 8.25), contradicting the assumption that greater computational sophistication yields judgments more aligned with human perception. These findings are consistent with the hypothesis that the models examined lack the socio-phonetic tolerance that characterizes human accent assessment and suggest that they should not be regarded as reliable substitutes for human raters in L2 accentedness evaluation tasks.

articles.title: Criação de mapas com R (tmap)
articles.authors: Felipe Flores Kupske
articles.abstract: Este guia é uma introdução prática à criação de mapas no R com o pacote tmap, desenvolvida como material de apoio para estudantes e pesquisadores das áreas de linguística, sociolinguística, sociofonética e áreas afins que desejam representar dados em mapas geográficos. O guia cobre: (1) instalação e carregamento dos pacotes necessários, com notas sobre problemas comuns de instalação; (2) criação de mapas   nativos do tmap, incluindo mapas estáticos, interativos, com múltiplas camadas e facetas; (3) criação de mapas do Brasil e de estados a partir de arquivos shapefile(.shp) do IBGE; (4) integração de dados externos ao shape file para visualização de variáveis quantitativas em mapas; e (5) exportação de mapas em diferentes formatos. O pacote sf (Simple Features) é adotado como substituto do rgdal, descontinuado em dezembro de 2023. Parto do pressuposto que o leitor tenha familiaridade básica como R e o RStudio, como instalar pacotes, abrir arquivos e executar funções simples.

articles.title: Unpacking Conceptions of Being American in Relation to Identity and Mental Health Among Emerging Adults of Color
articles.authors: Dulce Wilkinson Westberg; Michelle Pasco; Bethany Cruz; Shima Golmohamadi; Alan Meca
articles.abstract: This study sought to examine how U.S. young adults of color understand American identity in the context of heightened sociopolitical tensions and how these understandings relate to measures of cultural identity and mental health. We used qualitative methods to identify themes in open-ended responses about American identity from 450 young adults of color during the first administration of Donald Trump. Quantitative methods were then used to examine themes in relation to cultural identity and mental health outcomes. Extending past work, we identified themes characterizing what it means to be American, ways youth felt American, and ways youth felt not American. Themes related with measures of cultural identity and mental health and varied based on race/ethnicity, gender, and nativity, revealing diversity in how American identity is experienced. Findings underscore the complexity of national identity among young adults of color who are navigating what it means to be American amid rising sociopolitical conflict.

articles.title: Systematic review of genetic associations between irritability and mental health disorders
articles.authors: Nhu Huynh; Christopher Kendall; Evelyn Ayaji; Nicole Roslin; Roxy O'Rourke; Christie Burton; Jacob Vorstman; Jennifer Crosbie
articles.abstract: Irritability is a transdiagnostic trait associated with a wide range of psychiatric disorders, but the extent to which this co-occurrence is related to genetic factors is unclear. This systematic review aims to provide an overview of studies examining the genetic associations between irritability-related traits and diverse mental health disorders and traits. Studies published up to May 2025 were retrieved from MEDLINE, EMBASE, PsycINFO, Web of Science, and the reference lists of included studies. Studies of human participants reporting quantitative genetic associations using twin or genome-wide genetic analyses (i.e., genome-wide association study [GWAS] and polygenic risk score [PRS] studies) were screened for inclusion based on pre-determined criteria. A total of 30 studies met the inclusion criteria, including six twin and 24 genome-wide genetic analyses. The results indicated shared genetic liability (e.g., positive genetic correlation) between irritability-related traits and specific mental health outcomes, with the most replicated evidence observed for internalizing (i.e., depression; rg=0.46-0.56) and externalizing (i.e., ADHD; rg=0.29-1.00) domains. Emerging evidence for other domains, such as neurodevelopmental disorders, psychotic disorders, and substance use disorders, remained inconclusive due to the limited number of studies on these disorders. Research using standardized measurement of the irritability phenotype and more ancestrally-diverse samples is needed to better understand the genetic architecture of irritability and its transdiagnostic role in mental health disorders.

articles.title: Faith and the Other. Revisiting the Transcendence of the Other in the Psychology of Religion
articles.authors: Heinz Streib
articles.abstract: This article is an exercise in revisiting the concept of ‘faith’ in the psychology of religion. It addresses questions such as: What is faith? Is faith different from religion? In what ways? What does it mean defining faith as “ultimate concern”—as some colleagues already do? Answers are inspired by the philosophies of Paul Tillich and Emmanuel Levinas. An examination of Tillich’s philosophy of religion reveals that “ultimate concern” is a highly critical concept that, exactly by pointing to the Ultimate, entails a specific, radically semiotic understanding of transcendence. From reading Levinas, we conclude that ethics is grounded in the primacy of the Other, the transcendence one encounters in the face of the Other, and in the diachrony of the Good. Taken together, the insights from Tillich and Levinas suggest conceptualizing ‘faith’ as “ultimate concern” by which we are “grasped and turned to the infinite” (Tillich) and respond to the experience of transcendence that speaks to us in the face of the Other (Levinas). This confronts psychology with the challenge of operationalizing such experiences of transcendence of the Other. While the model of faith styles, which is based on Fowler’s theory of faith development, can be regarded as a beginning, the article concludes with the suggestion of a research program that features xenosophia.

articles.title: Efficacy of Imitation Training for Individuals with Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Systematic Review
articles.authors: Hooshang Dadgar; Zahra Moshtaghi Fard; Yang Zhang
articles.abstract: This review examined imitation training for children with autism. Based on 38 articles—including 27 single-subject designs and 6 randomized controlled trials (RCTs)—findings suggest imitation-based approaches, especially reciprocal imitation training (RIT), may be a helpful first option. These approaches can lead to moderate-to-strong gains in imitation skills, potentially supporting growth in other social/developmental areas. Effectiveness may depend on the child's age, delivery format, imitation type, and existing skills. Structured methods might bring quick progress, while naturalistic, socially responsive approaches could lead to more lasting and transferable skills. New methods like telehealth and robot-assisted training appear promising but need more study. Overall, imitation training seems to work best when started early, tailored to the child, and done with care.

articles.title: When I say... clinically significant change
articles.authors: Christoph Lindner; Steffen Zitzmann
articles.abstract: Clinically significant change is a central but ambiguous concept in clinical practice and medical education. This brief “When I say...” manuscript clarifies three practical meanings of the term: recovery, response, and interpretation using additional reference points such as patient experience, clinical judgement, functioning, and follow-up course. The article aims to help learners and clinicians distinguish detectable change from change that matters for patient care.

articles.title: How is AI impacting people?
articles.authors: Christopher Summerfield; Rebecca Anselmetti; Shahar Avin; Vanessa Cheung; Tom Eccles; Anna Gausen; Kobi Hackenburg; Keno; Hannah Rose Kirk; Lennart Luettgau
articles.abstract: AI systems are now used by a billion people worldwide. What is their impact on human users? Popular narratives of concern argue that AI may be undermining our cognitive abilities, polluting our information ecosystem, corroding human relationships, damaging our mental health, and rendering us vulnerable to persuasion, manipulation, emotional dependence, and social engineering. Does the empirical evidence support these claims? Emerging research has begun to study the impact that AI has on people, via model evaluations, user studies, analysis of consumer chatlogs, and agent-based simulations. Here, we review this research, to summarise what we know about how AI is impacting people.

articles.title: Age, Education, and Sex Differences  in Smartphone-based Cognitive Assessment in Daily Life
articles.authors: Antonio Terracciano; Martina Luchetti; Justin Brown; Hannah Lovett; STEPHAN; Angelina Sutin
articles.abstract: Sociodemographic differences in cognition are well-documented with traditional testing but less understood in daily life. This study used digital phenotyping via Ecological Momentary Assessment to examine sociodemographic differences in cognitive mean performance, intra-individual variability, and learning. A community sample of cognitively unimpaired adults (N=2,570; ages 40–93) completed smartphone-based trail making and visual memory tasks four times daily for eight days. Completion rates (>70%) and construct validity against traditional tests were high, but lower in older groups. Multilevel modeling indicated that older age was associated with lower mean performance and higher intra-individual variability. Higher education was associated with better and less variable cognitive performance. Notably, older and less-educated individuals had greater learning gains, suggesting significant latent plasticity. Sex differences were small and task-specific. Digital phenotyping in naturalistic settings demonstrates that older age and lower education are related to worse cognitive performance, greater variability, but sustained learning in daily life.

articles.title: Reassessing purported prospective links between mobile phone addiction, bedtime procrastination, and physical activity: A commentary on Xu and Li (2026)
articles.authors: Kimmo Sorjonen; Bo Melin; Marika Melin
articles.abstract: Xu and Li recently reported prospective associations between mobile phone addiction, bedtime procrastination, and physical activity based on analyses using cross-lagged panel models (CLPMs). However, estimates obtained from CLPMs are known to be vulnerable to statistical artifacts and may not necessarily reflect genuine prospective influences. We reanalyzed the reported correlational structure using alternative analytic specifications and obtained markedly different conclusions depending on model choice, including positive, negative, and null effects. When these discrepant estimates were meta-analytically synthesized, the overall effects were indistinguishable from zero. These findings suggest that the reported associations may not provide convincing evidence of prospective influence and that the conclusions drawn by Xu and Li may be premature. More generally, the present commentary highlights the importance of evaluating findings across multiple plausible analytic approaches and avoiding strong inferences based on a single model specification.

articles.title: The Link Between Diverse Words and Original Ideas Is Weakening  in the AI-Era College Admissions
articles.authors: Kibum Moon; Kostadin Kushlev; Andrew Bank; Benjamin Lira Luttges; Indre Viskontas; James C. Kaufman; Dan Richard Johnson; Angela Lee Duckworth; Adam Green
articles.abstract: Concerns that AI homogenizes human thinking appear at odds with findings that LLM writing is perceived as more creative than human writing. We propose that LLMs enhance surface-level diversity while simultaneously homogenizing underlying ideas. Four large-scale natural experiments tested this preregistered “semantic disjunction” hypothesis in the high-stakes context of college admissions. Comparing personal statements written by 372,793 applicants after-versus-before ChatGPT’s 2022 release, multi-level semantic analyses revealed increasing surface diversity of words simultaneous with decreasing conceptual diversity of sentences (within essays) and whole documents (between essays). Three controlled experiments provided causal evidence that AI use produced semantic disjunction, including in applicants from the natural experiment, and indicated an apparent root mechanism: a positive association between surface and conceptual diversity in humans was weakened or reversed in LLM writing. Despite conceptual homogenization, raters perceived post-ChatGPT essays as more creative. Semantic disjunction explained this apparent paradox: increasing word-level diversity explained increasing creativity judgments, overriding effects of decreased conceptual diversity, even when raters (human and AI) considered many essays together. Avoiding over-reliance on word-level surface diversity by considering multiple semantic levels is likely to support more complete understanding of AI’s effects on human thinking. Disjunctive trends were widespread, but were strongest in minoritized applicants.

articles.title: Making Room for Theory in DIF and Measurement Invariance Analysis with Covariate Effects on Groups of Items
articles.authors: Sanford R Student; Klint Kanopka
articles.abstract: We propose and study an extension of moderated nonlinear factor analysis that models covariate effects—that is, differential item functioning (DIF)—at the level of item groups rather than, or in addition to, individual items. This allows for a more theory-driven approach to DIF and measurement invariance testing in which researchers can form and test hypotheses about why items differ in how they function across an arbitrary number of categorical or continuous covariates. As a motivating example, we demonstrate gender effects on a developmental measure where scores that assume invariance overstate boys’ cognitive development and understate their fine and gross motor development, with opposite implications for girls. Simulation demonstrates the empirical identifiability and predictable statistical performance of the proposed model, and additional empirical examples demonstrate the approach’s substantial flexibility.

articles.title: Beliefs in and experiences of sorcery, black magic and brujería among psychedelic users: a quantitative and qualitative survey
articles.authors: Jules Evans; Christian Jurlando; David Luke; Maja Kohek; Marta Majer; kim van oorsouw
articles.abstract:
Sorcery, black magic, and supernatural harm are central to many Amazonian shamanic traditions, yet their role in contemporary Western psychedelic culture has received little systematic empirical attention. Ethnographic work suggests that Western participants in ayahuasca tourism often downplay indigenous sorcery frameworks in favour of psychological or therapeutic interpretations of difficult experiences. Yet, anecdotal reports indicate that some participants leave ceremonies convinced they have been subjected to shamanic attack or supernatural harm. No quantitative study has examined the prevalence of such beliefs and experiences, the conditions under which they arise, or their implications for participant welfare in ceremonial settings.
This study addresses that gap through a cross-sectional, retrospective, anonymous online survey of adults with personal, professional, or cultural involvement in psychedelics. Participants were recruited through psychedelic community networks and harm reduction organisations, including ICEERS, Chacruna, the Challenging Psychedelic Experiences Project, and the Fireside Project. The final analytic sample comprised 895 participants with at least one lifetime psychedelic experience.
We aim to estimate the prevalence of beliefs in supernatural harm among Western psychedelic users; examine whether psychedelic experiences and immersion in psychedelic culture are associated with shifts in such beliefs; characterise experiences interpreted as black magic or supernatural harm; assess whether facilitator authority and fear of magical retaliation inhibit criticism of ceremonial leaders; and compare these patterns between those who regularly participated in ayahuasca ceremonies and the rest of the sample. The findings aim to inform both research on psychedelic-induced belief change and harm reduction frameworks in ceremonial settings.

articles.title: Intention and foresight in deliberate actions: An ERP study
articles.authors: Tomáš Dominik; Emma Chen; Maverick Morales; Ilai Gavish; Melissa M. Brillhart-Gara; Gideon Yaffe; Uri Maoz
articles.abstract: The distinction between intention and foresight is critical for law and morality. For example, a firefighter who forces a hose through a parked car to reach a hydrant may foresee the damage without intending it—unless the car belongs to a rival, in which case the damage may be intended after all. This study aimed to disentangle the neural correlates of intention and foresight using a novel paradigm. Twenty-five participants completed a task inspired by the game Frogger, where hitting objects could be desirable (earning points) or inconsequential. Outcomes varied by likelihood (high vs. low) and actuality (hit vs. miss), resulting in a 2×2×2 design: intention (high/low) × outcome (hit/miss) × expectedness (expected/unexpected). Electroencephalography (EEG) revealed that feedback-related negativity (FRN) amplitude was larger for participants during high-intention than low-intention outcomes while the P3 potential was larger for unexpected than expected outcomes. However, neither signal exclusively reflected intention or foresight; the FRN also indexed expectedness, and the P3 reflected intention. To address this overlap, we used exploratory effect-matched spatial (EMS) filtering, which identified a widespread brain-activity pattern that better dissociated intention from foresight. These findings enhance our understanding of the neural processes underlying complex decision-making, with potential applications in ethical and legal frameworks.

articles.title: AI Consciousness Will Divide Society
articles.authors: Lucius Caviola
articles.abstract: As AI systems become increasingly advanced and human-like, societies will face a fundamental question: are these systems conscious, and if so, do they deserve moral or legal rights? This chapter argues that rather than converging on a shared answer, society is likely to enter a period of confusion, uncertainty, and deep disagreement. Drawing on recent empirical evidence, it shows that both experts and the public are already divided on the possibility of AI consciousness and the moral status of digital minds. It argues that this division is driven by structural features of the issue, including the difficulty of conceptualizing and measuring consciousness, conflicting economic, political, and emotional incentives, and a disconnect between AI systems’ internal architectures and their outwardly human-like behavior. Such disagreement creates risks of political polarization, regulatory instability, geopolitical tension, and an increased risk of ultimately misattributing moral status. It concludes by outlining strategies for improving expert coordination, fostering constructive public discourse, designing AI systems to reduce moral confusion, and reducing exclusive reliance on consciousness in moral and policy decisions.

articles.title: The influence of visual landmarks on spatiotopic localization and object-location binding
articles.authors: Zitong Lu; Julie Golomb
articles.abstract: Despite receiving visual inputs based on retinotopic (gaze-centered) coordinates, we are able to perceive the spatiotopic (world-centered) locations of objects. Intriguingly, some studies have reported strong evidence that object-location binding occurs in retinotopic coordinates, but other recent studies have explored whether more naturalistic contexts and factors can trigger spatiotopic object-location binding. Here, we examined the influence of visual landmarks. While previous work has supported a benefit of landmarks on spatial localization, it remains unclear whether this benefit would also extend to object-location binding across saccades. In separate blocks, participants completed two tasks: a spatiotopic localization task and a location-irrelevant object identity judgment task known to elicit a spatial congruency bias (SCB, a measure of object-location binding); we examined performance on both tasks with and without visual landmarks. As expected, in the localization task, landmarks consistently improved spatiotopic location memory precision. However, in the object identity judgement task, landmarks alone were not sufficient to induce spatiotopic object-location binding. In Experiment 1, when participants performed a single saccade during the delay between two stimuli (static context), the SCB was purely retinotopic and not different between landmark conditions (present vs. absent). However, in a more dynamic context (Experiment 2) when participants performed additional eye movements during stimulus presentation, the presence of landmarks led to a significant spatiotopic SCB. In summary, landmarks alone aren’t enough to trigger spatiotopic object-location binding, but they can help in a more dynamic context. These results provide new insight into visual stability across eye movements in complex, dynamic environments.

articles.title: Neuroscience-informed Psychoeducation (NIPE): How Brain Awareness Supports Brain Recovery
articles.authors: Tara Rezapour; Jamie Berry; C. Munro Cullum; Behzad Elahi; Islam Elbanna; Medhat Elsabbahy; Mehdi Farokhnia; Nabila Haque; Jing Jin; Mario Juruena
articles.abstract: Over the past two decades, Neuroscience-Informed Psychoeducation (NIPE) has gradually evolved and become integrated into various therapy modules for psychiatric and neurological disorders. Although NIPE shares certain features with conventional psychoeducation, it is distinguished by key content and structure-related elements. Its content centers on brain-based explanations of the mechanisms and symptoms underlying psychiatric and neurological disorders, as well as the neural processes associated with treatment response, remission, and recovery. NIPE promotes self-awareness and insight and may contributes to motivation for treatment and treatment adherence. It also informs patients, caregivers, and communities how to play an active role in the prevention, treatment, and recovery process based on this mechanistic understanding. Structurally, NIPE is designed to align with how the brain learns and adapts, improving the learning experience to promote more enduring outcomes. By clarifying the processes and goals of brain recovery, increasing self-awareness and self-efficacy, and supporting adherence to prescribed treatments, NIPE may enhance motivation and retention, and facilitate recovery while reducing shame and stigma. This paper provides an introductory overview of NIPE including its definitions and terminology, the current evidence base, clinical implications, and directions for future development.

articles.title: The Open Science Movement and Clinical Psychology Training: Rigorous Science is Transparent Science
articles.authors: Donald R Lynam; David Sbarra; Jennifer L Tackett; P. Priscilla Lui; Riley McDanal; Katherine Schaumberg; Aidan G.C. Wright; Yexinyu Yang; Joshua Miller
articles.abstract: Clinical psychology, like other disciplines, is facing a replication/credibility crisis that undermines the evidentiary basis of our science. The Open Science Movement (OSM) offers solutions through practices such as preregistration, Registered Reports, and sharing of data, materials, and code. Clinical psychology has been slow to adopt these reforms, leaving trainees underprepared for emerging norms. This article reviews the factors that contributed to the crisis, outlines the necessary role of transparency in distinguishing rigorous from flawed research, and documents the limited uptake of open science practices in clinical journals and graduate training. We argue that transparency is an ethical as well as methodological imperative and propose a competency-based model for embedding open science principles into doctoral education. We conclude by calling on programs, journals, and accrediting bodies to make transparency a core requirement, essential for restoring credibility and advancing a cumulative, trustworthy clinical science.

articles.title: Cognitive load disrupts different stages of prediction in second-language speakers during a visual world task
articles.authors: Naomi Nota; Ruth Corps; Lauren Hadley; Graham Naylor; Martin John Pickering
articles.abstract: There is evidence that language prediction involves two stages: an automatic (associative) stage and a non-automatic (strategic) predictive stage. Prior work with L1 speakers shows that cognitive load disrupts the distinction between both stages and selectively delays associative prediction, leaving strategic prediction unaffected. Notably, L2 speakers show delayed strategic prediction compared to L1 without external cognitive load, raising questions about whether L2 processing imposes load-like demands. We conducted a preregistered visual world eye-tracking experiment with L2 English speakers, manipulating load via a verbal working memory task. Under no load, L2 speakers showed two prediction stages, with delayed strategic prediction relative to L1. Under load, the distinction between stages disappeared and associative prediction was delayed. These findings support a distinct-constraints account: associative and strategic prediction differ in their dependence on cognitive resources, and delayed strategic prediction in L2 speakers may reflect reduced processing capacity rather than a qualitatively different predictive mechanism.

articles.title: The Perils of Partialing Optimism: Relationships with Personality and Health
articles.authors: Suzanne Carrie Segerstrom; Donald R Lynam
articles.abstract: The question of whether optimistic or pessimistic items in the Life Orientation Test-Revised better predict psychological and physical outcomes is often addressed by entering both subscales in the same model. This approach risks the “perils of partialing” in which partial constructs have lower reliability and different nomological nets than their unpartialed counterparts. The present studies tested the effects of partialing optimism and pessimism using structural equation modeling to test differences in external validity and intraclass correlations (ICC) to test similarity of profiles. In Study 1 (N = 2,281 older adults), the optimism-pessimism correlation was modest (r = -0.32), but nomological nets for unpartialed and partialed constructs with personality and health were significantly different (all p < .00001). In Study 2 (N = 739 younger and midlife adults), the optimism-pessimism correlation was higher (r = -0.61). Nomological nets were significantly different (all p < .00001). Examination of item correlations indicated that partialed constructs may have lost convergent validity with key theoretical elements of trait optimism. Differences between optimism and pessimism were fewer than those between each and its partialed counterpart, and results with partialed optimism and pessimism constructs should be viewed with caution.

articles.title: How Adults in Spain Perceive Love:  Individual Differences in Cultural Attunement and Evaluative Style
articles.authors: Saida Heshmati; Lindy Williams; Dana Hussein; Jaymes Paolo Rombaoa; Royce Anders; Zita Oravecz
articles.abstract: Love is culturally universal in presence, yet highly variable in how people perceive it. The present study examined individual differences in cultural attunement and judgment styles related to love. Adults (N = 502) living in Spain rated 61 everyday scenarios according to how likely each would make most people feel loved.
