articles.abstract: Evolution by natural selection produces the appearance of design in minds, brains, and behavior. While rational models of cognition are usually considered to be consistent with evolution, they are rarely derived from distinctively evolutionary assumptions. Bayesian modeling, reinforcement learning, rational analysis, and resource rational analysis all postulate optimization objectives of coherence in information use or maximization of expected value, potentially under resource constraints. However, cognition is performed by an individual within a lineage where success is determined by the persistence of the lineage across generations. Here, we present a signal detection model that shows how this distinctly evolutionary objective produces predictions that depart from those of rational models. We find that the source of environmental uncertainty determines cognitive design, with weak priors being favored in environments dominated by change. This ensures offspring vary so that some profit against novel challenges, and is favored even at the expense of accurate decision making. Cognitive structure should reflect stable environmental trends, but in fluctuating environments selection strips structure away to release behavioral diversity. We highlight that evolution maximizes the geometric mean number of offspring over generations, an objective that recurs across optimization problems where outcomes accumulate sequentially, including in finance, control engineering, and information theory.

articles.title: Norms of Disgust Expressions Across 10 Countries
articles.authors: Roza Gizem Kamiloğlu; Joshua M. Tybur
articles.abstract: Disgust is an emotion with three functionally distinct domains: pathogen disgust guards against disease, moral disgust condemns social and ethical violations, and sexual disgust deters unwanted sexual contact. In a preregistered study, 4,653 participants across 10 countries judged how frequently people in their community express each kind of disgust through the face, body, voice, and words, and how appropriate expressions are in private versus public and toward close versus distant others. Pathogen disgust was rated as expressed more than moral disgust through the face and body, and moral disgust more than pathogen disgust through words. Across domains, expressing disgust was judged more appropriate in private and toward close others. Pathogen disgust was judged the least appropriate to direct at another person. These patterns held in the same direction across the 10 countries. Results suggest culturally consistent expectations of disgust expressions, which might align with their functions.

articles.title: Does Alienation (or Negative Emotionality) Contaminate  the Results of Engineering Student Surveys?
articles.authors: Chris C. Martin
articles.abstract: Engineering educators often look for an association between some kind of treatment (e.g., microaggressions) and some outcome (e.g., belonging, satisfaction). However, some psychological researchers have questioned the validity of this method because negative emotionality can create spurious correlations between self-reported treatment and outcomes. Negative emotionality is the trait-like tendency to consider oneself maltreated and exploited. A person high in negative emotionality should report a high level of victimization (e.g., via microaggressions) and low level of satisfaction regardless of actual circumstances. Negative emotionality thereby acts as a third-variable confound. Our primary goal was to examine whether negative emotionally has this confounding effect. We measured perceived justice (treatment) and inclusion (outcome) in 38 student teams in a project-based engineering course (N = 227). Measures were administered across five time points to minimize measurement error and enable the modeling of lagged effects. A random-intercept cross-lagged panel model (RI-CLPM) was used for modeling. The results indicated that justice and inclusion had reciprocal effects on each other. Negative emotionality pulled down self-reported ratings of justice (p = .006) and inclusion (p = .085). However, the addition of negative emotionality did not change the overall results. Thus, researchers may therefore benefit from measuring this factor but its usage in a statistical model does not seem essential. Results pertaining to other personality traits will also be discussed as part of this presentation. The second goal of this presentation is to introduce engineering education researchers to the RI-CLPM model for handling multiple waves of longitudinal data from students in teams.

articles.title: Distracted and Misled Yet Capable of Transfer: Word Knowledge and Executive Functions Contribute to Analogical Transfer in 2.5- to 5.5-Year-Olds
articles.authors: Katarzyna Bobrowicz; Angélica Mendes; Diederick C. Niehorster; Cassandra Origer
articles.abstract: Analogical transfer, the ability to generalize a solution onto a seemingly different but functionally similar problem, has the potential to boost individual learning. Analogical transfer draws on the ability to disregard irrelevant information in favor of relevant information. Although early analogical transfer has been repeatedly investigated, early ability to prioritize truly relevant over irrelevant, distracting or misleading, information remains understudied. Therefore, here, we tested whether children between 2.5 and 5.5 years (N = 116; 63 boys/53 girls; 89.13% had at least one parent with a college or university degree; residing in Sweden or Luxembourg), could successfully execute such transfer. Children participated in a play session at day-care facilities, where they attempted to transfer relevant tool-use knowledge across two analogical problems despite a potentially distracting or a potentially misleading problem solved in between. Results showed that analogical transfer despite distracting or misleading information develops in early childhood, and that it may be available to children as young as 2.5 years. Furthermore, two core executive functions, set-shifting and working memory, are related to such transfers. Set-shifting positively contributed to transfers despite misleading information, and working memory contributed positively to transfers despite distracting information but negatively to transfers despite misleading information. Finally, word knowledge contributed negatively to transfers despite distracting information.

articles.title: Sharing the Same Path? The Role of Similarity and Shared Reality with Supervisors in PhD Candidates’ Work Engagement
articles.authors: Jeanne Boisselier; Eric Bonetto; Nicolas Coli
articles.abstract: Research on doctoral supervisory relationships has increasingly emphasized the importance of similarity between supervisors and PhD candidates. However, the literature remains unclear about what this similarity actually consists of and whether its different forms matter equally for doctoral experience. Addressing this gap, the present study examines the relationships between similarity with the supervisor, supervisory relationship quality, and PhD candidates’ work engagement. More specifically, it distinguishes between two forms of perceived similarity: deep-level similarity, referring to similarity in psychological characteristics, and shared reality, referring to a common perspective on doctoral work. We surveyed 225 PhD candidates from universities across France. The findings support the distinction between deep-level similarity and shared reality as two separate forms of similarity in doctoral supervision. Multiple regression analyses show that, although both forms predict supervisory relationship quality, only shared reality significantly predicts PhD candidates’ work engagement, and this association is not mediated by relationship quality. These results suggest that what matters for PhD candidates’ engagement is not only being similar to one’s supervisor, but also sharing a common understanding of the doctoral project. By demonstrating the distinctive role of shared reality, this study advances current models of supervisory similarity and offers a more precise account of the relational mechanisms that sustain doctoral supervision.

articles.title: From Blaming to Explaining: The Normative Definition of Crime as a Core Challenge for Criminal Psychology
articles.authors: Marie Joséphine Hamatschek; Stefanie Schmidt
articles.abstract: Crime is defined by being unacceptable; it is thus normative. We argue that this normativity makes many psychological crime theories circular and unfalsifiable. The first pitfall is that thinking about ‘crime’ means evaluating. Therefore, we need to distinguish between our roles as empirical scientists and as value-guided humans. A positivist research culture impedes this, ironically undermining value-free science. The second pitfall is more fatal yet: Crime is a term without psychological meaning. Accordingly, theorists tend to attribute crime to similarly normative concepts (normative contagion), so that they blame (e.g., as ‘antisocial’) rather than explain. These concepts feign coherence via uniform (un)desirability (normative clamp). We thus risk merely restating – in technical language – that crime is bad. Fallback concepts (e.g., aggression), being just as value-laden, are no remedy. Fortunately, we do have a discipline apt to study the normative without judging: psychology. After all, the good-bad distinction is a product of the human mind. The logical move is simple: Treat evaluation as the fact it is – psychologically. This lets us model, in a falsifiable manner, how legal norms causally shape individual action. In its psychological essence, crime is moral, law-relating action. To do it justice, theorists should: (1) spell out normative premises, (2) model internal normativity descriptively, and (3) include the law as a mental standard. To be clear, science cannot tell us why people do bad things. However, psychologists can hope to explain why humans violate their internalised laws. When explaining crime, normativity cannot be avoided, but harnessed.

articles.title: The Impact of Acuity Loss on Medical Data Visualisation: Assessing Gist, Verbatim, and Decision-Making
articles.authors: Wai Lam Leung; Oliver Runswick; Claire Louise Heard; Tim Rakow
articles.abstract: Data visualisations are widely used in medical contexts to support informed consent and shared decision-making, and may particularly benefit patients with low health literacy and numeracy. However, visual acuity loss, a common and increasingly prevalent impairment, may limit their effectiveness. This study examined whether individuals can still draw appropriate inferences from visualisations under degraded acuity. Sixty participants with normal or corrected vision completed a within-subject experiment with simulated acuity loss at four levels (no acuity loss, mild, moderate, and severe acuity loss). Participants viewed bar charts, pie charts, and icon arrays, with gist and verbatim understanding and decision quality assessed. Results showed that knowledge and decision quality declined as acuity decreased, with severe acuity loss reducing decision accuracy to near-chance levels for pie and bar charts. However, icon arrays consistently supported better gist and verbatim understanding, reduced decisional reliance on medical professionals, and improved decision quality across all acuity conditions.

articles.title: Faculty Evaluations of DEI Statements for Academic Hiring
articles.authors: Nathan Honeycutt
articles.abstract: Diversity, equity, and inclusion (“DEI”) statements have become a popular additional evaluative criterion for academic hiring and promotion, but little to no empirical research has been conducted to assess how university faculty evaluate these statements. The present set of experiments sought to fill this void. Across seven studies (ntotal=4,953), tenured/tenure-track university faculty rated DEI statements that did not discuss race/ethnicity and gender diversity actions or efforts (i.e., statements that lacked equalitarian content) significantly lower than those that did. Specifically, faculty perceived these “alternative” statements to be weaker DEI statements, and perceived the applicants who submitted these statements to be less competent, less hireable, and less likable. Furthermore, faculty were less likely to recommend–often by a large margin–that the applicants behind these “alternative” statements pass an initial screening and be advanced for further review. Evaluative penalties were most severe toward DEI statements focusing only on viewpoint diversity actions and efforts (Studies 1-4), but also manifested for statements focusing only on rural diversity (Study 6), or only on socioeconomic diversity (Study 7). Findings for disability diversity (Study 5) were mixed. These studies provide novel insight on how DEI statements are perceived and evaluated by university faculty.

articles.title: Retinotopic mapping of cortical plasticity in age-related macular degeneration
articles.authors: Antonin Duret; Jean-Noël Vallée; Anastasiya Lahutsina; Diana Rdeini; Matthieu Lepetit- Coiffé; Kiyoko Gocho; Denis Sheynikhovich; Stephen Ramanoel
articles.abstract:
Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) is a leading cause of central vision loss,
driven by progressive damage to the central retina, and results in profound changes in visual
perception and behavior. While this retinal damage is well characterized, its consequences on
cortical organization remain incompletely understood. This knowledge gap is a major
challenge, as it limits our ability to predict functional vision loss, track disease progression, or
evaluate the success of cortical-level rehabilitative therapies.To bridge this gap, retinotopic
mapping provides a powerful framework to directly link retinal lesions to their cortical
representation, serving as a primary tool to investigate how these central visual field deficits
impact the brain's organizational structure. However, its application to AMD is limited by
several methodological challenges, including fixation instability, restricted visual field
stimulation, biases in population receptive field estimates, and difficulties in accurately
localizing lesion projection zones. Furthermore, current approaches often rely on simplified
representations of retinal damage and insufficient integration of multimodal data. This review
synthesizes current knowledge on cortical changes in AMD and highlights key methodological
limitations. Addressing these challenges will require improving spatial precision through the
integration of precise retinal measurements, multimodal neuroimaging incorporating ocular
tracking and advanced computational modeling, in order to better characterize cortical
plasticity and develop reliable biomarkers for clinical applications.

articles.title: Mapping the neural dynamics of learning others' social preferences from decision timing
articles.authors: Sophie Bavard; Sebastian Gluth
articles.abstract: Inferring what others prefer from observing their behavior is a fundamental component of social cognition, shaping how we navigate everyday interactions. While research has shown that beyond decision outcome, humans also utilize decision speed as a reliable cue for social inference, the neural mechanisms underlying such inferences remain poorly understood. In this preregistered EEG and pupillometry study, we investigate how the brain extracts preference information from the temporal dynamics of others' decisions. Participants observed others making allocation decisions in a modified Dictator Game, seeing only how long each decision took and never which option was chosen, while we orthogonally manipulated decision speed and decision difficulty. EEG recordings revealed two distinct signatures: an early posterior positivity sensitive to the dictator's decision speed, consistent with temporal surprise, and a later fronto-central negativity sensitive to decision difficulty. Pupillometry converged with the fronto-central pattern: sensitivity to difficulty was present from the start, while sensitivity to speed emerged only after observers had learned to link speed with difficulty, pointing to a later belief-updating process. Together, these findings reveal that the brain integrates both temporal and decision-related information during social observation, going beyond simple duration tracking to support preference inference from others' decision speed.

articles.title: Newborns’ neural tuning to the native language is revealed by infant-directed speech
articles.authors: Martina Dvorakova; Josef Urbanec; Jan Kremlacek; Katerina Chladkova
articles.abstract:
Newborn infants process a native language differently than a distinct non-native language, showing behavioral preference for the former and language-specific neural activation. At the same time, young infants show preferences for infant-directed speech (IDS) over adult-directed speech (ADS) and evidence suggests this preference is mirrored at the neural level. There is a confound: most studies assessing young infants’ responses to native versus non-native languages employ IDS stimuli, leaving it unclear whether IDS enhances or diminishes language-specific processing. Here, we examined whether newborns (n=62, 1–6 days old) differentially track their native versus an unfamiliar language and whether this differs between IDS and ADS.
Newborns showed stronger neural tracking of native-language Czech than non-native language Russian speech in IDS, whereas this language-specific difference was reduced or absent in ADS. In particular, newborns tracked their native-language infant-directed speech most strongly in the delta band, reflecting an early sensitivity to slow temporal structure in native-language speech. 
These findings indicate that early neural processing is tuned to the rhythmic properties of the native language, especially when the language is presented in infant-directed speech. Sensitivity to slow temporal structure may scaffold early segmentation, attentional organization, and the emergence of more fine-grained linguistic representations. Together, the results show that newborns can discriminate native from non-native speech and that IDS enhances the neural encoding of native-language rhythms, providing a foundation for early language acquisition.

articles.title: Diverse Mechanisms of Adaptive Variability in Human Behavior
articles.authors: Janne Reynders; Tom Verguts; Senne Braem
articles.abstract: Adaptive variability in behavior is crucial when environments promote unpredictability, yet its underlying cognitive mechanisms remain poorly understood. Volatile and adversarial environments can elicit variability by abrupt rule switches and explicitly reinforcing variable choices, respectively; however, literature lacks paradigms, computational models, and data to investigate and compare how (human) agents address such challenges. We introduce a novel hide-and-seek paradigm and use computational modeling to disentangle five candidate cognitive mechanisms of variability: stochastic choice, fast reinforcement learning, action-frequency-based memory, sequence-frequency-based memory, and episodic memory. Across three experiments (N = 290), we show that participants flexibly increased learning rate in volatile environments, but stochasticity, action-frequency-based memory, and reliance on episodic memory in adversarial environments. For the first time, these findings uncover that adaptive variability is not a unitary phenomenon but instead emerges from the flexible recruitment of multiple cognitive mechanisms as environmental demands intensify.

articles.title: Enhancing Intellectual Humility about Political Topics: An Intervention Tournament Including Five Conceptual Replications
articles.authors: Larissa Knöchelmann; Adina Janßen; Julia Dörbaum; Daniel W. Heck; Christopher Cohrs
articles.abstract: Intellectual humility, the recognition of one’s intellectual limitations, is promising to make controversial political debates more constructive. However, it is unknown how to best enhance a person’s intellectual humility in experiments or interventions. Therefore, we studied how to promote intellectual humility by adapting and conceptually replicating five different approaches suggested in previous work: the explanatory depth approach, growth mindset intervention, a humility information text, listing unknowns, and self-distancing. Results of a pre-registered online intervention tournament (N1 = 1,503) showed that intellectual humility about political topics was enhanced via providing information about the benefits of intellectual humility in controversial political discussions. Study 2 replicated and extended this finding regarding expressed intellectual humility in written text with a sample quota-stratified for Germany’s population regarding age, gender, and education (N2 = 1,654). However, the intervention did not lead to higher engagement with diverse viewpoints and effects on general intellectual humility and pre-post differences in self-rated knowledge were inconsistent. We discuss how our studies provides a starting point for effectively increasing intellectual humility in experiments and in interventions.

articles.title: Explaining forgetting at different timescales requires a time-variant forgetting function
articles.authors: Maarten van der Velde; Florian Sense; Jelmer P Borst; Hedderik van Rijn
articles.abstract: Employing memory models in practical applications provides an opportunity to test and refine theories on a large scale. The better models work in real-world scenarios, the more they can benefit educational practice. Using recall data spanning timescales from minutes to weeks, we find that a single forgetting curve cannot simultaneously capture short- and long-term performance, and show that an accurate model therefore requires a forgetting function that changes with time.

articles.title: Listeners’ Expressions of Engagement Shape Speakers’ Emotions  Moment by Moment in Conversational Self-Disclosure
articles.authors: Santeri Lepistö; Pyry Heikkinen; Diana Tamir; Emmi Koskinen; Markus Söderman; Mariel Wuolio; Jan Lindström; Niklas Ravaja; Anssi Peräkylä; Ville Johannes Harjunen
articles.abstract: Conversation partners signal their engagement in multiple ways, including eye gaze, nodding, and facial expressions. While disruptions to this engagement—whether due to listeners’ divided attention or constraints of the communication medium—are associated with changes in speakers’ stress levels, it remains unclear how specific expressions of listener (dis)engagement drive fluctuations in speakers’ emotional states on a moment-to-moment basis. Here, we analyzed naturalistic conversations in face-to-face and video-mediated settings to test how changes in listeners’ attentional engagement translate into observable engagement behaviors, and how these behaviors, in turn, influence speakers’ emotions. A sample of 160 participants (data collected in 2023–2024) engaged in a dyadic emotional self-disclosure task either face-to-face or via video call, with listeners interacting either with full attention or in the presence of an attentional distractor. During the interactions, we tracked listeners’ engagement behaviors (nodding, gaze, smiling) and changes in speakers’ emotional state (autonomic arousal). The results showed that attentionally engaged listeners displayed more engagement behaviors, which in turn regulated speakers’ emotional responses across different timescales. At the level of the whole encounter, listeners’ active nodding during attentional disengagement reduced speakers’ autonomic arousal. At the moment-to-moment level, listeners’ nodding, gaze diversion, and smiling all transiently increased speakers’ arousal. The effect of gaze diversion was weaker in video-call interactions, whereas the effect of smiling was stronger under full attention than under attentional distraction. These findings provide new insight into listener–speaker emotional dynamics and how they are disrupted in online interaction and under divided attention.

articles.title: Happier but not more reasonable: Acute physical exercise does not improve rational decision-making
articles.authors: Michal Remiszewski; Gabriela Rajtar; Marcin Maciejczyk; Tomasz Ligeza
articles.abstract:
Physical exercise not only improves overall health but also positively impacts basic cognitive functions, including working memory, attention, and inhibitory control. An intriguing yet underexplored area of research is the influence of exercise on higher cognitive functions, such as decision-making. In this study, we investigated the effects of acute aerobic exercise on rationality in a financial decision-making task that employed the framing effect, a cognitive bias in which individuals’ choices depend on the way information is presented. Participants were randomly assigned to either an exercise group or a control group. The exercise group cycled for 20 minutes at moderate intensity, while the control group rested for 20 minutes. We measured rationality and mood in both groups before and after the intervention.

Results showed that the control group performed significantly more rationally in the post-test compared to the pre-test, whereas the exercise group showed no such improvement. At the same time, mood improved significantly in the exercise group but not in the control group. We speculate that the positive mood induced by exercise may have fostered a sense of illusory control, heightened optimism, and increased risk-taking tendencies, which may have obscured the learning effects observed in the control group.

articles.title: No evidence of differences between hierarchical versus distinction relations in self-based ACT exercises: A critical reanalysis of Foody et al. (2013) and Foody et al. (2015)
articles.authors: Ian Hussey
articles.abstract: The evidential link between Relational Frame Theory (RFT) and Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) is a matter of on-going debate. Foody et al. (2013) is frequently cited as evidence for ACT’s concept of Self-as-Context and as evidence for the ties between ACT’s middle level clinical terms and the more basic account of language and cognition provided by RFT. However, the failed replication study by Foody et al. (2015) typically goes uncited. Given their importance, I critically re-evaluate the results of both articles. I found several issues, the most important of which was that the central claim does not correspond with the reported analyses. When I extracted summary statistics from both studies and used them to conducte a multiverse analysis, that mapped directly onto the original claim (i.e., that the hierarchy condition reduced distress more than the distinction condition), null results were found under every set of conditions. Taken together, results illustrate how weak or flawed research practices and uncritical citation of results can hinder cumulative science and evidence based clinical practice.

articles.title: Shared and Diverse Impressions of Portrait Paintings: An Exploratory Study from the Perspective of Individual Differences
articles.authors: Takanori Sano; Hideaki Kawabata
articles.abstract: This study examines the agreement of the five dimensions of impression evaluation of portraits (attractiveness, beauty, abstract-figurative, emotional arousal, and masculinity-femininity) and their relationship with individuals’ aesthetic interests and attitudes. We utilized data gathered from 621 Japanese participants in a web experiment to analyze the agreement of evaluations using Mean Minus One (MM1) and the causal relationship between impression evaluations and personal traits using linear non-Gaussian non-cyclic model (LiNGAM). The results showed that beauty evaluations had a higher MM1 than attractiveness, indicating that the beauty evaluations of the portraits were relatively consistent, and that there are standard criteria based on visual characteristics and compositional elements. However, the participants’ evaluations of attractiveness and emotional arousal tended to vary significantly owing to subjective factors. The LiNGAM causal structure analysis showed that sex differences in meaning interpretation may be involved in aesthetic judgments. Attractiveness predicted beauty in male portraits, whereas beauty influenced attractiveness and emotional arousal in female portraits. Furthermore, we confirmed a mediating path through which personal traits, such as moral beauty and beautiful ideas, influence final evaluations via masculinity-femininity impressions and emotional arousal. These findings highlight both the agreement and structural diversity underlying impression formation.

articles.title: Continuity Therapy for couples living with acquired brain injury: a non-randomised uncontrolled feasibility study
articles.authors: Gerard Anthony Riley; Urszula Gajewska; Barbara Hagger; Asweeni Jansdin Raj; Natasha Yasmin; Giles Yeates
articles.abstract:
Background:  Brain injuries can have a significant negative impact on marriages/partnerships. Available interventions either require specialists trained in relationship therapy (which most rehabilitation services do not have access to) or offer more structured manualised approaches that are restricted in scope and have generally led to only small or no improvement.  Continuity Therapy was developed to provide a manualised approach that can be delivered by brain injury clinicians without specialist training, and that addresses a broader range of relationship issues.  The aims of the study were to complete a preliminary evaluation of the benefits of the therapy to determine whether progression to a randomised controlled trial is justified; to obtain feedback that will be used to refine the therapy; and to evaluate selection criteria and patient-reported outcome measures. 
Methods:  Fifteen couples living with brain injury received the therapy.  They completed questionnaires about their relationship and psychological wellbeing before and after the therapy, and at 3-month follow-up.  They and the therapists were also interviewed (or provided written feedback) about their experience of the therapy.
Results:  Although not everyone benefitted, participants showed a mean large effect improvement on post-therapy questionnaires about their relationship, and this was sustained at 3 months. They showed a mean moderate effect improvement in their individual psychological wellbeing that was again sustained at 3 months.  Feedback provided suggestions about improvements to the therapy; information about the circumstances of couples that impacted the effectiveness of the therapy (relevant to selection criteria); and information about outcome measures.
Conclusions: The therapy showed sufficient promise to support progression to a randomised controlled trial. A range of outcome measures is required to capture the benefits of the therapy for the relationship. Some refinement of the selection criteria is also necessary.
Trial registration: The study was registered with ISRCTN: The UK’s Clinical Study Registry (registration number – 93611293) on 6.6.2023.

articles.title: Modifying Empathic Disequilibrium Through a Brief Mindfulness-Based Meditation Training
articles.authors: Ido Shalev; Emily Hayun; Alal Eran; Florina Uzefovsky
articles.abstract: Whether a person feels overwhelmed by others' emotions or remains detached from them may depend not on how much empathy they have, but on the balance between the cognitive and emotional components of empathy. An imbalance between emotional and cognitive empathy, termed empathic disequilibrium, has consistently distinguished clinical from non-clinical groups across a range of conditions, independent of overall empathy levels. Given these links, empathic disequilibrium may be experienced by some as distressing, yet whether it is malleable and what mechanisms may underlie such change remains untested. Building on the view that empathic disequilibrium acts as a form of emotion dysregulation in interpersonal contexts, and on its links to emotional reactivity, this study tested whether a brief mindfulness intervention can modify empathic disequilibrium. A total of 157 psychology students were assigned to ten days of daily, 10 minute guided mindfulness training (n = 89) or to a control group involving emotion regulation lessons and daily mood ratings (n = 68). Empathic disequilibrium and emotional reactivity were assessed at baseline and post-intervention using validated self-report measures. Compared with the control group, mindfulness training reduced both forms of empathic disequilibrium. Contrary to our hypothesis, this reduction was not mediated by changes in emotional reactivity, even though reactivity itself decreased. Complementary Bayesian and propensity-score sensitivity analyses supported the robustness of these effects. These findings provide the first evidence that empathic disequilibrium is modifiable in the general population, and suggest that mechanisms beyond emotional reactivity sustain this interpersonal imbalance.

articles.title: Associative Memory Constraints on the Storage and Retrieval of Action-Effect Relations
articles.authors: Marcel Raphael Schreiner; Wilfried Kunde
articles.abstract: How are motor activities linked to their perceptual outcomes? We here test the idea that the storage and retrieval of action-effect relations is subject to constraints of associative memory. Specifically, we argue that mutual interference between such representations should favor the storage and retrieval of unique (one-to-one) action-effect relations as compared to overlapping (one-to-many) action-effect relations (uniqueness effect). Moreover, we assume that increasing the number of repetitions of action-effect episodes facilitates learning and modulates this uniqueness effect. In three experiments, participants first produced certain visual effects through motor activities and were later asked to retrieve the activity that had produced a certain probed visual effect before. For the most part, retrieval performance increased with an increasing number of action-effect repetitions. Retrieval performance was higher for unique as compared to overlapping action-effect relations if the number of repetitions varied between different action-effect episodes (Experiment 3) but not if it was kept constant (Experiment 1). This uniqueness effect in Experiment 3 was not substantially modulated by the number of action-effect repetitions. Overall, results suggest that the storage and retrieval of action-effect relations are subject to certain associative memory constraints. We discuss these findings against the backdrop of associative and episodic retrieval accounts.

articles.title: Immune system dysregulation in Psychopathology: Insights from the Complete Blood Count (CBC)
articles.authors: Farhad Montazeri
articles.abstract:
We explored biological correlates of psychopathology in 1,229 subjects of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, who completed both Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL 6-18) and a set of laboratory tests (Complete Blood Count, High-Density Lipoprotein, total cholesterol, and ferritin). An Ordinary Least Squares model identified independent associations between the CBCL 'Total Problems' scale, as a measure of psychopathology load, and the first factor of the clusters formed by the laboratory tests: 'inflammation' and 'anemia'. 

In the 'inflammation' cluster, platelet count, white blood cells, monocyte and neutrophil counts, lymphocyte percentage, and HDL correlated with CBCL scales. Markers of neutrophilic inflammation correlated stronger with the 'internalizing' dimension, while male sex correlated with the 'externalizing' dimension. Erythropoiesis negatively correlated with several scales across both. Eosinophils correlated with ADHD. The 'Total Problems' scale was independently associated with the 'Inflammation factor' (β = 0.081, CI [0.023, 0.139]), the 'Anemia factor' (β = -0.070, CI [-0.129, -0.014]), and male sex (β= 0.199, CI [0.085, 0.310]).   

Background (auto) inflammation, anemia (likely secondary to the former), and male sex are robust correlates of the psychopathology load. This study sheds light at the biological processes involved in dimensional psychopathology and can improve detection and inform future interventions.

articles.title: Measuring Felt Safety Where It Can Actually Be Seen: The Development of the Dyadic Emotional Safety & Inclusion Instrument (DESI-18)
articles.authors: Vince Jacques de Blauwe; Clifford Stevenson; Antonio Fidalgo
articles.abstract: Felt safety, the subjective sense that one is safe enough to be oneself with another person, is central to whether a first meeting connects or merely happens, yet it is unusually hard to measure: the act of capturing it tends to change it. This paper documents the development of the Dyadic Emotional Safety & Inclusion (DESI-18) instrument, a brief partner-rated measure for first-meeting dyads, and tells the story honestly, including the two measurement failures that shaped it. The construct was pursued across three vantage points. A physiological operationalisation (HRV and electrodermal activity) was abandoned at pilot when the sensors disrupted the very encounter they were meant to read. A single-coder behavioural sheet (the NSI), fielded across fifteen dyads, then surfaced a masking paradox: observable behaviour can look warm while felt experience is not, so behaviour alone cannot reach the construct. This forced the partner-report vantage, in which each person rates the other; DESI-18 is its current expression. We are explicit about the limits. The partner-report line has produced only a single-dyad proof-of-concept, with no reliability or factor-analytic evidence yet. The current four-factor structure (Engagement, Responsiveness, Rapport, We-ness) is a theory-led hypothesis, not a data-derived solution; it was co-developed with the second author, who joined the project at the prior six-factor DESI-18 stage and led the move from a safety-and-withdrawal framing to a social-cure, We-ness framing. We present the construct, its genealogy, the instrument, and what a dedicated validation study must test.

articles.title: Two distinct causal beliefs organizing intuitions about mind, action, and body
articles.authors: Joseph Outa; Shari Liu
articles.abstract: Prior research reveals that people tend to hold two conflicting intuitions: On one hand, people think other people have ethereal minds, which are a distinct kind of thing from, and even exist independently from, physical actions and living bodies. On the other hand, people also expect immaterial mental states to routinely interact with physical actions and bodily events, for example by reasoning about mental states as the causes of physical actions. What allows human minds to maintain these opposing beliefs simultaneously? In this paper, we propose that these two beliefs reflect two kinds of causal knowledge: one that organizes people’s capacities in terms of causal origins (e.g. a mind that gives the capacity to remember and think; a physiological body that enables being tired and hungry), and a second that organized them in terms of the causal chains connecting specific capacities to each other (e.g. that remembering something can cause someone to feel hungry). We tested this proposal in 6 pre-registered experiments in US adults (total N = 670). In Experiment 1, we measured people’s beliefs about the similarity and causal connections between 15 actions, mental and bodily events, and replicated the observation that people think of these 3 domains as distinct yet connected. In 5 further experiments (Experiments 2-6), we explored the causal content they articulate by investigating their socio-cognitive functions. Causal chains were used to explain, predict, and intervene on others, and causal origins were used to make inductive inferences, interventions, and reason about biological bases of behavior. These results bridge prior research on latent dimensions of mind (1), theory of mind (2), and mind-body dualism (3, 4), providing a unified account of three distinct and interacting branches of social knowledge: knowledge of other people’s minds, actions, and physiological bodies.

articles.title: Development and Validation of the Children’s Scale for Social-Emotional Learning (CHISSEL): A Culturally Responsive Measure for Indian Middle Schoolers
articles.authors: Shalaka Sharad Shah; Shivakumar Jolad; Sairaj Patki
articles.abstract: A set of three studies was conducted to develop a culturally appropriate Children’s Scale for Social-Emotional Learning (CHISSEL) to assess SEL in Maharashtra, India. Study 1 (CHISSEL 1.0; N = 1,647) with middle schoolers developed 36 culturally adapted items based on Western frameworks of self-management, decision-making, relationship skills, social awareness, and self-awareness. Following expert review and Marathi translation, 28 items were retained. Reliability was acceptable (α = .71), and exploratory factor analysis (EFA) identified five factors explaining 30.73% of the variance. Study 2 (CHISSEL 2.0; N = 3,142) with middle schoolers expanded the scale with 10 life-skills items, improving reliability (α = .75). EFA identified four factors: social-emotional development, social support, self-management, and peer support, and confirmatory factor analysis supported a final 25-item structure. Study 3 (CHISSEL 3.0) with middle school teachers (N = 168) established validity through a mixed-methods approach. Content validity showed acceptable agreement across domains (Aiken’s V = .72 to .75). Consistent with Messick’s unified framework, response-process evidence from teachers’ interpretations and revisions confirmed item clarity and construct alignment. The CHISSEL is discussed as a contextually grounded, reliable SEL scale for Indian schools, available in English and Marathi, with implications for curriculum, interventions and policy.

articles.title: A Multidisciplinary Perspective on Research Participation in the Era of Post-Amyloid Disclosure and Disease-Modifying Therapies
articles.authors: Joseph Giorgio; Caroline Faucher; Sarah Sanders; Jacob Morrison; Shiami Luchow; Michael Breakspear; Anna Behler; Renate Thienel
articles.abstract: The emergence of disease-modifying therapies for Alzheimer's Disease (AD) and advances in plasma biomarker diagnostics for AD have transformed the landscape of research participation. Older adults are increasingly learning of elevated amyloid-beta (Aβ) through primary care, clinical trials or research involvement. This can occur years before the onset of cognitive symptoms. Motivations for involvement and expectations of participants therefore might shift following disclosure. This likely shift, from predominantly altruistic engagement toward monitoring and intervention-oriented motivations, especially in Aβ- positive participants, blurs the boundary between research and clinical care, generating ethical and practical challenges for research teams. Drawing on multidisciplinary clinical and research experience, this perspective discusses how motivational divergence following Aβ disclosure shapes participant-researcher interactions and outlines the governance structures, ethical considerations, role definitions, and communication frameworks required to navigate these challenges. We provide ten recommendations to support safe and ethically robust research participation in the post-disclosure era.

articles.title: Sleep-wake timing is central to adolescent sleep health: longitudinal cohort network analyses supporting adding circadian metrics to multidimensional sleep health
articles.authors: Walterson Tak Sang Wong; Zepeng Gou; Flora Le; Pilar Artiach Hortelano; Jessica Nicolazzo; Julia E. Stone; Niamh Lewis; Monika Raniti; Anthony Hand; Evangelos Chachos
articles.abstract:
Objectives: The RU-SATED sleep health model encompasses RegUlarity, Satisfaction, Alertness during wake, Timing, Efficiency and Duration of sleep, but lacks explicit representation of circadian processes. We applied network analysis to data from adolescents to (1) examine relationships and relative centrality of each sleep health dimension; and (2) explore the effects of adding circadian variables to this sleep health model.
Methods: We assessed 175 adolescents (98 female, 12.8±0.4 years) six-monthly over two years. At each timepoint, participants completed two-weeks of at-home sleep monitoring using daily diaries and wrist actigraphy, and an online survey assessing diurnal preference. At baseline and annual timepoints, circadian timing was assessed via salivary dim light melatonin onset (DLMO). Network analysis quantified relationships among sleep regularity index, sleep satisfaction, daytime alertness, sleep-wake times, sleep efficiency/continuity, and total sleep time at each timepoint. Centrality analyses identified the node (variable) most strongly connected with the rest of the nodes for each network. Additional analyses added diurnal preference or DLMO as a node.
Results: The most central (i.e., strongly connected) nodes were sleep onset time, wake time and total sleep time, with sleep onset time and wake time becoming the most central at participants ages 14-15 years. Both diurnal preference and DLMO related to sleep onset and wake timing across timepoints. Diurnal preference also related to satisfaction, regularity, and alertness across timepoints.
Conclusions: Adolescents’ sleep-wake timing was strongly linked to other sleep health dimensions and to circadian phase and diurnal preference. Findings support considering circadian processes when promoting adolescents’ sleep health.

articles.title: Using Large Language Models to Detect Values in Political Speech
articles.authors: Hongkun Zhang; Rong Bai; Yuanze Liu; Joshua Conrad Jackson
articles.abstract: We introduce a new method for detecting values in political speeches using Large Language Models (LLMs). Our method has a three-stage architecture. First, it identifies values in speeches and weights them by importance. Second, it scores each value according to relevance to dimensions of a theoretical taxonomy. Third, these scores are combined to compute the speech’s position in multidimensional value space. We evaluate this method using a sample of 8,270 substantive Congressional speeches spanning 2015–2024. We detect values in these speeches, and score each speech according to the dimensions from Schwartz’s theory of basic human values. Results show that the pipeline’s value extraction is consistent across repeated runs (run-to-run reliability), and that the resulting value scores have high internal consistency. They also show high structural construct validity, recovering the theorized structure of the Schwartz dimensions. Value scores predict legislators’ party membership (Democrats in Congress are more likely to emphasize universalism, whereas Republicans are more likely to emphasize power), and also predict legislator ideology (measured through voting patterns) above and beyond fixed-effects and text-frequency baselines. We view this method as complementing traditional self-report measures of values. By measuring values in text, we can study values in new populations (e.g., political elites, historical figures), and quantify value shifts following sudden exogenous shocks.

articles.title: Childcare Quality and Developmental Outcomes Under Cumulative Ecological Risk:  A Longitudinal Study
articles.authors: Yoo Jeong Jang; Michael Rodriguez
articles.abstract: The ecological model of human development posits that child development is shaped by multiple environmental contexts. Previous researchers have found that cumulative environmental risks across ecological levels—individual, familial, and societal—have detrimental effects on children’s development. In contrast, high-quality childcare has been shown to promote positive developmental outcomes, particularly for children raised in impoverished home environments. Few researchers, however, have examined the specific mechanisms through which different aspects of childcare environments buffer underserved children against the adverse effects of cumulative risk factors. Using longitudinal data from 14 childcare centers and 105 children and their families in the Midwestern United States, we examined potential protective mechanisms of childcare settings for children from low-income families through generalized estimating equations (GEE). As a marginal modeling approach, GEE allows for the examination of how various aspects of childcare settings and cumulative risk factors affected child development over time, taking into account the correlation between repeated measures. We found that better personal care routines and program structures predicted higher child developmental outcomes over and beyond the impact of cumulative risks. However, interactions between childcare characteristics and cumulative risk factors were insignificant, suggesting that the cumulative risks' impact on child development does not vary by childcare characteristics. These findings may offer valuable guidance to policymakers and educators on how to enhance non-parental childcare settings to provide greater protection for underserved children.

articles.title: “We could get out more often if accessibility was improved”: Inaccessible everyday environments, participation, and wellbeing among autistic people and families in Australia
articles.authors: Chris Edwards; Abigail Love; Ru Ying Cai; Iliana Magiati; Gail A Alvares; vicki gibbs
articles.abstract: Autism accessibility research has documented barriers in specific settings, including healthcare, education, employment, transport, and community spaces. However, less is known about the accessibility barriers autistic people and families experience across everyday life, or how these experiences relate to wellbeing and participation. This mixed-methods survey examined accessibility barriers, participation, and perceived wellbeing impacts among 149 autistic adults and 97 parents/caregivers of autistic people in Australia. Autistic adults reported on their own experiences, while parents/caregivers reported on behalf of the autistic person they supported across seven domains: healthcare, employment, employment support, transport, retail, education and training, and community and leisure activities. Barriers were widespread, often experienced across multiple domains, and commonly perceived to have moderate-to-large impacts on wellbeing. Sensory environments were the most frequently endorsed barrier type. Reflexive thematic analysis of 998 open-text responses generated four themes: environments were actively hostile to autistic needs; services were available but not always accessible; access depended on whether people understood autistic needs; and repeated inaccessibility narrowed everyday life. Improving wellbeing and participation for autistic people and their families requires services, systems, and communities to take responsibility for accessibility.

articles.title: Beyond Agency Lists Regulatory Stewardship and System Visibility in New Zealand and the Australian Commonwealth
articles.authors: Michael McGuinness
articles.abstract: The abstract argues that a fair Trans-Tasman comparison should examine New Zealand’s unitary national regulatory system against the Australian Commonwealth’s national regulatory architecture, rather than comparing New Zealand with the entire Australian federation. Using the Seven-Layer Regulatory Architecture, it shows that both systems contain similar layers of regulatory authority, including statutory regulators, departmental units, professional bodies, tribunals, cross-jurisdictional arrangements and regulatory dark matter. The main difference is organisational: New Zealand is more compact and coherent, while the Australian Commonwealth is denser and more dispersed across portfolios, statutory bodies, schemes and low-visibility mechanisms. The wider Australian federation then amplifies this density into greater fragmentation. The abstract concludes that regulatory reform should start with architecture-level visibility, not simple agency lists, and that stewardship must focus on where authority, capability, informal practice and system learning actually sit.

articles.title: From Efficiency to Sufficiency: A Measurement-Informed Framework for Cost-Aware Automated Essay Scoring
articles.authors: Yi Gui
articles.abstract: Compute cost in automated essay scoring (AES) is usually treated as a technical efficiency issue rather than a measurement design concern. Yet deployment decisions require more than reporting latency, model size, or inference expense. In high-stakes settings, total burden includes validation, monitoring, adjudication, compliance, and governance; in instructional settings, pricing, licensing, and integration may dominate cost. This paper reframes AES as a cost-constrained measurement system and introduces a four-layer framework—metering, allocation, unit economics, and decision—for translating raw computational signals into decision-relevant cost constructs. It defines cost per valid score (CPVS) and argues that AES systems should be compared by quality-cost sufficiency: minimizing cost among defensible scoring designs that satisfy measurement requirements. The framework provides a portable basis for representing, comparing, and evaluating cost across AES studies and deployment contexts.

articles.title: Algorithmic Ageism and the Governance of Later Life: How Automated Decision-Making Systems Reshape Inequality Among Older Adults
articles.authors: Oluwatimileyin Aborisade
articles.abstract: Automated decision-making systems increasingly mediate access to welfare benefits, healthcare, employment, and financial services. While sociological scholarship has critically examined algorithmic bias in relation to race, gender, and class, age remains under-theorized. This paper introduces the concept of algorithmic ageism to describe how automated systems reproduce and intensify age-based inequalities through data-driven governance. Drawing on sociological theories of ageism, surveillance, and governmentality, the paper analyzes how later life is rendered governable through algorithmic classification, risk scoring, and prediction. It argues that automated decision-making systems do not merely reflect existing age inequalities but actively reshape the governance of later life by embedding normative assumptions about productivity, risk, and dependency. The paper concludes by discussing the implications of algorithmic ageism for social inequality, democratic accountability, and the regulation of automated systems.

articles.title: Sibling Correlations and SES Overlaps in Late-Career Swedes
articles.authors: Martin Hällsten
articles.abstract: To what extent is socioeconomic standing (SES) uni- or multidimensional? SES is typically measured using variables such as education, occupation, income, and, more recently, wealth, but do these variables reflect the same or different hierarchies? This paper uses the methodology of Karlson and Birkelund (2025) to study how sibling correlations, as a measure of the role of family background, overlap across SES dimensions. Four dimensions of SES are considered: education, occupation, income (earnings and disposable income), and wealth. The findings reveal three clusters in the overlap pattern of sibling correlations across these dimensions: (1) education/occupation, (2) income, and (3) wealth. This pattern remains consistent across subsamples defined by sex. The analysis shows that a unidimensional sibling factor, assuming a single latent SES factor, fails to capture the observed sibling correlations adequately. Still, the unidimensional factor accounts for modest to substantial portions of the sibling correlations across outcomes. The evidence for a single latent family factor weakens further when additional outcomes such as crime, cognitive ability, and physical capacity (health) are included in a younger male sample born between 1958 and 1968. A life-course extension shows substantial, though incomplete, stability in the family characteristics shaping income across early-, mid-, and late-career stages. In conclusion, while a unidimensional SES factor exists, it is substantively dominated by multidimensionality in intergenerational transmission.

articles.title: Americans Favor Predistributive Over Redistributive Economic Policies
articles.authors: David Broska; Jonne Kamphorst; Robb Willer
articles.abstract: Redistributive policies designed to reduce economic inequality through taxes and transfers (e.g., safety-net benefits funded by progressive taxation) receive less support in the U.S. than in many Western democracies. Recent work suggests Americans may be more receptive to “predistributive” policies, which seek to reduce economic inequality before taxes and transfers (e.g., minimum-wage increases). Yet existing evidence leaves open whether this support gap characterizes contemporary Americans overall, and whether it persists when the same policy proposals are described in predistributive versus redistributive terms, with their core provisions held constant. Across 31 nationally representative U.S. survey waves fielded from 2015 to 2024 (total N=384,248), we find greater support for policies classified as predistributive than redistributive. We then fielded a preregistered experiment with a national U.S. sample (N=1,009), randomly varying whether otherwise identical policy proposals were described as increasing earnings for people lower in the income distribution, shifting resources from higher- to lower-income individuals, or presented only with the brief policy description given in all conditions. Support was higher under predistributive than redistributive descriptions. Comparison with the control condition clarifies this difference: predistributive descriptions received support comparable to control, whereas redistributive descriptions received lower support. Exploratory analyses suggest perceived respect for hard work and deservingness of beneficiaries were associated with the support gap. Together, these results suggest Americans prefer policies that reduce inequality by influencing earnings more than through taxes and transfers.

articles.title: Where DESO Disappears: Spatial Inequality and Social Stratification at Labour Market Entry
articles.authors: Katy Morris
articles.abstract: At country-level, a host of evidence suggests there is a sizeable direct effect of social origin (DESO) on initial labour market outcomes, net of educational attainment. What is true at country-level is not always true below country-level, however. Using data from the UK Household Longitudinal Survey and the German Socio-Economic Panel, we show that variable spatial opportunity structures moderate the size of DESO at labour market entry, such that there are places where DESO disappears. Social origins assume greater importance as local labour market conditions deteriorate: in weak local labour markets, non-graduates are approximately 16 percentage points less likely to find employment if their parents are care workers rather than secondary school teachers, while graduates typically obtain first jobs that are 7–9 ISEI points lower in status. These findings highlight the distinctive geography of social stratification processes at labour market entry and potentially beyond.

articles.title: Fishing Sector Perspectives: Perceived Threats in the Polish Baltic Sea Fleet by Vessel Size and Target Species
articles.authors: Michael J. Rosciszewski-Dodgson; Giuseppe T. Cirella
articles.abstract: As ecological and economic pressures mount in the Baltic Sea, commercial fisheries face increasing challenges. This study investigates fishermen’s perceptions of four major threats—climate change, pollution, overfishing, and marine predators—with attention to vessel size (fishing boat, cutter, supercutter) and target species (demersal, mixed, small pelagic). Based on 129 surveys and 28 interviews across Poland, the analysis shows that while all threats are acknowledged, perceptions vary significantly by operational context. Seventy-eight coded interview quotations illustrate how sector-specific experiences shape these views, underscoring the need to integrate them into fisheries governance. Friedman test results (p < 0.001) confirm significant variation across threat categories, with the strongest contrasts for overfishing and predator concerns. Smaller vessels targeting demersal and mixed stocks identified predators as the primary threat, while supercutters and pelagic operators emphasized quota restrictions and international competition. Climate change and pollution were viewed as less immediate, though some reported localized impacts. Incorporating sector perspectives is essential for adaptive, sustainable fisheries management.

articles.title: Frame and Motivate: Testing strategies to increase participation in data donation studies.
articles.authors: Frieder Rodewald; Florian Keusch; Valerie Hase; Sebastian Prechsl; Frauke Kreuter; Mark Trappmann
articles.abstract:
Data protection laws in many jurisdictions grant users access to information that online platforms collect about them. Researchers capitalize on this legal requirement through data donation studies, where they ask users to request their personal data from an online platform and then share them as part of a survey. We experimentally tested two strategies to boost participation in a pre-registered study with over 2000 YouTube, Instagram or LinkedIn users from Germany: we varied (1) how we invited people, either framing the study as a web survey or directly describing it as a data donation study, and (2) the motivational appeals highlighting either prosocial advantages or personal gains from donating data.
We found that neither study framing nor motivational appeals significantly affected people's willingness to donate data or their actual donation rates. However, framing the study from the start as data donation research led to significantly more people starting the study. Our results demonstrate that researchers need to consider how they design invitations to data donation studies, while revealing that study framing or motivational appeals fail to increase respondents' willingness to donate or their actual donation behavior.

articles.title: Turning Pages into Screens: Media Adaptation and the Reclassification of Cultural Objects
articles.authors: Francesco Rampazzo; Kristopher Velasco
articles.abstract: When a cultural object moves from one medium to another, does it get reclassified, and does that reclassification change demand? We argue that it does, through a mechanism we call medium transposition: audiences evaluate objects by the conventions of the medium in front of them, so crossing media can shift the category in which an object is placed and reshape the demand that follows. We test this argument with Heartstopper, a queer young-adult graphic-novel series adapted by Netflix in April 2022, combining NielsenIQ BookData weekly sales across eleven national markets with 2,981 Amazon book reviews and 874 IMDb viewer reviews. The Netflix adaptation triggered a discontinuous increase of approximately 76,000 weekly book sales, largest in markets with dense local publishing infrastructure and running predominantly through local-language translation editions. Both audiences are responding to the same underlying story, yet they talk about it in strikingly different ways: Amazon reviewers focus on gifting and intergenerational circulation, while IMDb reviewers focus on LGBTQ+ visibility and self-recognition, a gap of 3.9 to one in representation language across platforms. A within-Amazon comparison, holding platform constant, shows that family-gift language rises and identitarian queer language declines after the Season 1 release, suggesting that the classification shift is tied to the medium crossing rather than only to differences between the two review platforms. Medium transposition offers a portable account of rapid, infrastructure-dependent, audience-driven reclassification, one that requires no shift in taste and no campaign by critics. The price, visible in the review evidence, is that expansion and dilution are not easily separated: the same process that routes Heartstopper through mainstream gatekeepers as ordinary teenage fiction also reduces the share of identitarian queer language in the discourse that surrounds it.

articles.title: Paid parenting leave from a child perspective: a pillar of sustainable development
articles.authors: Alzbeta Bartova; Margaret O'Brien; Thordis Reimer; Guðný Björk Eydal; Anna Escobedo; Cristina Castellanos Serrano; Jochen Devlieghere; Marc Grau-Grau; Philip Hwang
articles.abstract: Parenting leave policies play a crucial role in enabling children to grow up in economically secure, family-based care environments during the earliest and most sensitive stages of life. The first 1,000 days, from conception to a child’s second birthday, represent a critical developmental window in which early relationships, emotional regulation, health, and developmental trajectories are shaped. Yet parenting leave has too often been understood primarily as a labour market instrument or as support for parents, rather than as a policy that directly affects children’s rights, well-being, and life chances. This White Paper addresses this imbalance by reviewing parenting leave policies across Europe from a child-centred and child-rights perspective.

articles.title: Settlement, Return, or Onward Migration? An Intersectional Perspective on Migration Decisions
articles.authors: Katia Gallegos Torres; Agnieszka Kanas; Yuliya Kosyakova
articles.abstract: Out-migration is a central yet understudied dimension of migration processes, with important implications for labor supply, integration trajectories, and population composition. This paper examines how out-migration intentions vary across migrant groups and how economic ties, socio-cultural embeddedness, and transnational ties are associated with return and onward migration. Using recent data on immigrants in Germany, the analysis shows that out-migration intentions are shaped by multiple mechanisms rather than a single dominant factor. Socio-cultural embeddedness is the strongest correlate of lower mobility, whereas economic resources and transnational ties have more heterogeneous associations. Return and onward migration are linked to distinct configurations of attachments, and these patterns vary systematically by gender and migration motive. Men are more likely to report onward migration, and economic migrants exhibit higher overall mobility than family migrants and refugees. Overall, the findings highlight the importance of distinguishing between forms of mobility and moving beyond purely economic explanations to better understand migrant retention and mobility dynamics.

articles.title: TRANSCEND-T2D-1 and the First-Line Question: What Retatrutide's Phase 3 Results Mean for Diabetes Care, Access & Equity
articles.authors: Steven Minh Phat Duong
articles.abstract: The TRANSCEND-T2D-1 trial establishes retatrutide as the first triple agonist for type 2 diabetes. Over 40 weeks, 537 adults achieved placebo-subtracted HbA1c reductions of 0.88%–1.12% and 8.9%–12.7% (20–29 lbs) weight loss, exceeding tirzepatide's weight loss in SURPASS-1 and rivaling bariatric surgery. Yet retatrutide's efficacy comes with higher adverse events (discontinuation 2.5% overall, 5.1% for 12 mg vs. 0% placebo), a projected annual cost of $12,000, and a budget impact ICER estimates would exceed $880 million annually with <1% eligible patients treated. This creates a hidden equity mechanism: patients with paid sick leave can navigate dose escalation; those without cannot. Existing GLP-1 disparities forewarn. Non-Hispanic Blacks have 28% lower odds of GLP-1 prescription, and Black and Hispanic patients face lower out-of-pocket costs and lower fill rates. An unregulated gray market already flourishes: 15 pounds of retatrutide entered the U.S. between Jan–Apr 2026, with 14 hospitalizations reported. Three policy interventions, CMS Coverage with Evidence Development, FDA post-marketing diversity requirements, and state Medicaid VBP, could improve access if implemented before FDA approval, expected within 12-18 months. The next 18 months will determine whether this breakthrough succeeds or becomes a marker of who can afford the best care.

articles.title: Is it all about the costs? Cultural distance and adjustment of recent migrants
articles.authors: Andreas Genoni; Jean Philippe Décieux; Elke Murdock
articles.abstract: The cultural distance (CD) between the origin and destination country of migrants has long been considered important for individual adjustment. Past research predominantly followed a cost perspective, emphasising rising adjustment costs with increasing CD and postulating a negative linear relationship. Previous studies therefore overlooked potential benefits of CD for adjustment. We address this gap by taking a more differentiated perspective on CD and adjustment. Using probability-based survey data on recent German migrants , we examined migrant adjustment at different CD-levels. Employing multilevel regressions for sociocultural and psychological adjustment, the findings reveal an inverted-U shaped relationship between CD and both adjustment dimensions. This challenges the cost perspective, indicating an “optimal” range of intermediate CD as most beneficial for adjustment. Thus, the prevailing view on negative consequences of CD for adjustment may underestimate the challenges for culturally close migrants, and overlook that certain levels of cultural dissimilarity can facilitate migrants’ adjustment.

articles.title: Disfungsi Pengawasan Badan Permusyawaratan Desa: Subordinasi Struktural, Asimetri Informasi, dan Desain Kelembagaan Alternatif
articles.authors: Sahroni Darmawan
articles.abstract: The Village Consultative Council (BPD) was designed under Law 6/2014, amended by Law 3/2024, as an institutional counterweight to the village head. In practice, it operates in dysfunction due to structural subordination and fiscal dependency. Through policy analysis of Law 3/2024, GP 16/2026, and MoHA Regulations 110/2016 and 20/2018, this article identifies seven design failures, including budget dependency, recruitment vulnerabilities, weak enforcement, and information asymmetry. Comparative analysis with local councils in India, Bangladesh, and the Philippines reveals that oversight independence requires protected budgets and proactive information access. This article recommends three policy innovations via regional discretion without statutory amendment: a protected budget mechanism (10–15% of the Village Fund Allocation), a proactive right-to-receive information framework, and an institutionalized Village Social Audit Forum integrated with regional inspectorates and measured through a Village Democracy Health Index (VDHI).  Keywords: village governance, BPD oversight, institutional design, social audit, Village Fund Allocation

articles.title: Aspirational Mathematics and the Behavioral Sciences: The Columbia Behavioral Models Project, 1952–1957
articles.authors: Jefferson Pooley
articles.abstract: The Behavioral Models project (1952–1957) at Columbia University had a clear mission: to make mathematics legible to social scientists. The project, funded by the Office of Naval Research (ONR), enlisted mathematicians and philosophers to demonstrate how mathematical models could be applied to the study of human behavior. Measured against this aim, the project was a failure. The flavors of mathematical modeling promoted by the project were rarely adopted by mainstream social scientists, the target audience. If anything, the disciplines embraced (or re-embraced) the correlational tradition of inferential statistics—an approach that the project had defined itself against. The paper argues that the Behavioral Models Project (BMP) is an expression of aspirational mathematics. The postwar behavioral sciences movement imagined itself, in a gauzy, rhetorical sense, in the image of the natural sciences. But its more proximate reference discipline was economics, which was already self-segregating by the early 1950s. Economists’ mix of mathematical rigor and policy influence, on early and notably partial display in these years, made their discipline an object of envy and qualified emulation. The BMP—framed as a maths booster shot for the behavioral sciences—was a high-profile index of that dynamic. Mathematical sophistication, if not necessarily the economists’ variety, was something to cultivate. As the BMP case helps to show, the aspiration went unfulfilled.

articles.title: Focused MAIHDA: Studying Intersectional Inequalities through the Lens of a Focal Social Identity
articles.authors: George Leckie
articles.abstract: Intersectional MAIHDA (Multilevel Analysis of Individual Heterogeneity and Discriminatory Accuracy) is a widely used approach in quantitative intersectional research for exploring how mean outcomes vary across combinations of social identity characteristics, such as gender, race, and social class. However, in many studies, researchers are especially interested in a particular identity, such as gender, operationalised (imperfectly) by sex. This gives rise to additional questions that cannot be addressed using standard MAIHDA: Does the magnitude and importance of intersectional inequalities differ between men and women? If so, who experiences greater inequality? To what extent do additive patterns of intersectional inequality align across the sexes, and where do they differ? Are departures from these general patterns, reflecting unique intersectional experiences, more pronounced for one sex than the other? Finally, at the level of specific strata, where do the largest sex differences emerge? To address these questions, we introduce a focused-lens approach to MAIHDA that enables researchers to investigate situations in which sex—or any other social identity—is of particular interest. In doing so, we expand the range of questions that MAIHDA can address and thereby broaden its contribution to quantitative intersectional research. We motivate and illustrate this approach by revisiting the seminal BMI MAIHDA application of Evans et al. (2018).

articles.title: The Red Queen at the Grassroots: The Co-Evolution of Cadre Resistance and  Regime Control
articles.authors: Xuanyuan Li
articles.abstract:
Why do Chinese grassroots cadres sometimes voluntarily report their own 
disciplinary violations, triggering investigations that suspend their impossible policy 
tasks? And what happens when the disciplinary system learns to strike back? Drawing 
on 32 in-depth interviews with current and former cadres in S City, H Province, 
combined with official statistics and policy documents (2020–2026), this paper 
documents an emerging phenomenon:"cadre self-informing",and traces its evolution 
from a rational risk-avoidance strategy to a failing one.
Self-informing becomes rational under a triple condition: fiscal depletion 
(local self-sufficiency rate below 35%), incentive inversion (the penalty for 
"incompetence" outweighs that for "corruption"), and the collapse of social trust. 
Facing zero-budget demolition tasks and catastrophic sanctions for failure, cadres 
strategically exploit a procedural rigidity,that every disciplinary report must be 
investigated,to pause the implementation clock and seek refuge in a bounded, 
predictable investigation rather than an unbounded, career-ending charge of 
incompetence.
Yet the system does not remain inert. Through longitudinal tracking of a key 
case,a cadre whose self-informing strategy survived a traditional investigation but 
unraveled spectacularly under an AI-enhanced one,this paper reveals that the 
disciplinary apparatus is itself a learning entity. AI-driven tools compressed 
investigation timelines, shattered the informational opacity "battle-tested" cadres had 
relied upon, and fundamentally recalibrated the risk calculus of self-informing.
Drawing on the Red Queen effect, this paper reconceptualizes the "execution 
limit" of China's pressure-based system not as a static boundary, but as a moving front 
in an ongoing arms race between agents and the institutions that discipline them. The 
findings advance institutional gap theory beyond state-society contention into internal 
regime-agent dynamics, and identify a "high-pressure trap" in adaptive 
authoritarianism: over-mobilization drains the very resources and trust that 
adaptability requires, generating the evasion it seeks to extinguish.

articles.title: Rethinking Certification Culture in Procurement and Supply Chain: Why Capability Must Replace Credentialism in the Age of AI
articles.authors: Alaa Khodroj
articles.abstract: Certification bodies have long shaped the professional landscape of procurement and supply chain, offering standardized knowledge and global recognition. Yet the increasing reliance on theoretical exams, recurring fees, and credential centric hiring has created structural challenges that limit inclusivity, undervalue experience, and fail to reflect the realities of modern practice. This paper examines the limitations of certification driven evaluation, the social and economic pressures it creates, and the widening gap between certification standards and real world capability — especially as artificial intelligence now performs many tasks traditionally tested in certification exams. Rather than dismissing certification bodies, the paper proposes a shift toward capability based, evidence driven, AI enhanced validation that honors practical judgment, ethical decision making, and lived professional experience. The goal is reform, not rejection: a modernized model where certification bodies evolve from exam providers into capability verification authorities.

articles.title: The Structural Failure of Passive Observation and Recovery by Source-Independent Action: Minimal Results in a Finite, Elementary Setting
articles.authors: ZHIKE ZHANG
articles.abstract: Using only elementary finite mathematics—the pigeonhole principle and the finite law of total probability, with no causal graph, no measure theory, and no axiom of infinity—we characterise when passive observation suffices to predict interventions and when it does not, obtaining three results. First, impossibility: every bounded representation f : X → Z with |X| > |Z| satisfying a sufficient-statistic condition admits a pair of environments that are observationally identical yet interventionally distinct, on which every passive strategy fails at every sample size. The obstruction performs no arithmetic on probabilities—distributions enter only through equality and its negation, so it holds verbatim with distributions replaced by abstract labels; arithmetic enters exactly once, a single inequality converting the failure into error floors uniform in the sample size. Second, sufficiency: under a source-invariance condition on the outcome mechanism, any non-adaptive policy that draws each action value from a source independent of the latent state recovers the interventional distribution by a single finite sum.
