Similar results are obtained when measuring SES with household income. Additional analyses show that absences in later years mediate the SES-achievement gaps in Years 6 and 11. Our findings suggest that improving attendance will help reduce the SES-achievement gap.

articles.title: Between Invisible and Illegitimate: How the DSA ‘sees’ Resistance to Content Moderation
articles.authors: Naomi Appelman
articles.abstract: For as long as platforms have been moderating people’s online participation they, in turn, have found ways to resist such sociotechnical governance. This paper offers two contributions to the field of platform governance through consolidating and mapping the sociological literature on these different practices of resistance and, subsequently, systematizing their legal classifications under the EU Digital Services Act (DSA) in a typology. Deepening the understanding of this relationship between resistance and the legal framework will help in delineating the types of conflicts the DSA can(not) address and what space it leaves people to resist moderation by means outside the law, which is especially relevant in the context of rising platform illiberalism. The paper illuminates how the DSA is largely unable to ‘see’ practices of resistance, disregarding the systemic conditions they respond to, and in the rare instances that it does acknowledge resistance, renders it illegitimate.

articles.title: Centring Resistance: How the EU Law and Political Economy of Content Moderation Structures Online Participation
articles.authors: Naomi Appelman
articles.abstract:
How people resist the governance of their online participation offers vital insight into how power functions on platforms, yet platform governance research has insufficiently recognised the methodological value of these practices. Grounded in radical democratic theory, this paper argues that centring concrete instances of resistance as entry points uniquely illuminates the legal and political-economic structures of content moderation.
 
The paper develops a theoretical framework positioning practices of resistance not as mere technical workarounds but as democratic sites of contestation that reveal how power circulates within content moderation systems. Drawing on standpoint epistemology and Tully's conception of 'practices of freedom', it argues that the experiences of those subjected to platform governance hold privileged epistemic value, particularly given the restrictive access to platform data that limits other modes of inquiry. 

This framework is applied through three vignettes centring Black women, fat women, and sex workers who develop strategies of circumvention, challenge, and refusal in response to moderation systems that systematically exclude them. Each vignette uses a specific practice of resistance as an entry point to illuminate a distinct dimension of the political economy of content moderation: the recession of contestable sites into opaque algorithmic systems; the collective visibility generated by circumvention practices such as algospeak; and the structural limits of resistance shaped by platforms' advertiser-driven business models and the EU's Digital Services Act.

The paper concludes that centring resistance as a method is essential for understanding how legal and political-economic power operates in online speech governance.

articles.title: Prices in Different Modes of Sale: Theory and RCT Evidence from Forced Sales
articles.authors: Matz Dahlberg; Mikael Lundholm; Mattias Nordin; Henk Schouten
articles.abstract: The mode of sale may affect prices not only through selling rules, but also by changing the composition of potential buyers. We study this mechanism by randomly varying the mode of sale in a three-year field experiment, conducted with the Swedish Enforcement Authority, that assigns foreclosed homes to auction or brokered sale. Assignment to brokered sales raise prices by about 15% relative to auctions. Using pre-treatment appraisals, we show that the foreclosure discount is mode-dependent: auction sales occur at a substantial discount, whereas brokered sales occur close to appraised market value. To explain why the discount arises in auctions, we develop a model in which auction participation barriers deter household buyers and create scope for resale-oriented professional buyers, lowering prices and potentially generating inefficient allocations. Historical individual-level foreclosure data support the mechanism: repeat buyers are present in auctions, purchase at larger discounts, and resell more quickly than other buyers.

articles.title: Agent-based models of social media: A scoping review and research agenda
articles.authors: Aytalina Kulichkina; Daisuke Nakamura; Taehee Kim; Jula Lühring; David Garcia; Dongyoung Sohn; Annie Waldherr
articles.abstract: Social media research has advanced our understanding of individual-level processes in online communication, yet these insights are rarely linked to meso- and macro-level outcomes relevant to the field, including polarization dynamics, misinformation spread, and public opinion formation. Agent-based modeling (ABM), as a method of constructing theoretical representations of systems, offers a powerful approach to bridging this gap by linking micro-level interactions to meso-level processes and emergent macro-level phenomena. While ABM has been widely adopted in computer science, engineering, mathematics, and economics to model social media communication, such applications often rely on theoretical assumptions and empirical findings outside of social media research. To address this disconnect and encourage broader adoption of ABM in the field, we systematically review how the method has been applied across various disciplines to study social media phenomena and offer a comprehensive foundation for building on existing models. We identify common practices, research gaps, and opportunities for theoretical, methodological, and open science advancements. The resulting agenda provides a practical entry point for re-searchers new to modeling and recommends grounding relevant models in established theories and insights from social media research.

articles.title: Effect of financial satisfaction on life satisfaction among older adults in Finland
articles.authors: Ilari Ilmakunnas; Liisa-Maria Palomäki
articles.abstract:
Objectives. As populations age, it is increasingly important to understand the factors that shape life satisfaction among older adults. Financial security is widely considered one of the major life domains that contributes to life satisfaction. However, there is a scarcity of longitudinal studies examining how financial satisfaction affects overall life satisfaction among older adults. Previous analyses have mainly focused on cross-sectional associations rather than causal designs.
Methods. This study addresses several existing research gaps by using longitudinal survey data on Finnish old-age pensioners aged 64 or over. Fixed effects linear regression models are used to help isolate the effect of financial satisfaction. A sensitivity analysis is conducted to determine whether unobserved confounders could explain the results. In addition, to contextualize the observed effect of financial satisfaction, we compare that effect with the effects of changes in major life-course events.
Results. The study finds that financial satisfaction has a considerable effect on overall life satisfaction. Sensitivity analysis suggests it is unlikely that the effect of financial satisfaction is explained by unobserved confounders, lending further support to the idea that there is a causal relationship. Changes in financial satisfaction are more relevant to life satisfaction than changes in self-rated health. Changes in partnership status or work status had no effect on life satisfaction.
Discussion. Overall, the study shows that changes in financial satisfaction influence life satisfaction among older adults. Improving financial security in retirement can therefore be expected to also improve subjective well-being among aging populations.

articles.title: The Absorbent Mind: A Study of Parental Awareness at Blue Blocks Montessori School
articles.authors: Pavan Goyal; Raunak Sharma
articles.abstract: This study examines parental understanding of the Montessori concept of the Absorbent Mind through the lens of structured home-based observations conducted across January and February 2026. Drawing on 99 parent-reported observations across two branches of Blue Blocks Montessori School, Hyderabad, the study explores parental awareness across four domains: language, social behaviour, movement, and environmental order. It additionally documents parent-reported intensity of sensitive periods and the consistency of mindful parental behaviour as a form of environmental input. Findings indicate that while most parents demonstrated strong observational awareness across domains, conceptual knowledge of the Absorbent Mind consistently outpaced the practice of sustained mindful engagement with children. Sensitive period intensity showed a clear peak in language and movement. The paper concludes by discussing implications for parent education within Montessori settings.

articles.title: Empirical Verification of an Asset Dynamics Model in Open Economies: An Equilibrium Theory Endogenizing Imbalances Bridging Steady-State Conditions and Social Adaptation
articles.authors: Kazuhito Kitamura
articles.abstract:
The general equilibrium theory, upon which mainstream modern macroeconomics relies, fails to adequately explain the realities enveloping the contemporary economy, including persistent global imbalances. In contrast, our previous studies (Kitamura, 2025, 2026) have proposed a theoretical model based on "asset dynamics" that conceptualizes the global economy as a dissipative structure woven by the concentration and diffusion of funds. We understand it as an "equilibrium theory endogenizing imbalances" where economies, each harboring its own imbalances, maintain a balance by compensating for each other's surpluses and deficits.
This paper statistically verifies the empirical validity of this theoretical framework using multi-source macroeconomic data and socio-psychological indicators. Targeting approximately 80 countries for which data are available, we redefine a socio-psychological indicator based on a large-scale international survey regarding time value, patience, and other attributes, as a proxy for the divergence between the real interest rate and the time preference rate—which constitutes one side of the steady-state condition derived from our theoretical model. Conducting a regression analysis using the structural terms that constitute the right-hand side of the conditional equation, namely population changes, capital transfer, and asset preference, we obtained statistically significant results, including the sign conditions. Furthermore, by utilizing these estimation results, we visualized the dissipative structure that weaves the global economy, rendering the contrasting positions and interdependence of the two major economic superpowers as data-driven facts, such as the United States as a self-organizing economy and China as a dissipative economy.
The contributions of this paper provide an empirical foundation for a new macroeconomic paradigm that reframes imbalances not as an undesirable state to be eliminated, but endogenously explains them as the energy required for the global economic system to maintain its vitality.

articles.title: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN PUBLIC FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT: A SYSTEMATIC REVIEW OF READINESS, GOVERNANCE, RISKS, AND APPLICATIONS IN BUDGETING, PROCUREMENT, AND EXPENDITURE MONITORING
articles.authors: RALPH RENDELL TOLEDO
articles.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly transforming public-sector operations by enhancing data-driven decision-making, automation, and governance capabilities. Within Public Financial Management (PFM), AI applications have emerged in budgeting, procurement, auditing, fraud detection, and expenditure monitoring, offering significant opportunities to improve efficiency, transparency, accountability, and fiscal sustainability. Despite growing interest in AI-enabled governance, existing research remains fragmented across technological, organizational, and governance perspectives. This study addresses this gap by conducting a systematic literature review of AI applications in Public Financial Management, with particular emphasis on budgeting, procurement, and expenditure monitoring. Guided by the PRISMA 2020 framework, the review synthesized 72 studies published between 2015 and 2026 from academic journals, conference proceedings, books, and institutional reports. Thematic analysis identified five major themes: (1) AI applications in Public Financial Management, (2) organizational readiness for AI adoption, (3) algorithmic accountability and responsible AI governance, (4) implementation risks and challenges, and (5) Public Financial Management performance outcomes. Findings indicate that successful AI implementation depends not only on technological capability but also on data readiness, digital infrastructure, human capital, leadership commitment, regulatory preparedness, and robust governance mechanisms. Transparency, explainability, accountability, auditability, ethical compliance, and human oversight emerged as critical determinants of responsible AI adoption. Drawing upon the review findings and Toledo’s (2026) algorithmic accountability framework, the study proposes a conceptual governance framework linking AI readiness, AI adoption, governance mechanisms, risk management, and PFM performance. The framework contributes to the literature by integrating previously fragmented research streams and provides policymakers and practitioners with a structured approach for the responsible deployment of AI in government financial systems.

articles.title: Bias by Design? An Illustrative Examination of the Effects of Sex in Tests of Lexical Ability
articles.authors: Jeanette Melin; Emil Lundell; Henrik Nordström
articles.abstract: This study concerned a selection of synonym and antonym items, which were previously shown to exhibit sex bias. They were recomposed into new item sets and retested to illustrate potential risks in item analysis and the implications of relying on biased items when measuring lexical ability. A total of 2,923 individuals taking admission tests for basic military training in Sweden between mid-November 2024 and mid-January 2025 responded to selections of synonym and antonym items alongside the Swedish Enlistment Battery. Item properties were analyzed within the framework of the Rasch model, focusing on subsets of items categorized by their pre-defined sex bias. Estimates of lexical ability based on these subsets were compared using t-tests, and stanine categories were analyzed through ordinal logistic regressions. As expected, the use of potentially sex-biased items led to inconsistent estimates of lexical ability, systematically favoring either male or female test takers, depending on the composition of the items. When all items were combined, clear evidence of multidimensionality and violations of measurement invariance emerged. By highlighting critical risks associated with biased item content, this study provides an illustrative case of concerns about fairness in verbal testing. It offers valuable insights for future item development and encourages continued methodological research to support the creation of equitable and valid assessments of lexical ability.

articles.title: DETERMINANTS AND ECONOMIC IMPACT OF CROP DIVERSIFICATION: A MICRO-LEVEL ANALYSIS OF PRODUCTIVITY, INCOME RESILIENCE, AND CLIMATE ADAPTATION IN AGRARIAN ECONOMIES
articles.authors: Dr Sadasiba Tripathy
articles.abstract:
Abstract
Indeed, the potential of diversifying crops as a strategy to address these concurrent issues of climate variability, income insecurity, and productivity stagnation in agri-based economies is picking up momentum. The present paper constructs a micro level framework to study determinants and economic consequences of crop diversification in an eastern semi-arid agrarian district in eastern India among small scale farmers, marginal farmers and medium farmers. The study employs a mixed primary - secondary data design with 240 farm households in six villages structured in a crops hierarchy to calculate diversification based on Herfindahl concentration ratio and Simpson Crop Diversification Index. Using descriptive, exploratory statistics, correlation analysis and OLS regression analysis, the analysis cross-references diversification and farm productivity, gross margin per acre, and income resilience, and climate adaptation behaviour. The findings also show that the accessibility of irrigation water, receiving educational information, knowledge of extension officer, and proximity of markets, institutional credit, risk perception and climate change adaptation strategies have significant effects on crop diversification. Households in the high diversification group contribute more crop-equivalents than those in the cereal-concentrated group and enjoy better income returns per acre and reduced income variability. The estimated improvements in farm income and resilience scores are significant between every 0.10 increase in the Simpson diversification index, regardless of the number of other factors adjusted for, such as farm size, input intensity, use of irrigation, and adoption of technology. The rationale behind diversification also points to its ability to be a mechanism for livelihood risk-staking management rather than just a adapting to cropping patterns, particularly under climate shocks manifested in mono-cropping rice-wheat systems. The paper makes a case for shifting focus from area substitution to integrated policy support for water security, water extension, local value chains, water aggregation/drying, price assurance and farmer-producer institutional support. Results are relevant to sustainable inclusive agricultural development, climate resilient rural livelihoods and provision of farmers/agricultural policy at regional level in India.
Keywords: Crop diversification; farm income; climate adaptation; productivity; smallholders; Herfindahl index; Simpson index; India
JEL Codes: Q12; Q15; Q18; Q54; O13; C31

articles.title: Behavioral Determinants of Credit Score Improvement and the Role of Structured Credit Counseling in Millennial Debt Recovery
articles.authors: Antwaine Bullocks
articles.abstract: This study, informed by the practitioner expertise of Antwaine Bullocks, known as the Credit Genius, investigates the behavioral determinants of credit score improvement among millennial consumers in the United States and evaluates the role of structured credit counseling programs in accelerating debt recovery outcomes within this demographic. Millennials represent the largest generational cohort in the American workforce yet exhibit disproportionately low credit scores and elevated levels of consumer debt. Drawing on a mixed-methods framework integrating survey data from 312 millennial respondents aged 22 to 36 with qualitative interviews conducted at Rapid Credit Boosters, a San Diego-based credit counseling organization, this paper identifies financial self-efficacy, payment consistency, and credit utilization awareness as primary behavioral predictors of score improvement. Counseled participants recorded an average FICO score improvement of 74 points over six months compared to 31 points among non-counseled peers. The study concludes that coach-led, individualized credit counseling targeting behavioral and psychological dimensions of credit management constitutes an effective and scalable pathway to financial inclusion for the millennial generation.

articles.title: Administrative Trauma and Performativity: Reconciling Identity, Ethics, Morals, and Pragmatics across Operational Strata During Complex Humanitarian Emergencies
articles.authors: Amaya Alexandra Ramos
articles.abstract: As in many professions, human-focused administrative personnel must balance public perception with both safety and desirable service outcomes. Those with high-level, yet ultimately intermediary coordinating roles, have the added pressure of unifying interest blocs situated across varying operational strata, each moving at its own tempo and in accordance with its own objectives.  At times, these diverse priorities may even be in direct contradiction. However, while such brokering may be natural to the role, successful management of complex humanitarian emergencies requires extreme clarity of focus and vision, and as such, there is a heightened emphasis on harmonizing operations towards these larger objectives at all costs, often doing so in environments that are under-resourced in terms of information, materials, manpower, training, and political support. In addition to these baseline stressors, public administrators specifically carry the performance pressure of serving as proxies for a large, optics-and ethics-sensitive social apparatus —the government—  whose operations must be upheld through careful calibration of the social contract. In the course of emergency response, such personnel must therefore navigate traumatic situations that may violate aspects of their deeply held personal beliefs, all while strictly managing personal affect. These cognitive, ethical, and moral reconciliation processes can be intensely draining, and without a minimum amount of decision making authority combined with specialized logistical and emotional support, specialized administrative personnel  may be at risk for a unique and intense kind of moral injury stemming from unremitting crisis combined with suppression of their instinct for advocacy. Via this lens, and in the interest of calling for greater literature on the humanitarian political economy, as well as administrative experiences within migration health research, this report briefly recounts various aspects of the administrative moral injury borne by United States public servants and similar personnel during the 2021 evacuation of eligible parties from Afghanistan following the U.S. military withdrawal from the country and the ensuing Taliban takeover.

articles.title: Unmoved by the Machine: Stability in Finnish Adolescents’ Work-Life Expectations Through the Generative-AI Transition, 2021-2026
articles.authors: Juhani Merilehto
articles.abstract:
The public release of ChatGPT in November 2022 and the subsequent escalation of future
of-work discourse have been accompanied by forecasts that a large share of cognitive work
is exposed to large language models. If adolescents form occupational expectations partly in
response to the perceived future of work, this period should register as a shock to those ex
pectations. Using seven annual waves of a large repeated cross-sectional survey of Finnish
lower- and upper-secondary students (the Nuorten tulevaisuusraportti; 20202026, n ≈ 4,900
6,400 per wave; the core work-expectation battery is observed from 2021), we document the
opposite: aggregate work-life self-expectations are remarkably stable across the generative-AI
transition. Belief in nding one’s place in working life moved within an eight-point band (68
76%) across the observed 20212026 waves; eagerness for working life and felt occupational
competence were e ectively at; the ranking of attractive elds was nearly unchanged. The
few genuine movements a slow rise in entrepreneurial openness and a pre-2022 rise in work
aversion predate or run orthogonal to the AI shock. We interpret this stability through three
converging mechanisms documented elsewhere: the historical stickiness of teenage aspirations,
an invulnerability bias by which disruption is attributed to others’ jobs rather than one’s own,
and the muted realized labour-market e ects of generative AI through 20242025. The claim
throughout concerns aggregate measured expectations, not subjective AI awareness or anxiety.
The nding is descriptive, not causal: the instrument was not designed to measure AI, and
within-wave subgroup gaps dwarf any temporal drift. We argue the null is nonetheless informa
tive for debates that assume youth expectations track technological headlines.

articles.title: The Humane Gate: A Scientific Blueprint for Algorithmic Gatekeeping in Social Media Platforms
articles.authors: Sherif Rashwan
articles.abstract: Algorithmic gatekeeping is the process by which computational systems select, rank, suppress, label, recommend, or route information before it reaches users. This paper develops a comprehensive descriptive research blueprint for any future social media platform that must govern attention, safety, credibility, creator opportunity, public relevance, and user autonomy. The proposed mechanism, called the Multi-Layer Algorithmic Gatekeeping Ecosystem (MAGE), treats gatekeeping as a measurable socio-technical process rather than a hidden ranking trick. MAGE combines content integrity scoring, social graph context, creator reputation, user intent, epistemic value, harm risk, fairness constraints, diversity exposure, machine-learning classifiers, large language models, agentic AI review assistants, human review, appeal rights, audit logs, and public accountability. The paper provides mathematical definitions, optimization objectives, pseudocode tables, descriptive research variables, simulated platform scenarios, validation procedures, distributed microservice designs, and out-of-the-box implementation modules such as pluralistic feeds, friction gates, civic-mode routing, provenance markets, deliberation chambers, epistemic nutrition labels, and user-controlled gatekeeping sliders. The contribution is both a formal theory of humane epistemic gatekeeping and a platform-ready governance architecture that can be implemented before launch, studied descriptively after launch, and audited continuously as the platform evolves.

articles.title: Anthropocene and Empire: Discourse Networks of the Human Record
articles.authors: James Jaehoon Lee
articles.abstract: Scientists have recently proposed 1610 as a candidate for the first year of the new geological epoch known as the Anthropocene, pinpointing the rise of European colonialism and the global movement of life among ecosystems it precipitated as the triggers for measurable changes in the earth system. Using digital humanities methods to mine historical archives, this essay evaluates the relation between colonialism, early modern globalism, and the origins of the Anthropocene. We argue that the Anthropocene was initiated by a premodern earth system that defined life across the globe in spatial terms, furthering the goals of empire — a regime of biopower that has not been adequately acknowledged in debates on the Anthropocene.

articles.title: Using agent-based simulations to anticipate risks and mitigation measures in platform governance
articles.authors: Max van Drunen; Natali Helberger; Aytalina Kulichkina; Annie Waldherr
articles.abstract: Risk analysis and mitigation in platform governance are constrained by the difficulties of testing changes to platforms’ systems and accessing data. Other fields, ranging from public health to economics, as well as an emerging body of platform research, use agent-based modeling to overcome this obstacle by empowering researchers and regulators to test changes virtually. However, it is still unclear how and under what conditions agent-based modeling can effectively support platform governance. This article aims to reduce this uncertainty in two ways. First, it provides a theoretical framework on the benefits and risks of using agent-based modeling in platform governance, drawing on literature on platform risk assessment, the use of agent-based modeling in other fields, and anticipatory governance and evidence-based policymaking. Second, to validate this framework and clarify the conditions under which regulators can rely on evidence produced by agent-based models, the article reports the results of interviews with 18 regulators. The article finds that agent-based simulations have significant potential to complement other sources of evidence used in platform risk assessment. Their main added value is that they allow researchers and regulators to more easily, ethically, and independently explore the potential impacts of changes to platform design, and provide interactive and visual scenarios that concretize abstract platform dynamics.

articles.title: Social Media and Digital Politics: Networked Reason in an Age of Digital Emotion
articles.authors: James Jaehoon Lee; Jeffrey Layne Blevins
articles.abstract: Informed by critical theory, this book employs Social Network Analysis (SNA) to examine the ever-increasing impact that social media has on politics and contemporary civic discourse. In just the past decade, social media platforms have been at the forefront of political discord that played out in the January 6th insurrection, the expulsion of a US President from major social media platforms, the attempted regulation of social media in various states, and the takeover of Twitter (now "X") by one of the richest and (arguably) most financially influential persons in the world. This book examines these phenomena through a comprehensive and in-depth exploration of their meaning and implication for democratic society. Informed by SNA, James Jaehoon Lee and Jeffrey Layne Blevins examine several types of social and political commentary on one of the most influential social media networks and argue that the use of emotional appeals in these posts about social and political topics degrades the quality of civic discourse and encourages the abandonment of reasoning in democratic self-governance. A timely and vital text for upper-level students and scholars in a variety of disciplines from media and communication studies, journalism, and digital humanities to social network analysis, political science, and sociology.

articles.title: When the Compound Speaks: Documenting the Transformation of Indigenous Igbo Architectural Identity in Anambra State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Godsportion Emeka
articles.abstract: The Igbo compound is not simply a collection of buildings arranged around a shared space. It is a spatial argument about how the world is ordered, how authority flows through a family, how the living maintain their relationship with the ancestors and how a community understands itself in relation to the land it occupies. At the centre of that argument stands the obi, the reception house of the compound head, whose physical form, interior arrangement and ritual significance together constitute one of the most culturally dense architectural types in West Africa. This article reports on original ethnographic fieldwork conducted across multiple communities in Anambra State, Nigeria, as part of the EUNIC/Goethe-Institut Nigeria Roots and Roofs Programme. Seven community members were interviewed in person, including elders, obi custodians, engineers, a former community president general and a traditional ruler who serves as Chairman of the Anambra State Traditional Rulers Council. Their testimony, presented here with direct quotation from interview transcripts, documents the nature and extent of the architectural transformation that Igbo domestic space has undergone over the past generation, the materials and cosmological knowledge being lost in that transition, and the enduring significance of the obi as a spatial, social and spiritual institution. The article draws on this primary evidence alongside the scholarly literature on Igbo architecture and spatial culture to argue that what is being lost in the shift from earthen, open and cosmologically grounded compound forms to walled, cement-built modern houses is not merely a matter of materials or aesthetics but of the social and spiritual fabric of community life itself. It calls for the systematic documentation of surviving traditional compounds and obi structures, the integration of vernacular architecture study into Nigerian building technology education, and a design culture that takes seriously the spatial wisdom embedded in the Igbo compound tradition.

articles.title: “I even saw four deer grazing”: online-offline entanglements in the psilocybin pluriverse
articles.authors: Michał Wanke; Paweł Matuszewski; Piotr Siuda
articles.abstract:
Purpose
This paper aims to examine evolving practices and discourses surrounding psilocybin mushrooms on an online drug forum. Situating use within hybrid digital and ecological contexts, it traces shifts in user engagement before and after the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic to contextualize localized realities of the contemporary psychedelic renaissance.

Design/methodology/approach
Using an agential cut framework, the authors conducted a thematic analysis of approximately 3,000 qualitative posts from the largest Polish forum (mid-2000s–2023). The study tracks how forum users integrate digital tools (mapping, smartphone verification) with environmental field observations, mapping the online-offline entanglements and more-than-human relations of foraging.

Findings
Analysis reveals a post-2020 shift from immersive, metaphysical narratives toward predictive, scientifically informed and risk-managed practices. Building directly on critical drug scholarship regarding assemblage thinking and ontological multiplicity, the authors conceptualize the forum as an apparatus within a psychedelic pluriverse, where radically different versions of psilocybin simultaneously coexist, negotiate legitimacy and sustain themselves.

Originality/value
By integrating longitudinal forum analysis with a multispecies and pluriverse perspective, the study shows how digital platforms and embodied ecological practices jointly shape contemporary psychedelic practices, highlighting their relational, contingent and ethically informed character and demonstrating how shifts in discursive framings coincide with changing modes of ecological attention and risk reflexivity.

articles.title: Cultural Representation on AI-Generated Music Platforms: A Quantitative-Qualitative Study
articles.authors: Yiren Zhao; Luca Casini
articles.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) music generation platforms are now widely available worldwide and appear capable of generating music in any language or style describable through text. However, these services are largely developed within Anglo-American cultural contexts, raising questions about how culturally marked differences are represented beyond dominant musical norms. This article examines cultural representation in AI-generated music by focusing on songs associated with Chinese language and cultural references on two major text-to-audio platforms, Suno and Udio. The study adopts qualitative-quantitative approach. Computational text analysis is used to cluster Chinese-language lyrics and analyze user-applied tags, while qualitative musicological case studies examine how these representational patterns are articulated into specific musical instances. The findings show that Chinese cultural references are primarily encoded through a limited set of symbolic markers, such as language labels, traditional instruments, and broad stylistic descriptors. While lyrics frequently draw on classical poetry and religious texts, their sonic realization often relies on globally dominant musical templates, producing hybrid and unstable representations. The article demonstrates that AI music platforms not only reshape the boundaries of musical creation but also actively intervene in the reproduction of cultural representation on a global scale. It contributes a methodological framework for empirically examining cultural representation in AI-generated music and highlights the value of interdisciplinary collaboration between musicology and data science.

articles.title: The role of altmetrics in citation emergence: a temporal approach
articles.authors: José Luis Ortega; Evangelina Becerra Rodero; Sergio Castro Cortacero
articles.abstract: This study aims to identify differences in the temporal distribution of altmetrics, detect associations between them, and determine which metrics have the greatest capacity to influence citation impact. Mentions and their appearance dates for 7,963 research publications were extracted from Altmetric.com. Several data mining techniques (transition matrices and association rules) and survival analysis methods (the Kaplan-Meier estimator, Cox proportional hazards models, and Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) models) were used to find longitudinal relationships between altmetrics and bibliographic citations. The results showed that there are two groups of metrics according to their temporal distribution. Attention metrics (social networks and digital media) are those that occur almost immediately after publication and have a short lifespan, while publication metrics, which come from edited publications (citations, Wikipedia, policy documents, patents), appear later and accumulate gradually over time. Social networks and digital media have greater interactions among themselves than publication metrics. Regarding the role of altmetrics in preceding citation impact, social networks have the ability to trigger the appearance of citations and shorten the time it takes for them to occur. Specifically, Research Highlights and Clinical Guidelines, two metrics associated with qualitative assessments, are those that most strongly influence the appearance of citations.

articles.title: Consent to sharing shopping data for health research: a mixed-methods study
articles.authors: Romana Burgess; Poppy Taylor; Kate Shiells; Mark Mumme; Andy Boyd; Anya Skatova
articles.abstract:
Introduction: Shopping data offer detailed, objective records of diet and lifestyle habits, providing a valuable but under-used resource for health research. While willingness to share personal data has been widely studied, no previous research has directly compared stated willingness with subsequent actual data sharing within the same cohort. Understanding whether willingness predicts actual consent, and how this varies by sociodemographic characteristics, motivations, and barriers, is essential to improve recruitment strategies and the representativeness of collected data.
Methods: We employed a mixed-methods approach using the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC). Quantitative analyses combined sociodemographic variables with responses from a 2018 survey assessing willingness to consent to shopping data linkage and outcomes from a 2023 loyalty card consent campaign. These were complemented by semi-structured interviews in 2019 exploring attitudes towards sharing shopping data. 
Results: In 2018, 60.7% of participants indicated willingness to share shopping data (n = 2142, 9230 invited). In 2023, actual consent—requiring provision of loyalty card details for five UK retailers (Sainsbury’s, Tesco, Boots, Morrisons, and Coop)—was obtained from 511 of 6170 invited participants. Demographic characteristics were broadly comparable across samples. Stated willingness was associated with higher odds of actual consent (adjusted OR = 1.48, 95% CI: 1.16–1.90), indicating that willingness is a meaningful predictor of subsequent data sharing. Sociodemographic characteristics and motivations (e.g., societal benefit, scientific interest) showed modest associations with both willingness and consent and moderated the willingness–consent relationship. Interviews reinforced the importance of trust in ALSPAC and clear communication about data use, while highlighting privacy concerns as minor barriers.
Conclusion:  This study provides the first within-cohort evidence that willingness to share shopping data predicts subsequent data sharing, albeit imperfectly. Willingness surveys can therefore inform recruitment planning, but additional strategies are required to improve realised consent rates. Addressing practical barriers, clarifying data use, and highlighting societal and scientific benefits may help narrow this gap; Targeted engagement, including through informative materials and outreach events, may help to reach groups less likely to consent. These insights can inform data collection in longitudinal cohorts and wider population samples.

articles.title: Data Note: Linking Shopping Transaction Data into the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children
articles.authors: Romana Burgess; Mark Mumme; Andy Boyd; Anya Skatova
articles.abstract:
Introduction: This data note describes the collection and linkage of participant’s Sainsburys shopping data into the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC). 
Methods: Data were collected via informed participant consent and Subject Access Requests under UK GDPR, securely transferred and integrated into the ALSPAC database using unique study identifiers. Participant consultation and a technical pilot ensured transparent, acceptable, and secure linkage. 
Results: The dataset contains anonymised transactions for 244 participants, covering 658,375 item purchases between July 2014 and February 2024. Transactions include an item description, price, quantity, time, date, store location, and other variables. Items fall into 82 categories (e.g., produce) and 1002 subcategories (e.g., bananas). In terms of representativeness, the cohort is skewed towards females, consists of participants of similar age, and is predominantly made up of individuals with higher educational attainment and of White ethnicity.
Conclusion: The linkage of shopping records complements ALSPACs extensive existing dataset, which comprises over 30 years of sociodemographic and health-related data. The linkage stands as a novel opportunity for future research into dietary habits, nutritional intake, and subsequent health outcomes. Access to data may be granted to approved researchers upon reasonable request, following a proposal submission through the ALSPAC website (http://www.bristol.ac.uk/alspac).

articles.title: Gendered attrition patterns in a male-typed work setting - Comparing gender differences in attrition among Dutch police staff to the public sector
articles.authors: Gemma G. M. Geuke; Amy Nivette; Isabelle W. J. van der Vegt; Eva Jaspers
articles.abstract: Research shows that women leave male-dominated work settings more often than their male colleagues. In public services such as policing, disproportionate attrition of women slows improvement of gender equality, but can also affect legitimacy and service outcomes. Typically, attrition of women in policing is studied in isolation, while sources (and thus, solutions) of inequalities may lie in societal or broader organizational dynamics. Using monthly employment register data, this study compared attrition of Dutch female policing staff to those of their male counterparts and their peers in other public subsectors. Findings indicated that, while women were more likely to leave the Dutch policing sector than men, several other subsectors showed similar gender differences. Results suggest that approaches to improve gender equality, both in policing and in other ‘gender-typed’ subsectors, should not be limited to increasing representation and should involve reviewing implicit assumptions about employees, including procedures for appraisal.

articles.title: Understanding app-diary study participants' interaction with personalized feedback using in-app paradata
articles.authors: Thijs Cornelis Carrière; Barry Schouten; Bella Struminskaya
articles.abstract:
In app-based data collection, researchers increasingly provide participants with personalized feedback based on participants’ data and study behavior. Personalized
feedback is hypothesized to have positive effects on participant engagement and data quality. However, empirical evidence on these positive effects and research on the mechanisms on the effects that feedback has on participation remains scarce. We investigate what characteristics participants have who interact with their feedback and whether feedback affects data quality in an app-based household budget diary study in the general population of the Netherlands. The timing of providing personalized feedback was varied by random assignment to two experimental conditions: half of the participants received their personalized feedback instantly, and another half received their personalized feedback after 14 days, at the end of the study. We used mobile app paradata to study actual feedback-viewing behavior. We find that most but not all participants view their personalized feedback and older participants are more likely to visit their feedback more often and for longer periods. We find no difference in data quality between participants that received instant feedback or delayed feedback, but using paradata, we find a positive relationship between data quality and the duration of viewing the feedback by participants. This finding suggests that the collection of in-app paradata can provide more nuance about the effect of personalized feedback on data quality, and that paradata can inform researchers about whether the costly efforts of implementing personalized feedback are worth undertaking.

articles.title: AI in the Loop?! A Systematic Review of the Use of Large Language Models in Survey and Public Opinion Research
articles.authors: Leah von der Heyde; Florian Keusch; Trent D Buskirk; Adam Eck
articles.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly transforming the entire pipeline of the survey and public opinion research process. The complexities of LLMs and the magnitude of their anticipated disruption warrant the specific attention of the field, but existing overviews are non-systematic, narrow, or theoretical. In this systematic, comprehensive review of the empirical literature, we investigate how LLMs are used before, during, and after data collection. We conduct a quantitative and qualitative assessment of 136 studies, synthesizing at which tasks, contexts, and research designs LLMs succeed and fail and which applications are underexplored. We find that up until 2025, research has mainly focussed on classifying text data, generating survey data, and developing survey instruments. Although there is a large diversity of possible models and interaction approaches, most research designs apply single models of the GPT family with zero-shot prompting to English-language contexts, putting the generalizability and replicability of individual studies into question. Nevertheless, patterns emerge: Across applications, LLMs perform better when approximating broad, well-represented aggregate patterns than nuanced individual attitudes, topics, or constructs. LLMs can be considered as supporting humans rather than replacing them. Going forward, careful model choices, reporting standards and survey-specific benchmarks would propel the field towards more generalizable use cases. Although the rapid technological development conflicts with rigorous qualitative assessments, survey methodologists are well-positioned to adopt LLMs, which will continue to depend on human-curated input and validation data. With this review, we expand the understanding of the potentials, concerns, and gaps of LLM use in survey and public opinion research, providing a starting point for future innovations. We make our thoroughly coded database available for future use and expansion.

articles.title: Cuerpos que nunca existieron: la prohibición de los deepfakes íntimos no consentidos en la reforma del AI Act europeo y la tutela civil de la persona
articles.authors: Andrés Gabriel Varas Quijón
articles.abstract:
This paper analyzes the civil-law protection of personality rights against non-consensual intimate deepfakes. It examines the prohibition introduced into Article 5 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (the AI Act) by the provisional agreement of 7 May 2026 on the "AI Omnibus," which bans AI systems designed to generate non-consensual intimate images of real persons and child sexual abuse material. The study argues that sexual deepfakes escape criminal provisions presupposing an authentic recording (Article 161-A of the Chilean Penal Code) and that the most suitable remedy is the civil protection of personality rights —image, honour, privacy and personal data, safeguarded by Article 19 No. 4 of the Constitution. It analyzes extra-contractual liability (Articles 2314 and 2329 of the Civil Code), moral damages and the obstacle of Article 2331, in dialogue with comparative law and Bill No. 17.795-19.

(Español) El artículo analiza la tutela civil de los derechos de la personalidad frente a los deepfakes íntimos no consentidos, a la luz de la prohibición introducida en el artículo 5 del Reglamento (UE) 2024/1689 por el acuerdo provisional de 7 de mayo de 2026 sobre el «AI Omnibus», y de su recepción en el Derecho civil chileno: derecho a la propia imagen, daño moral y responsabilidad extracontractual.

articles.title: Mathematics of Forever Wars
articles.authors: Oleg Gleizer
articles.abstract: In this paper, we use a popular game-theoretical model, called the Game of Chicken, to generate a random walk in the plane that seems to model well the forever wars of the present, and of the past. The main point of the paper is to show that simple game-theoretical models have a lot of predictive power. The second point is that quantum game algorithms may offer solutions to social problems humanity was not yet able to see.

articles.title: Gastos Municipais em Saúde e Anos Potenciais de Vida Perdidos: Uma Análise em Painel para os Municípios Brasileiros
articles.authors: Jorge Luis Tonetto; Gustavo Inácio de Moraes
articles.abstract:
Esta pesquisa insere-se no campo da economia da saúde e analisa se os gastos públicos municipais em saúde influenciam o indicador de Anos Potenciais de Vida Perdidos (APVP) nos municípios brasileiros. Os resultados indicam evidências de que os gastos municipais em saúde estão associados à melhoria do indicador APVP, com destaque para os gastos em atenção básica, que se mostraram estatisticamente significativos, enquanto os gastos em assistência hospitalar não apresentaram significância estatística. As estimativas também indicam relação não linear entre gasto e resultado em saúde, sugerindo oportunidade na elevação seletiva dos gastos que se mostraram abaixo do ponto estimado de maior eficiência, especialmente na atenção básica. A análise foi realizada para o período de 2005 a 2014, considerando os municípios brasileiros com dados disponíveis em bases administrativas nacionais. O APVP foi calculado para cada município e ano, adotando-se 70 anos como limite de vida potencial. Empregou-se modelo econométrico com dados em painel e estimativas robustas, utilizando duas especificações: uma baseada na função saúde e outra nas subfunções atenção básica e assistência hospitalar. Os resultados também indicam melhora gradual no desempenho dos municípios ao longo dos períodos de gestão e sugerem ganhos de escala associados ao tamanho populacional dos municípios.
English
This research is situated within the field of health economics and analyzes whether municipal public health expenditures influence the indicator of Potential Years of Life Lost (PYLL) in Brazilian municipalities. The results provide evidence that municipal health expenditures are associated with improvements in the PYLL indicator, with particular emphasis on primary health care expenditures, which were statistically significant, while hospital care expenditures did not show statistical significance. The estimates also indicate a nonlinear relationship between spending and health outcomes, suggesting opportunities for selective increases in expenditures that remain below the estimated point of greater efficiency, particularly in primary health care. The analysis covers the period from 2005 to 2014 and includes Brazilian municipalities with available data from national administrative databases. PYLL was calculated for each municipality and year, adopting 70 years as the potential life limit. An econometric panel data model with robust estimates was employed, using two specifications: one based on the health function and another based on the subfunctions of primary care and hospital care. The results also indicate gradual improvements in municipal performance over successive administrative periods and suggest economies of scale associated with municipal population size.

articles.title: Gender, Populist Attitudes and Voting Behaviour: A Critical Revisitation of Spierings & Zaslove (2017) in Light of EES 2014–2024 Evidence
articles.authors: Paula Roméro Cajaty Lopes
articles.abstract: This article provides a systematic critical reassessment and empirical update of Spierings and Zaslove's (2017) seminal study on the gender gap in populist voting, integrating a decade of subsequent scholarship and comparative evidence from the European Election Studies (EES) of 2014 and 2024. The original work posited that female socialization toward consensus and conflict aversion structurally distanced women from the antagonistic rhetoric of populist parties, particularly those of the populist radical right (PRR). Drawing on nine-country EES 2014 data and a fifteen-country EES 2024 dataset, we demonstrate that the aggregate PRR gender gap has narrowed considerably — from an average of −5.7 percentage points in 2014 to −3.7 points in 2024 — while exhibiting pronounced cross-national heterogeneity. We attribute this convergence to three partially overlapping mechanisms: (1) the strategic de-demonization of PRR parties under female leadership, producing a 'maternal-authoritarian' aesthetic that lowers women's psychological costs of voting radical right; (2) the emergence of femonationalism as a gendered discursive register that reframes nativist and anti-immigration stances as pro-women positions; and (3) a generational re-sorting, whereby the gender cleavage has migrated from the general electorate to Generation Z, where male respondents support PRR parties at rates 15 percentage points above their female counterparts, while young women increasingly anchor the populist radical left (PRL). These findings challenge the passive socialization model and call for analytical frameworks that treat female agency as constitutive of contemporary populism rather than merely reactive to it.

articles.title: Problem-Based Learning for Mandarin Chinese in Sri Lanka: An SLA-Informed Instructional Design for Transforming Writing-Dominant Pedagogy into Communicative Competence
articles.authors: Chathuni Weerakoon; Syed Tahir Abbas
articles.abstract: Mandarin Chinese education in Sri Lanka is predominantly shaped by examination-oriented instructional traditions that prioritize character writing accuracy, grammatical translation, and memorization-based learning. While these approaches support foundational literacy development, they remain insufficient for fostering communicative competence, interactional fluency, and pragmatic language use in authentic contexts. This paper proposes a conceptual instructional design framework that integrates Problem-Based Learning (PBL), Second Language Acquisition (SLA) theory, sociocultural learning theory, Communicative Language Teaching (CLT), and Understanding by Design (UbD) to address this structural imbalance. Grounded in constructivist epistemology, the framework reconceptualizes Mandarin learning as a socially mediated, interaction-driven, and problem-oriented process of meaning negotiation rather than a writing-centered system of symbolic reproduction. A five-phase cyclical model epistemic disruption, scaffolded linguistic activation, interactional co-construction, adaptive communicative transfer, and metacognitive restructuring is proposed as the core mechanism of the framework. The paper argues that PBL functions as a systemic pedagogical redesign mechanism that activates SLA processes suppressed by writing-dominant instruction in Sri Lankan Mandarin classrooms. The study contributes a context-sensitive conceptual model for aligning instructional design with communicative competence development in under-researched CFL learning environments

articles.title: Orphaned Knowledge: A Custodial Theory of Authorship for the Age of Artificial Intelligence
articles.authors: Babu George
articles.abstract: Scholarly publishing and intellectual property law have converged on a similar rule for generative AI: a machine cannot be an author because it cannot be accountable, and outputs without a human creator do not fit established regimes of protection or credit. This article argues that these responses are sound but conceptually incomplete because they treat authorship mainly as a matter of origination and reward. Drawing on Foucault, Merton, Shapin, and Hardwig, I argue that authorship has long bundled three functions: origination, reward, and custodianship. Generative AI disrupts that bundle by separating origination from the forward-looking human work of defending, developing, teaching, remembering, and answering for knowledge over time. The result is what I call orphaned knowledge: findings that may be true and valuable yet lack any identifiable person committed to keeping them alive. The article makes two contributions. First, it shows that both the credit economy of journals and the property economy of copyright are structured around human knowers, leaving machine-originated knowledge in a double vacuum. Second, it advances a custodial theory of authorship that redefines the author’s signature less as a retrospective claim to credit than as a prospective commitment to steward a finding. On this view, a machine may originate, but only a human or institution can undertake custody. The age of machine discovery therefore forces scholarship to confront a prior question that earlier authorship debates have obscured: not only who made knowledge, but who will keep it.

articles.title: Long-term childhood poverty in Britain: trends and drivers across the 1991-2017 birth cohorts
articles.authors: Selçuk Bedük; Anna Yong
articles.abstract: Any experience of childhood poverty affects life chances, but longer exposure is particularly detrimental to education, health, and future earnings. This study examines trends in long-term childhood poverty in Britain. Using a life-course perspective, we tracked poverty from birth to age ten among 1991–2017 birth cohorts. Our findings show that, on average, 17 per cent of children spent at least half of their childhood in poverty. Long-term poverty affected 25 per cent of those born in the early 1990s, markedly declined to 13–14 per cent for cohorts born after the post-1997 welfare reforms, and rose again to 23 per cent for children born following the 2013 austerity reforms. These trends are driven by shifts in the penalties associated with work-family risk factors, rather than by changes in their prevalence. These shifts in penalties reflect broader changes in redistribution and predistribution. The decline in the 1990s was largely due to rising employment and earnings in low-income households, whereas the post-austerity surge stems from reduced redistribution. For cohorts born in the 2000s, social transfers played a substantial role in containing long-term poverty despite worsening predistribution. Overall, the findings show that long-term childhood poverty is a major challenge in Britain and highlight the need to strengthen redistribution and predistribution.

articles.title: Hierarchical Bayesian Modeling of Cross-League Performance Translation in Elite Football
articles.authors: Mohammad Arshan Shaikh
articles.abstract: Predicting footballer performance following cross-league transfers is challenging when statistics derive from distinct tactical and competitive environments. We introduce a hierarchical Bayesian framework for quantifying league translation effects separately for attacking/midfield and defensive player roles. Using bridge datasets of 174 attacking and midfield transfers and 106 defensive transfers from the four major European feeder leagues (La Liga, Bundesliga, Serie A, Ligue 1) and within-Premier League transfers as a domestic baseline (2017/18–2022/23), we fit joint multivariate Bayesian models decomposing translation effects into league, team, and age components while capturing metric correlations via LKJ covariance structures. The primary contribution is calibrated uncertainty quantification: 90% highest density intervals enabling principled probabilistic reasoning in recruitment decision-making. Validation on two held-out, strictly cross-league transfer cohorts (attacking and midfield n = 45, defensive n = 35) demonstrates near-nominal interval coverage on attacking metrics and excellent convergence diagnostics (zero divergences, R̂ ≤ 1.005, where R̂ is the Gelman–Rubin potential scale reduction factor). Results reveal asymmetric league effects: La Liga shows a credibly positive shot-creating actions association (posterior mean +0.306, 90% HDI [+0.015, +0.578]). The framework is currently restricted to Premier League destinations; extending to multi-destination prediction remains an important avenue for future work.

articles.title: Exploring attitudes and experiences of menopause in women from various cultures and ethnic groups: a scoping review
articles.authors: NIKKI JOANNE GARNER; Anna Sweeting; Jessica Marshall; Ishaani Thethy; Anne-Marie Minihane
articles.abstract:
Background
Ethnicity and culture influence menopause perceptions.  Initiatives exist to address menopause disparities; however, inequalities persist, and despite policy efforts, women from various cultures and ethnic groups continue to feel excluded from mainstream menopause discourse. 
Objectives
To understand women from various cultures and ethnic groups experiences of menopause and how attitudes towards menopause vary across ethnicities and cultures, to inform the development of more inclusive and culturally meaningful menopause research and care for all women.
Eligibility Criteria
Study focus was reported adult women’s experiences, attitudes, beliefs of menopause or discussions surrounding menopause. The study needed to be original research, report on a cultural/ethnic group, and reported in English. 
Sources of Evidence
Database searches were conducted in November 2024, in PubMed, MEDLINE and on the first 25-pages of Google Scholar. Reference list searching was also conducted on excluded review studies. 
Charting Methods
Studies were reviewed independently by two reviewers (NG/IT).  Following selection, the main reviewer (NG) extracted study characteristics and participant demographics. Outcome data extraction was guided by the themes of language use, support perceptions, ethnic-group differences, and menopausal experiences or beliefs. Data was extracted independently by the main reviewer (NG), and a 10% random sample was checked by the second reviewer (JM), with full consensus reached after discussion.  
Results
2047 papers met initial criteria for inclusion. Data was extracted from 65 studies involving 67,434 women. Six themes emerged from the data: Cultural differences, Menopause as a natural process, Cessation of menses as a positive attribute of menopause, ‘Bad blood’ as a negative attribute of menopause, Role of spouse and Engagement in peer support.
Conclusions
Few studies offer cross-cultural analysis making comparisons between ethnicities and cultures difficult. Women from various cultures and ethnic groups attitudes and beliefs about menopause vary widely. Health professionals should ensure support aligns with women’s beliefs and preferences. Future research should explore how acculturation influences menopausal experiences among women from various cultures and ethnic groups.

articles.title: An inductive-deductive circular methodology of the  research process: Systematic thinking from prior  assumptions to final synthesis
articles.authors: Jo Bjørkli Helgetun; Thibault Coppe
articles.abstract:
In this essay, we reflect on the circularity of social science research and how we can attempt 
to put this circularity into a system to help structure our thinking and research practices 
through structured inductive-deductive reflection. We present our model for thinking of 
research as a holistic process from making our prior assumptions concrete and updating them 
systematically through constant comparison and a circular inductive-deductive analysis, 
towards a synthesis of the research findings.

articles.title: TRACE: An Interactive AI and Compliance-by-Design Framework for Financial Crime Detection
articles.authors: Himaja Prabhala
articles.abstract:
The use of artificial intelligence is growing in the domain of cyber-enabled financial crime detection and has created a significant accountability gap: AI models have been increasingly influencing the regulatory compliance outcomes, and yet their logic is opaque to the humans who are experts and bear legal responsibility for decisions (International Monetary Fund, 2026; Radončić, 2026). Instead of designing AI models to be transparent from their foundation, institutions add the layer of post-hoc explanations onto the autonomous or semi-autonomous AI models (European Commission, 2021; FATF, 2021). Recent research on interactive AI shows analysts prefer to interact and be actively involved with the systems rather than passively consume the outputs (Raees et al., 2024; Wang, 2019). This paper argues that, in high-risk domains such as anti-money laundering, cyber-enabled financial fraud, and cybercrimes, AI should function as a rule-based and human-in-the-loop decision-support system which structures evidence and applies typologies, rules, and auditable variables to support risk assessments while retaining human judgement for final decisions (Bank for International Settlements, 2025; Fink, 2025). This paper introduces TRACE (Transaction Risk Analysis and Compliance Engine) as an interactive AI; it is the manifestation of the compliance-by-design principle. TRACE integrates a transparent rule-based support engine and mandatory human analysts’ approval into a single compliance-grade architecture. In this design, the AI only suggests the risk scoring and flags up factors.

To provide support for analysts to make decisions without compromising on transparency, TRACE integrates an auditable retrieval module that draws upon the curated regulated knowledge base, such as FATF, RBI guidelines, and national KYC derivatives. Upon analyst request, contextual evidence, and surfacing retrieved snippets with reliable sources, it remains separate from rule-based scoring or risk narratives (Johnson et al., 2021; Lewis et al., 2020; Saison Technology, 2026). The grounds on which TRACE operates are using the foundational principles of ethics and responsible AI. The risk alerts are flagged using transparency and precise regulatory standards with the aim of mitigating demographic bias in compliance assessments. As it strictly functions as a compliance support tool and not as any enforcement system, it reflects a clear commitment towards responsible AI in this domain, where errors carry serious consequences (AML RightSource, 2024; European Commission, 2021).

articles.title: Contained and Uncontained Experiments
articles.authors: Kevin Munger
articles.abstract: I introduce a typology of political science experiments based on their ``containedness." Contained experiments are those for which it is plausible to ``perform the same procedure" and expect ``the same result." Uncontained experiments are those for which practitioners expect intrinsic variation to dominate; even when performing the same procedure, the goal is to understand how results differ across time and context. I propose three conceptions of containedness: pragmatic (community expectations about replication), complexity (information required to specify the procedure), and temporal (speed of experimentation relative to drift in the phenomenon). Lab experiments are generally the most contained, survey experiments occupy a middle position, and field experiments are the least contained. I provide a diagnostic rubric for evaluating containedness and argue that distinct evidentiary strategies are appropriate at different levels. For highly uncontained experiments, ``replication" is not a coherent goal; knowledge accumulation must proceed through theory rather than induction.

articles.title: Sustainable Agricultural Livelihoods in Tribal Odisha: An Analysis of Indigenous Farming Systems, Market Access, and Socio-Economic Challenges
articles.authors: Dr Sadasiba Tripathy
articles.abstract: Because tribal agriculture in Odisha is located at the intersection of rainfed farming, forest dependence, smallholder risk and policy experimentation including in millets, women’s collectives, farmer producer organisations, forest rights, and decentralized procurement. This article examines the sustainability of livelihoods in tribal communities resulting from indigenous farming systems, which is related to market-access and socio-economic constraints affecting livelihood security. The analysis uses secondary data and considers government statistics, peer-reviewed literature, institutional reports, and policy documents using the Sustainable Livelihoods Framework and theories around agroecology, indigenous knowledge systems, and market access. The evidence demonstrates that mixed cropping, millet-pulse systems, low-external-input maintenance of landrace seeds, pest and soil management, livestock integration, forest-food systems, among others, enhance natural and social capital, facilitate dietary diversification of house barn, and build ecological resilience at rainfed and upland areas. Despite this potential, a number of factors limit their livelihoods, including: small and fragmented holdings, uncertainty in rainfall, land and forest tenure insecurity, limited irrigation, weak rural roads, inadequate storage and processing, dependence on intermediaries, poor access to institutional credit, gendered drudgery, and inadequate access to extension services.  The Odisha Millets Mission/Shree Anna Abhiyan, Agriculture Production Cluster model, self-help groups, tribal livelihood programmes and other recent interventions have facilitated better coordination, aggregation and price discovery in selected locations. However, impact of these interventions is contingent upon last mile infrastructure, procurement timing, working capital, participatory extension and culturally compatible technology. Sustainable tribal agriculture will require more than the promotion of crops. It will require rights-based governance of resources, resilient markets, women-centred skill-saving innovations, community seed systems, climate adaptation, and institutional convergence. The framework of integrated livelihood-market for tribal agrarian development in Odisha has been contributed through the study along with highlighting priorities for future field-based and longitudinal research.

articles.title: Genetic Mirroring in Adoptees: Toward a Formal Construct Definition, Nomological Network, and Operationalization Framework
articles.authors: Giulia Suzzi
articles.abstract: Genetic mirroring, the developmentally recurring need to perceive reflected biological similarity in genetically related others, has gained clinical salience as an explanatory framework for the identity experiences of adoptees and donor-conceived individuals. Despite this, the construct lacks the theoretical architecture required for empirical investigation: a precise definition, differentiation from adjacent constructs, and a plausible operationalization pathway. The present paper addresses that gap through four coordinated contributions. First, a formal construct definition is proposed, encompassing three theoretically distinct dimensions: biological salience, embodied discontinuity, and mirroring responsiveness. Second, the construct is situated within a nomological network specifying its hypothesized relationships with established psychological variables, including identity exploration, narrative identity coherence, attachment security, genealogical bewilderment, and physical self-concept. Third, a theoretical case is made for incremental validity over existing frameworks: genetic mirroring is proposed to capture variance in adoptee psychological functioning that neither attachment theory nor narrative identity frameworks can fully account for. Fourth, a preliminary scale structure is proposed as proof of concept. The paper's aim is to transform a clinically emergent construct into a formally theorizable and measurable entity, enabling the empirical program necessary to evaluate its scientific and practical significance.

articles.title: Hedging Under U.S. Reindustrialization: Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia in an Era of Intensifying U.S.–China Rivalry
articles.authors: Julie Yu-Wen Chen
articles.abstract: Southeast Asian states maintain deep economic ties with both the United States and China, and the region has become a major destination for supply-chain relocation from firms in both powers. Recent U.S. reindustrialization policies—including the extension of elevated tariffs to Southeast Asian exports—pose new challenges to the region's export-led growth model and to U.S. strategic positioning in the Indo-Pacific. Drawing on hedging theory, particularly Cheng-Chwee Kuik's distinction between return-maximizing and risk-contingency behaviors, this paper examines how three manufacturing-oriented, trade-dependent economies—Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia—adjusted their foreign-policy and geoeconomic orientations in response to successive waves of U.S. reindustrialization from 2017 to 2026. Using a theory-driven event-coding approach, the study analyzes 66 verified foreign-policy and geoeconomic events to trace directional tilts toward Washington, Beijing, or neutrality across three U.S. administrations. The findings show that U.S. reindustrialization has not uniformly pushed Southeast Asian states toward China. Instead, different phases of U.S. policy produced qualitatively distinct hedging responses, mediated by structural factors that vary across countries. The analysis also reveals a misalignment between U.S. tariff instruments and the goals of the Indo-Pacific Strategy: tariff shocks create openings for Chinese economic leverage in the very economies U.S. industrial policy seeks to draw closer. The durability of the Indo-Pacific Strategy will depend on whether Washington can align its economic tools with its security ambitions and provide the structural conditions that enable partners to hedge coherently under intensifying great-power competition.

articles.title: eHealth Use among Adults with Chronic Illness in the United States: Health Self-Efficacy and Health Literacy as Pathways to Mental Well-Being
articles.authors: Qianfeng Lu; Wen Jiao; Qiaofei Jovi Wu
articles.abstract: Patients with chronic illness face a considerable mental health burden. However, the pathways through which eHealth use influences mental health in this population remain poorly understood. Drawing on Street's (2003) three-stage model, this study examined the effects of eHealth use on mental health among chronic illness patients, with health self-efficacy as a mediator and health literacy as a moderator. Data from the Health Information National Trends Survey 7 (HINTS 7) were analysed among U.S. adults with chronic illness (N = 2,928) using the PROCESS macro. The direct effect of eHealth use on mental health was insignificant. eHealth use was negatively associated with health self-efficacy, which was then positively linked to mental health. Health literacy moderated the relationship between eHealth use and health self-efficacy, with the negative association significant at lower literacy levels but diminishing and trending positive at higher literacy levels. Consequently, the indirect effect of eHealth use on mental health through health self-efficacy was contingent on health literacy, being negative at lower levels and diminishing as literacy increased. These findings suggest that health literacy determines whether eHealth use burdens patients with chronic illness. Therefore, ensuring adequate patients health literacy is important in eHealth interventions targeting this population.
